The "List Limit" Error (procedural_overgeneralization): When listing factors for 36 and 48, students often miss the largest one (12) and stop at 6, thinking they've found the "greatest" simply because it's the largest one they thought of.

Distributive Property Drift (surface_feature_matching): When asked to rewrite $36 + 48$ as $12(3 + 4)$, students might correctly find the 12 but then put the original numbers inside the parentheses, writing $12(36 + 48)$.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m packing snack bags for the track team and I have exactly 36 granola bars and 48 apple slices. I want to make the most bags possible so that every bag has the exact same number of bars and the exact same number of slices, with nothing left over. Also, my friend is bringing juice boxes in packs of 8 and straws in packs of 12. How do I figure out the biggest number of bags I can make, and how many of each juice item we need so they match up perfectly?

misconceptions.title: LCM/GCF Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students often swap the two concepts, trying to find a multiple (like 144) for the snack bags instead of a factor (12), or trying to find a factor (4) for the juice boxes instead of a multiple (24).

misconceptions.title: The "List Limit" Error
misconceptions.description: When listing factors for 36 and 48, students often miss the largest one (12) and stop at 6, thinking they've found the "greatest" simply because it's the largest one they thought of.

misconceptions.title: Distributive Property Drift
misconceptions.description: When asked to rewrite $36 + 48$ as $12(3 + 4)$, students might correctly find the 12 but then put the original numbers inside the parentheses, writing $12(36 + 48)$.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you make 6 bags, you use 6 bars per bag. Could you make even more bags and use fewer bars in each one?"
"Wait—if juice comes in 8s and straws in 12s, does buying 4 of each actually make them equal, or does it just give you 4 of each pack?"
If you take 12 out of both 36 and 48, what 'leftover' numbers are you multiplying 12 by to get back to your original totals?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you multiply two numbers together, that answer is ALWAYS their Least Common Multiple... or is there sometimes a smaller number that they both fit into?
sections.inference: I found out we actually have 60 napkins too. If we still have our 36 bars and 48 apples, do you think the 'biggest number of bags' we can make will stay at 12, or will adding the napkins force us to make smaller bags? And how would you write that new snack total using that distributive property 'parentheses' trick?

6.ns.b_party-planner.title: The Ultimate Party Planner
6.ns.b_party-planner.standardDescription: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
6.ns.b_party-planner.description: Teach Zippy about The Ultimate Party Planner
6.ns.b_party-planner.problemStatement: I’m helping my mom plan a massive graduation party for 1,248 people! We have a budget of \$5,616 for food, and I need to figure out exactly how much we can spend per person without going over. Plus, I have to buy hot dogs and buns, but hot dogs come in packs of 10 and buns come in packs of 8. If I don't want any leftovers of either, how many of each do I have to buy so they match up perfectly?
6.ns.b_party-planner.teachingPrompt: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
targetConcepts: Students can fluently divide multi-digit numbers using the standard algorithm.
Students can find the Least Common Multiple (LCM) of two whole numbers to solve real-world "matching" problems.
misconceptions: Division Remainder Confusion (procedural_overgeneralization): When dividing $5,616$ by $1,248$, students often struggle with multi-digit divisors, either guessing the quotient or misplacing the decimal point, leading to an unrealistic "per person" cost like \$0.45 or \$45.00.

Least Common Multiple (LCM) vs. Greatest Common Factor (GCF) (partial_concept_model): For the hot dog problem, a student might find the GCF (2) and think they only need 2 packs, rather than finding the LCM (40) to ensure the totals match.

Additive Hot Dog Logic (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): A student might think that since there is a difference of 2 between the packs ($10 - 8 = 2$), they just need to buy 2 more packs of buns than hot dogs to make it "even."

sections.studentPrompt: I’m helping my mom plan a massive graduation party for 1,248 people! We have a budget of \$5,616 for food, and I need to figure out exactly how much we can spend per person without going over. Plus, I have to buy hot dogs and buns, but hot dogs come in packs of 10 and buns come in packs of 8. If I don't want any leftovers of either, how many of each do I have to buy so they match up perfectly?

misconceptions.title: Division Remainder Confusion
misconceptions.description: When dividing $5,616$ by $1,248$, students often struggle with multi-digit divisors, either guessing the quotient or misplacing the decimal point, leading to an unrealistic "per person" cost like \$0.45 or \$45.00.

misconceptions.title: Least Common Multiple (LCM) vs. Greatest Common Factor (GCF)
misconceptions.description: For the hot dog problem, a student might find the GCF (2) and think they only need 2 packs, rather than finding the LCM (40) to ensure the totals match.

misconceptions.title: Additive Hot Dog Logic
misconceptions.description: A student might think that since there is a difference of 2 between the packs ($10 - 8 = 2$), they just need to buy 2 more packs of buns than hot dogs to make it "even."

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you buy one pack of each, you have 10 dogs and 8 buns. If you buy two of each, you have 20 and 16. Do the numbers seem to be getting closer or further apart?"
"Wait—if you have \$5,000 for 1,000 people, that's about \$5 each. Does your answer of \$4.50 for 1,248 people sound like it's in the right ballpark?"
Is there a 'magic number' that both 8 and 10 can divide into perfectly?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you have two different sized packs of food, you can ALWAYS find a total number where they match up perfectly just by multiplying the two pack sizes together... but is that the *smallest* amount you could buy, or is there sometimes a smaller 'matching' number?
sections.inference: I found out that the juice boxes come in packs of 12 and the straws come in packs of 15. If we use that same 'matching' trick we used for the hot dogs, do you think we'll end up needing more or fewer packs than we did for the 8 and 10 packs? And what is the smallest number of juice boxes we can buy so every box gets exactly one straw?

6.ns.c.5_zero-point-mystery.title: The Zero Point Mystery
6.ns.c.5_zero-point-mystery.standardDescription: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, positive/negative electric charge); use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
6.ns.c.5_zero-point-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Zero Point Mystery
6.ns.c.5_zero-point-mystery.problemStatement: I'm looking at a weather app and a bank statement, and they both have negative numbers, but they feel totally different. The temperature says $-4^\circ\text{C}$ and my bank account says $-\$15.00$. If 'zero' usually means 'nothing,' does that mean it's actually possible to have 'less than nothing' when it comes to money or weather? And like, if the temperature goes to $0^\circ\text{C}$, is that the same thing as having exactly $\$0.00$ in my pocket?
6.ns.c.5_zero-point-mystery.teachingPrompt: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, positive/negative electric charge); use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
targetConcepts: Students can use positive and negative integers to represent real-world quantities.
Students can explain the contextual meaning of zero in various scenarios (e.g., sea level, freezing point, zero balance).
misconceptions: Zero as "Empty" (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students often think zero always means "nothing exists." In temperature, $0^\circ\text{C}$ is a specific physical state (freezing), not the absence of temperature.

Magnitude vs. Direction (partial_concept_model): A student might think $-\$15.00$ is "more money" than $\$5.00$ because 15 is a bigger digit, failing to realize the negative sign represents a debt (owing money) rather than owning it.
Contextual Inconsistency (surface_feature_matching): Students might struggle to explain what zero means in different stories—like why 0 feet elevation is the beach, but 0 dollars is an empty wallet.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm looking at a weather app and a bank statement, and they both have negative numbers, but they feel totally different. The temperature says $-4^\circ\text{C}$ and my bank account says $-\$15.00$. If 'zero' usually means 'nothing,' does that mean it's actually possible to have 'less than nothing' when it comes to money or weather? And like, if the temperature goes to $0^\circ\text{C}$, is that the same thing as having exactly $\$0.00$ in my pocket?

misconceptions.title: Zero as "Empty"
misconceptions.description: Students often think zero always means "nothing exists." In temperature, $0^\circ\text{C}$ is a specific physical state (freezing), not the absence of temperature.

misconceptions.title: Magnitude vs. Direction
misconceptions.description: A student might think $-\$15.00$ is "more money" than $\$5.00$ because 15 is a bigger digit, failing to realize the negative sign represents a debt (owing money) rather than owning it.

misconceptions.title: Contextual Inconsistency
misconceptions.description: Students might struggle to explain what zero means in different stories—like why 0 feet elevation is the beach, but 0 dollars is an empty wallet.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you owe your friend \$5, is that the same as having \$5 in your hand? How would you write those two different 'fives'?"
"Wait—if the thermometer hits 0, does the air just disappear, or is it just the point where water turns to ice?"
If a hiker is at $-20$ feet and a diver is at $-50$ feet, which one is actually deeper underground or underwater?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that in EVERY situation with negative numbers, 0 is always the 'starting line' or the middle point between two opposites... or is there a case where 0 is just a random number in the middle of a list?
sections.inference: I found a map of Death Valley where the sign says '282 feet below sea level.' If we call sea level '0,' do you think we should write that as $282$ or $-282$? And like, if a bird is flying at $100$ feet above sea level, how much 'distance' is actually between the bird and the ground in Death Valley?

6.ns.c.6.a_double-flip-challenge.title: The Double Flip Challenge
6.ns.c.6.a_double-flip-challenge.standardDescription: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, e.g., -(-3) = 3, and that 0 is its own opposite.
6.ns.c.6.a_double-flip-challenge.description: Teach Zippy about The Double Flip Challenge
6.ns.c.6.a_double-flip-challenge.problemStatement: I was playing a game on a number line where a 'Negative Card' tells you to jump to the exact opposite side of 0. If I'm standing at $5$ and I pull one card, I land on $-5$. But what happens if I pull *two* 'Negative Cards' in a row? My friend says I end up right back where I started at $5$, but that feels like I just did a lot of jumping for nothing. How can a negative of a negative turn back into a regular positive number?
6.ns.c.6.a_double-flip-challenge.teachingPrompt: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, e.g., -(-3) = 3, and that 0 is its own opposite.
targetConcepts: Students can identify that $a$ and $-a$ are on opposite sides of 0 on a number line.
Students can demonstrate that the opposite of an opposite is the original number ($-(-p) = p$).
Students recognize that 0 is its own opposite.
misconceptions: The "Cumulative Negative" Trap (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students often think that more negative signs just make a number "more negative." They might assume $-(-5)$ is actually $-10$ or a "super negative" 5, rather than a directional flip.

The Zero Gap (partial_concept_model): A student might think the opposite of $5$ is $0$ because they confuse "opposite" with "resetting to zero," or they might think the opposite of $0$ is some other number because they don't see $0$ as its own mirror.

Distance vs. Location (surface_feature_matching): Students may focus only on the digit '5' and ignore the sign, failing to see that the sign is a specific instruction about which side of the "0-Border" they are standing on.

sections.studentPrompt: I was playing a game on a number line where a 'Negative Card' tells you to jump to the exact opposite side of 0. If I'm standing at $5$ and I pull one card, I land on $-5$. But what happens if I pull *two* 'Negative Cards' in a row? My friend says I end up right back where I started at $5$, but that feels like I just did a lot of jumping for nothing. How can a negative of a negative turn back into a regular positive number?

misconceptions.title: The "Cumulative Negative" Trap
misconceptions.description: Students often think that more negative signs just make a number "more negative." They might assume $-(-5)$ is actually $-10$ or a "super negative" 5, rather than a directional flip.

misconceptions.title: The Zero Gap
misconceptions.description: A student might think the opposite of $5$ is $0$ because they confuse "opposite" with "resetting to zero," or they might think the opposite of $0$ is some other number because they don't see $0$ as its own mirror.

misconceptions.title: Distance vs. Location
misconceptions.description: Students may focus only on the digit '5' and ignore the sign, failing to see that the sign is a specific instruction about which side of the "0-Border" they are standing on.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you turn around 180 degrees, you're facing the opposite way. What happens if you turn 180 degrees *again*?"
"Wait—if 5 is 5 steps to the right of zero, and -5 is 5 steps to the left, are they still the same distance away from the center?"
If you pull a 'Negative Card' while standing on 0, where do you go? Can you even jump to an 'opposite' side of the center if you're already on it?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you put an EVEN number of negative signs in front of a number (like $--5$ or $----5$), you will ALWAYS end up back on the positive side... or is there a way that four negatives still stay negative?
sections.inference: I found a 'Mystery Card' that says $-(-(-7))$. If we follow the 'flip' rule, where do you think I'll land on the number line? And if I wanted to get back to $0$ from there, would I need to add or subtract 7?

6.ns.c.6_mirror-world-map.title: The Mirror World Map
6.ns.c.6_mirror-world-map.standardDescription: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.
6.ns.c.6_mirror-world-map.description: Teach Zippy about The Mirror World Map
6.ns.c.6_mirror-world-map.problemStatement: I was looking at a map of a city where 'Central Station' is at $(0,0)$. My friend said that if I walk 3 blocks East and 4 blocks North, I'm at $(3,4)$. But then he said if I walk 3 blocks West and 4 blocks South, I'm at $(-3, -4)$. I'm confused—if I walked the same total distance both times, why do the numbers have minus signs? Is $(-3, -4)$ a 'fake' place, or is it just a different way to describe where I am on the grid?
6.ns.c.6_mirror-world-map.teachingPrompt: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.
targetConcepts: Students can represent negative rational numbers as points on a horizontal or vertical number line.
Students can identify and plot ordered pairs $(x,y)$ in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane.
Students can recognize that the signs of numbers in ordered pairs indicate locations in specific quadrants.
misconceptions: Mirror Image Confusion (partial_concept_model): Students often struggle with the direction of the negative axes. They might think $(-3, 4)$ is in the bottom-right instead of the top-left, or they might flip the x and y coordinates entirely.

The "Subtracting" Sign (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students often see the minus sign as an instruction to *do* something (subtract) rather than a *location* (to the left of or below zero). They might try to "solve" $(-3, -4)$ to get $-7$.

Number Line Directionality (whole_number_bias): On the horizontal axis, students may intuitively move right for larger numbers, but they often struggle to realize that $-2$ is "greater" than $-5$ because it is further to the right.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at a map of a city where 'Central Station' is at $(0,0)$. My friend said that if I walk 3 blocks East and 4 blocks North, I'm at $(3,4)$. But then he said if I walk 3 blocks West and 4 blocks South, I'm at $(-3, -4)$. I'm confused—if I walked the same total distance both times, why do the numbers have minus signs? Is $(-3, -4)$ a 'fake' place, or is it just a different way to describe where I am on the grid?

misconceptions.title: Mirror Image Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students often struggle with the direction of the negative axes. They might think $(-3, 4)$ is in the bottom-right instead of the top-left, or they might flip the x and y coordinates entirely.

misconceptions.title: The "Subtracting" Sign
misconceptions.description: Students often see the minus sign as an instruction to *do* something (subtract) rather than a *location* (to the left of or below zero). They might try to "solve" $(-3, -4)$ to get $-7$.

misconceptions.title: Number Line Directionality
misconceptions.description: On the horizontal axis, students may intuitively move right for larger numbers, but they often struggle to realize that $-2$ is "greater" than $-5$ because it is further to the right.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you stand at 0 on a number line and take 5 steps to the right, you're at 5. If you take 5 steps to the left, where are you? Is that spot the same distance from 0 as the first one?"
"Wait—if you are at $(0,0)$ and you only move 'West,' which number in your $(x,y)$ pair changes? What if you only move 'South'?"
Does the minus sign tell you *how far* you walked, or just *which direction* you turned from the starting point?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you have a point like $(2, 5)$, its 'opposite' point $(-2, -5)$ will ALWAYS be the exact same distance from the center $(0,0)$, just in a perfectly diagonal direction... or is there a point where the distances don't match up?
sections.inference: I found a secret shop located at $(5, -2)$. If I start at the center $(0,0)$, do I need to walk East or West first? And then do I go North or South? If I decided to move to the 'mirror' version of that shop across the vertical y-axis, what do you think my new coordinates would be?

6.ns.c.7.b_deep-freeze-challenge.title: The Deep Freeze Challenge
6.ns.c.7.b_deep-freeze-challenge.standardDescription: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts. For example, write -3°C > -7°C to express the fact that -3°C is warmer than -7°C.
6.ns.c.7.b_deep-freeze-challenge.description: Teach Zippy about The Deep Freeze Challenge
6.ns.c.7.b_deep-freeze-challenge.problemStatement: I was looking at the temperatures for two different cities in Antarctica. City A was $-15^\circ\text{C}$ and City B was $-25^\circ\text{C}$. My friend says City B is 'higher' because 25 is a bigger number than 15, but the weather reporter said City A is actually warmer. If I have to write a math sentence using a 'greater than' symbol ($>$), how do I show that $-15$ is the warmer one, and what does that tell us about where they sit on a number line?
6.ns.c.7.b_deep-freeze-challenge.teachingPrompt: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts. For example, write -3°C > -7°C to express the fact that -3°C is warmer than -7°C.
targetConcepts: Students can translate real-world descriptors (warmer, deeper, more debt) into inequality statements using rational numbers.
Students can explain the meaning of an inequality statement in the context of a specific scenario.
misconceptions: Magnitude-Only Thinking (whole_number_bias): Students often focus on the "absolute" size of the digit (25 vs 15) and assume that because $25 > 15$, then $-25$ must be greater than $-15$. They struggle to realize that with negative numbers, "greater" means "further to the right" or "warmer/higher."

Symbol Confusion (procedural_overgeneralization): A student might correctly identify that City A is warmer but write $-25 > -15$ because they think the "big side" of the symbol should point to the "big digit."

Contextual Inversion (partial_concept_model): Students may understand the math on a flat number line but fail to translate it to real-world "value," thinking a bank balance of $-\$50$ is "better" than $-\$10$ because 50 is more.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at the temperatures for two different cities in Antarctica. City A was $-15^\circ\text{C}$ and City B was $-25^\circ\text{C}$. My friend says City B is 'higher' because 25 is a bigger number than 15, but the weather reporter said City A is actually warmer. If I have to write a math sentence using a 'greater than' symbol ($>$), how do I show that $-15$ is the warmer one, and what does that tell us about where they sit on a number line?

misconceptions.title: Magnitude-Only Thinking
misconceptions.description: Students often focus on the "absolute" size of the digit (25 vs 15) and assume that because $25 > 15$, then $-25$ must be greater than $-15$. They struggle to realize that with negative numbers, "greater" means "further to the right" or "warmer/higher."

misconceptions.title: Symbol Confusion
misconceptions.description: A student might correctly identify that City A is warmer but write $-25 > -15$ because they think the "big side" of the symbol should point to the "big digit."

misconceptions.title: Contextual Inversion
misconceptions.description: Students may understand the math on a flat number line but fail to translate it to real-world "value," thinking a bank balance of $-\$50$ is "better" than $-\$10$ because 50 is more.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you are standing at $-25$ on a number line, do you have to move right or left to get to $-15$? Which direction always leads to 'greater' numbers?"
"Wait—if $-1^\circ$ is just barely freezing and $-50^\circ$ is a total blizzard, which one is closer to being a 'hot' day?"
Does the minus sign act like a 'reverse' button for how we usually think about which number is bigger?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you have two negative numbers, the one that looks 'smaller' (like 15 vs 25) is ALWAYS the one that is actually the greater value... or is there a case where the bigger digit stays the winner?
sections.inference: I found a third city, City C, that is at $-5^\circ\text{C}$. If we put all three cities in a line from coldest to warmest, where would City C go? And like, if a scientist says a temperature is 'less than $-30^\circ\text{C}$,' does that mean it's getting closer to zero or getting even colder?

6.ns.c.7.c_bank-balance-blur.title: The Bank Balance Blur
6.ns.c.7.c_bank-balance-blur.standardDescription: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation. For example, for an account balance of -30 dollars, write |-30| = 30 to describe the size of the debt in dollars.
6.ns.c.7.c_bank-balance-blur.description: Teach Zippy about The Bank Balance Blur
6.ns.c.7.c_bank-balance-blur.problemStatement: I looked at my bank account app and it says my balance is $-\$40.00$. My older brother says, 'Whoa, you have a 40-dollar debt!' but I told him he was wrong because the number is negative, so it's 'less than zero.' He says the 'size' of my problem is still 40. If the math symbol $|-40|$ just turns the number into a regular 40, does that mean the negative sign just disappears, or is it telling us something else about how far I am from having an empty wallet?
6.ns.c.7.c_bank-balance-blur.teachingPrompt: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation. For example, for an account balance of -30 dollars, write |-30| = 30 to describe the size of the debt in dollars.
targetConcepts: Students can define absolute value as the distance a number is from zero on a number line.
Students can interpret absolute value in real-world contexts to describe the "size" or "magnitude" of a quantity (like debt or depth).
misconceptions: The "Make it Positive" Magic Trick (procedural_overgeneralization): Students often view absolute value as a "button" that just removes negative signs without understanding that it measures **distance**. This leads to confusion when they see $|40|$ and try to change it to $-40$, thinking the symbol just "switches" signs.

Value vs. Magnitude (partial_concept_model): A student might think that because $-40 < -10$, the "absolute value" of $-40$ should also be smaller. They struggle to separate the *location* of the number (value) from its *intensity* or *size* (magnitude).

Distance as Negative (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): A student might try to say a distance is $-40$ feet, not realizing that distance (how far you traveled) is always a positive count, even if you are traveling in a negative direction.

sections.studentPrompt: I looked at my bank account app and it says my balance is $-\$40.00$. My older brother says, 'Whoa, you have a 40-dollar debt!' but I told him he was wrong because the number is negative, so it's 'less than zero.' He says the 'size' of my problem is still 40. If the math symbol $|-40|$ just turns the number into a regular 40, does that mean the negative sign just disappears, or is it telling us something else about how far I am from having an empty wallet?

misconceptions.title: The "Make it Positive" Magic Trick
misconceptions.description: Students often view absolute value as a "button" that just removes negative signs without understanding that it measures **distance**. This leads to confusion when they see $|40|$ and try to change it to $-40$, thinking the symbol just "switches" signs.

misconceptions.title: Value vs. Magnitude
misconceptions.description: A student might think that because $-40 < -10$, the "absolute value" of $-40$ should also be smaller. They struggle to separate the *location* of the number (value) from its *intensity* or *size* (magnitude).

misconceptions.title: Distance as Negative
misconceptions.description: A student might try to say a distance is $-40$ feet, not realizing that distance (how far you traveled) is always a positive count, even if you are traveling in a negative direction.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you walk 10 steps East, how far did you walk? If you walk 10 steps West, how far did you walk? Is the 'how far' part different?"
"Wait—if you owe \$40 ($-\$40$) and your friend owes \$10 ($-\$10$), who has the 'bigger' debt to pay back?"
Does the absolute value symbol $| \text{ } |$ care about which *side* of zero you are on, or just how many *units* you are away from it?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that the absolute value of a number is ALWAYS a positive number (or zero), no matter what the original number looked like... or is there a special 'super-negative' number that stays negative even inside the bars?
sections.inference: I found two divers: Diver A is at $-25$ feet and Diver B is at $-40$ feet. If we use absolute value to talk about their 'depth,' Diver B has a greater absolute value ($|-40| > |-25|$). Does having a 'greater absolute value' mean they are closer to the surface or deeper in the ocean? And what would happen to that 'magnitude' if they both swam up 10 feet?

6.ns.c.7.d_debt-vs-dollars.title: The Debt vs. Dollars Dilemma
6.ns.c.7.d_debt-vs-dollars.standardDescription: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order. For example, recognize that an account balance less than -30 dollars represents a debt greater than 30 dollars.
6.ns.c.7.d_debt-vs-dollars.description: Teach Zippy about The Debt vs. Dollars Dilemma
6.ns.c.7.d_debt-vs-dollars.problemStatement: I'm looking at two bank accounts. Account A has $-\$50.00$ and Account B has $-\$10.00$. My math book says that $-50 < -10$ because $-50$ is further left on the number line. But my dad says that Account A actually has a 'greater debt' than Account B. How can a number be 'less than' another number but also be 'greater' at the same time? Is my dad wrong, or is the math book just talking about something else?
6.ns.c.7.d_debt-vs-dollars.teachingPrompt: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order. For example, recognize that an account balance less than -30 dollars represents a debt greater than 30 dollars.
targetConcepts: Students can distinguish between the order of rational numbers (inequalities) and the order of their absolute values (magnitudes).
Students can explain real-world scenarios where a "lesser" rational number represents a "greater" physical quantity (like debt or depth).
misconceptions: The "Greater" Confusion (semantic_overgeneralization): Students often confuse the word "greater" (meaning a larger value/further right) with "greater" (meaning a larger magnitude/further from zero). They struggle to realize that a *smaller* value ($-50$) can represent a *larger* physical quantity (a \$50 debt).

Sign-Value Conflict (partial_concept_model): A student might think that if the absolute value is larger ($|-50| = 50$), then the original number must also be larger, failing to account for the negative sign's impact on the number's position.

Contextual Inversion (surface_feature_matching): When asked which debt is "less," a student might pick $-\$50$ because it is the "lesser" number on the number line, even though a \$50 debt is actually a "bigger" financial problem than a \$10 debt.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm looking at two bank accounts. Account A has $-\$50.00$ and Account B has $-\$10.00$. My math book says that $-50 < -10$ because $-50$ is further left on the number line. But my dad says that Account A actually has a 'greater debt' than Account B. How can a number be 'less than' another number but also be 'greater' at the same time? Is my dad wrong, or is the math book just talking about something else?

misconceptions.title: The "Greater" Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students often confuse the word "greater" (meaning a larger value/further right) with "greater" (meaning a larger magnitude/further from zero). They struggle to realize that a *smaller* value ($-50$) can represent a *larger* physical quantity (a \$50 debt).

misconceptions.title: Sign-Value Conflict
misconceptions.description: A student might think that if the absolute value is larger ($|-50| = 50$), then the original number must also be larger, failing to account for the negative sign's impact on the number's position.

misconceptions.title: Contextual Inversion
misconceptions.description: When asked which debt is "less," a student might pick $-\$50$ because it is the "lesser" number on the number line, even though a \$50 debt is actually a "bigger" financial problem than a \$10 debt.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you owe your friend \$50, is that a 'bigger' or 'smaller' deal than owing them \$10? Now, which of those numbers is actually further to the left on the number line?"
"Wait—if a submarine is at $-100$ feet and a fish is at $-20$ feet, the submarine's elevation is 'less' than the fish's. But which one has a 'greater' depth?"
Does the word 'greater' change meaning if you are talking about the *number itself* versus the *size of the debt*?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that for any two negative numbers, the one that is 'less' on the number line will ALWAYS have the 'greater' absolute value... or is there a case where the smaller number also has the smaller absolute value?
sections.inference: Imagine two hikers. Hiker A is in a valley at $-40$ feet elevation, and Hiker B is even lower at $-75$ feet. We know that $-75 < -40$. If a park ranger says, 'Hiker B has a greater distance to climb to get back to sea level,' is he talking about the *elevation* or the *absolute value*? And what would happen to those two numbers if they both climbed up 100 feet?

6.ns.c.8_quad-city-courier.title: The Quad-City Courier
6.ns.c.8_quad-city-courier.standardDescription: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
6.ns.c.8_quad-city-courier.description: Teach Zippy about The Quad-City Courier
6.ns.c.8_quad-city-courier.problemStatement: I'm working as a bike courier in a city where the streets are a perfect grid. The 'Town Square' is at $(0,0)$. I just delivered a package at the Library, which is at $(3, 5)$, and now I have to go to the Juice Bar at $(3, -4)$. My GPS says I don't have to turn at all because they are on the same straight line! If I just go straight, how many blocks do I have to bike? My friend says it's only 1 block ($5 - 4$), but looking at my map, I have to pass the whole Town Square line first. How do I use the numbers to find the 'total' trip?
6.ns.c.8_quad-city-courier.teachingPrompt: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
targetConcepts: Students can graph ordered pairs in all four quadrants to solve real-world problems.
Students can calculate the distance between two points with the same x-coordinate or same y-coordinate using absolute value.
misconceptions: The Subtraction Trap (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students often subtract the absolute digits regardless of the signs (e.g., $5 - 4 = 1$), failing to realize that if the points are in different quadrants, they must travel *to* the axis and then *past* it.

Axis Confusion (partial_concept_model): A student might try to find the distance between the x-coordinates (3 and 3) and conclude the distance is 0, not realizing that the change in position is occurring along the y-axis.

Ignoring the Origin (surface_feature_matching): Students may forget that 0 acts as a "bridge." They might struggle to see that the distance is the sum of the absolute values ($|5| + |-4|$) when crossing an axis.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm working as a bike courier in a city where the streets are a perfect grid. The 'Town Square' is at $(0,0)$. I just delivered a package at the Library, which is at $(3, 5)$, and now I have to go to the Juice Bar at $(3, -4)$. My GPS says I don't have to turn at all because they are on the same straight line! If I just go straight, how many blocks do I have to bike? My friend says it's only 1 block ($5 - 4$), but looking at my map, I have to pass the whole Town Square line first. How do I use the numbers to find the 'total' trip?

misconceptions.title: The Subtraction Trap
misconceptions.description: Students often subtract the absolute digits regardless of the signs (e.g., $5 - 4 = 1$), failing to realize that if the points are in different quadrants, they must travel *to* the axis and then *past* it.

misconceptions.title: Axis Confusion
misconceptions.description: A student might try to find the distance between the x-coordinates (3 and 3) and conclude the distance is 0, not realizing that the change in position is occurring along the y-axis.

misconceptions.title: Ignoring the Origin
misconceptions.description: Students may forget that 0 acts as a "bridge." They might struggle to see that the distance is the sum of the absolute values ($|5| + |-4|$) when crossing an axis.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you go from 5 floors up to the ground floor (0), how many floors is that? Now, how many more to get to 4 floors underground?"
"Wait—if the x-numbers are the same (3 and 3), are you moving side-to-side or up-and-down?"
Does the distance between $(3, 5)$ and $(3, 1)$ feel longer or shorter than the distance between $(3, 5)$ and $(3, -4)$?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if two points are in different quadrants (like one positive and one negative), you ALWAYS add their absolute values to find the distance... but if they are in the same quadrant, you ALWAYS subtract them. Is that a rule that works every time?
sections.inference: I have one last delivery. I'm at the Juice Bar $(3, -4)$ and I need to go to the Pizza Shop at $(-6, -4)$. Since the y-numbers are now the same, I'm moving horizontally. Will I have to cross the center '0' line (the y-axis) to get there? And based on our distance trick, how many blocks total will that trip be?

6.ns.c_submarine-skyscraper.title: The Submarine & The Skyscraper
6.ns.c_submarine-skyscraper.standardDescription: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers (integers, fractions, and decimals, including negative numbers).
6.ns.c_submarine-skyscraper.description: Teach Zippy about The Submarine & The Skyscraper
6.ns.c_submarine-skyscraper.problemStatement: I'm looking at a map of a coastal city. The top of a skyscraper is at 450 feet above sea level, but a research submarine is at 450 feet *below* sea level. My friend says they are at the 'same number' because they both say 450, but I feel like one of them should have a minus sign. If we use 0 to represent the surface of the ocean, how do we show that they are in totally opposite directions, and what does it mean if the sub moves to $-500$ feet?
6.ns.c_submarine-skyscraper.teachingPrompt: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers (integers, fractions, and decimals, including negative numbers).
targetConcepts: Students can use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts (elevation, temperature, debt).
Students can identify that a number and its opposite are equidistant from zero on a number line.
misconceptions: Absolute Value Confusion (partial_concept_model): Students may think that $-500$ is "bigger" than $-450$ because 500 is a larger digit, failing to realize that $-500$ represents a position that is *lower* (less) on the vertical number line.
Zero as 'Nothing' (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students often struggle to see 0 as a specific location (sea level, freezing point) rather than just "the absence of value."

Directional Neglect (surface_feature_matching): A student might describe the submarine's depth as "450" and the skyscraper's height as "450," losing the critical distinction between positive and negative elevation in a mathematical model.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm looking at a map of a coastal city. The top of a skyscraper is at 450 feet above sea level, but a research submarine is at 450 feet *below* sea level. My friend says they are at the 'same number' because they both say 450, but I feel like one of them should have a minus sign. If we use 0 to represent the surface of the ocean, how do we show that they are in totally opposite directions, and what does it mean if the sub moves to $-500$ feet?

misconceptions.title: Absolute Value Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may think that $-500$ is "bigger" than $-450$ because 500 is a larger digit, failing to realize that $-500$ represents a position that is *lower* (less) on the vertical number line.

misconceptions.title: Zero as 'Nothing'
misconceptions.description: Students often struggle to see 0 as a specific location (sea level, freezing point) rather than just "the absence of value."

misconceptions.title: Directional Neglect
misconceptions.description: A student might describe the submarine's depth as "450" and the skyscraper's height as "450," losing the critical distinction between positive and negative elevation in a mathematical model.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you are at 0 and you go up 10 feet, you are at 10. If you go down 10 feet from 0, where are you?"
"Wait—if a fish is at $-10$ feet and a shark is at $-50$ feet, which one is actually deeper in the water?"
Does the minus sign tell you *how much* distance there is, or does it tell you *which way* you went from zero?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you have two numbers like $10$ and $-10$, they are ALWAYS the exact same distance away from zero, just in different directions... or is there a number where the 'negative version' is further away?
sections.inference: I found a treasure chest at $-600$ feet and a sunken ship at $-300$ feet. If the submarine is currently at $-450$ feet, do you think it needs to go *up* or *down* to reach the ship? And like, if the temperature outside the sub drops from $2^\circ\text{C}$ to $-5^\circ\text{C}$, is it getting warmer or colder?

6.rp.a.2_lemonade-stand-off.title: The Lemonade Stand-Off
6.rp.a.2_lemonade-stand-off.standardDescription: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b != 0, and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship.
6.rp.a.2_lemonade-stand-off.description: Teach Zippy about The Lemonade Stand-Off
6.rp.a.2_lemonade-stand-off.problemStatement: I’m trying to win the 'Best Value' award at the school fair, but these two lemonade recipes are totally different: one uses 10 scoops of sugar for every 5 gallons, and the other uses 12 scoops for every 6 gallons. My friend says the second one is 'sweeter' because 12 is a bigger number, but I feel like we need to know exactly how much sugar is hiding in just one single gallon to really compare them. If we want to find that 'magic number' for one gallon, how do we get there from these big batches?
6.rp.a.2_lemonade-stand-off.teachingPrompt: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b != 0, and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship.
targetConcepts: Students can derive a unit rate $a/b$ from a given ratio $a:b$.
Students can use unit rate language (e.g., "5 scoops per gallon") to describe and compare two different ratio relationships.
misconceptions: Magnitude Bias (surface_feature_matching): A student might assume the 12-scoop recipe is sweeter simply because 12 is greater than 10, ignoring the total volume of liquid.

Subtraction Strategy (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): A student might think the difference is what matters (e.g., $10 - 5 = 5$ and $12 - 6 = 6$), concluding the 12-scoop batch is sweeter by one "unit."

Whole Number Preference (whole_number_bias): Students may struggle to find a unit rate if the division results in a fraction or decimal, leading them to round or guess instead of using the precise quotient.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m trying to win the 'Best Value' award at the school fair, but these two lemonade recipes are totally different: one uses 10 scoops of sugar for every 5 gallons, and the other uses 12 scoops for every 6 gallons. My friend says the second one is 'sweeter' because 12 is a bigger number, but I feel like we need to know exactly how much sugar is hiding in just one single gallon to really compare them. If we want to find that 'magic number' for one gallon, how do we get there from these big batches?

misconceptions.title: Magnitude Bias
misconceptions.description: A student might assume the 12-scoop recipe is sweeter simply because 12 is greater than 10, ignoring the total volume of liquid.

misconceptions.title: Subtraction Strategy
misconceptions.description: A student might think the difference is what matters (e.g., $10 - 5 = 5$ and $12 - 6 = 6$), concluding the 12-scoop batch is sweeter by one "unit."

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Preference
misconceptions.description: Students may struggle to find a unit rate if the division results in a fraction or decimal, leading them to round or guess instead of using the precise quotient.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you poured that 5-gallon batch into 5 separate one-gallon jars, how many scoops would end up in each jar?"
"Wait—does the 'sweetness' change if you drink a tiny sip versus the whole bucket?"
Okay, so if both recipes end up having the exact same amount of sugar for every 1 gallon, what does that tell us about the '12 vs 10' argument?
sections.generalization: I have this hunch that if you have a huge ratio, you can ALWAYS shrink it down to a 'per 1' number and it will still describe the exact same flavor or speed... or is there a point where shrinking the numbers makes them mean something totally different?
sections.inference: I found this third recipe called 'Mega-Sweet' that uses 15 scoops for 3 gallons, and another one that uses 20 scoops for 4 gallons. If they both have the same 'per gallon' amount, is there a way to figure out how many scoops we'd need for a giant 10-gallon tank without having to draw out every single gallon?

6.rp.a.3.a_growing-green-glaze.title: The Growing Green Glaze
6.rp.a.3.a_growing-green-glaze.standardDescription: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.
6.rp.a.3.a_growing-green-glaze.description: Teach Zippy about The Growing Green Glaze
6.rp.a.3.a_growing-green-glaze.problemStatement: I'm helping in the pottery studio and we found a recipe for 'Seafoam Green' glaze that uses 2 scoops of yellow powder for every 3 scoops of blue. We need to make a bunch of different sized batches for different classes, but I'm worried that if we don't keep the list of scoops perfectly lined up, some classes will end up with a 'Lime' color and others will get 'Ocean' blue. If we start a master list of how many yellow and blue scoops to use for any size jar, what's the secret to making sure every single row on our list actually makes the exact same shade of green?
6.rp.a.3.a_growing-green-glaze.teachingPrompt: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.
targetConcepts: Students can generate a table of equivalent ratios and identify the multiplicative relationship between rows.
Students can represent ratio pairs as coordinates and interpret the resulting linear pattern on a coordinate plane.
misconceptions: Additive Growth Pattern (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may add the same amount to both quantities (e.g., 2:3 becomes 3:4, then 4:5) instead of multiplying by a common factor.
Coordinate Swapping (misinterpretation_of_representation): Students might mix up the x and y axes when plotting, resulting in a line with a reciprocal slope that describes a different ratio.

Internal vs. External Scaling (partial_concept_model): Students might identify the +1 difference between 2 and 3 and attempt to maintain that difference in all table rows (e.g., 10 yellow and 11 blue) instead of scaling the 2:3 relationship.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm helping in the pottery studio and we found a recipe for 'Seafoam Green' glaze that uses 2 scoops of yellow powder for every 3 scoops of blue. We need to make a bunch of different sized batches for different classes, but I'm worried that if we don't keep the list of scoops perfectly lined up, some classes will end up with a 'Lime' color and others will get 'Ocean' blue. If we start a master list of how many yellow and blue scoops to use for any size jar, what's the secret to making sure every single row on our list actually makes the exact same shade of green?

misconceptions.title: Additive Growth Pattern
misconceptions.description: Students may add the same amount to both quantities (e.g., 2:3 becomes 3:4, then 4:5) instead of multiplying by a common factor.

misconceptions.title: Coordinate Swapping
misconceptions.description: Students might mix up the x and y axes when plotting, resulting in a line with a reciprocal slope that describes a different ratio.

misconceptions.title: Internal vs. External Scaling
misconceptions.description: Students might identify the +1 difference between 2 and 3 and attempt to maintain that difference in all table rows (e.g., 10 yellow and 11 blue) instead of scaling the 2:3 relationship.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you look at the yellow column, how many 'original batches' are hiding inside a row that has 10 scoops?"
"Wait—if the yellow scoops double, what has to happen to the blue scoops so the color doesn't change?"
If we put these pairs on a graph, do the dots wander around or do they all seem to follow one specific path?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you plot these 'same-color' batches on a graph, the dots will ALWAYS line up in a perfectly straight line that points right at the corner $(0,0)$... or is there a way to have the same ratio but the dots end up all over the place?
sections.inference: I found this other 'Forest Green' recipe that uses 4 scoops of yellow for every 6 scoops of blue, and it's making me wonder if it's actually just the same color as our 'Seafoam' one. If we put both recipes into one big table, is there a way to tell if they are the exact same shade before we even mix them? And like, if we needed to make a massive vat with 20 scoops of yellow, how many blue scoops would keep that same 'Forest' look?

6.rp.a.3.b_lawn-mowing-marathon.title: The Lawn Mowing Marathon
6.rp.a.3.b_lawn-mowing-marathon.standardDescription: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed. For example, if it took 7 hours to mow 4 lawns, then at that rate, how many lawns could be mowed in 35 hours? At what rate were lawns being mowed?
6.rp.a.3.b_lawn-mowing-marathon.description: Teach Zippy about The Lawn Mowing Marathon
6.rp.a.3.b_lawn-mowing-marathon.problemStatement: My older brother is trying to make some extra money this summer, and he told me it took him exactly 6 hours to mow 4 lawns in our neighborhood. He wants to sign up for a huge 'mowing marathon' on Saturday where he'd work for 15 hours straight at that same pace. If he doesn't slow down at all, how many lawns is he actually going to finish by the time he's done?
6.rp.a.3.b_lawn-mowing-marathon.teachingPrompt: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed. For example, if it took 7 hours to mow 4 lawns, then at that rate, how many lawns could be mowed in 35 hours? At what rate were lawns being mowed?
targetConcepts: Students can determine a unit rate (lawns per hour or hours per lawn) and apply it to a new quantity of time.
Students can use multiplicative scaling to solve rate problems where the target value is not a whole-number multiple of the original value.
misconceptions: Additive Thinking (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students might think that because 15 hours is 9 more than 6 hours, he will mow 9 more lawns ($4 + 9 = 13$).

Inverse Rate Application (procedural_overgeneralization): Students may calculate $6 \div 4 = 1.5$ but then divide the 15 hours by 1.5 instead of recognizing if they found "hours per lawn" or "lawns per hour."
Whole Number Bias (whole_number_bias): Students may struggle because 15 is not a multiple of 6, leading them to round the time to 12 or 18 hours to get a "cleaner" number of lawns.

sections.studentPrompt: My older brother is trying to make some extra money this summer, and he told me it took him exactly 6 hours to mow 4 lawns in our neighborhood. He wants to sign up for a huge 'mowing marathon' on Saturday where he'd work for 15 hours straight at that same pace. If he doesn't slow down at all, how many lawns is he actually going to finish by the time he's done?

misconceptions.title: Additive Thinking
misconceptions.description: Students might think that because 15 hours is 9 more than 6 hours, he will mow 9 more lawns ($4 + 9 = 13$).

misconceptions.title: Inverse Rate Application
misconceptions.description: Students may calculate $6 \div 4 = 1.5$ but then divide the 15 hours by 1.5 instead of recognizing if they found "hours per lawn" or "lawns per hour."

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Bias
misconceptions.description: Students may struggle because 15 is not a multiple of 6, leading them to round the time to 12 or 18 hours to get a "cleaner" number of lawns.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If he mows for 12 hours instead of 6, how many lawns does he get done? What about 3 hours?"
"Wait—does the 'time it takes for just one lawn' ever change while he's working?"
Okay, so if he mows 4 lawns in 6 hours, does that mean he's doing more than one lawn an hour or less?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you know how many lawns he mows in 6 hours, you can ALWAYS just multiply that number by 2.5 to find out what he does in 15 hours... or is there a way that 'multiplying by the jump' doesn't work for every situation?
sections.inference: I found this other kid who says he can mow 3 lawns in only 4 hours. If they both started at the same time and worked for a full 20-hour week, do you think there's a way to figure out who would finish more lawns without just drawing out a huge table for every single hour? And like, would the 'winner' change if they only worked for 2 hours?

6.rp.a.3.c_phone-battery-panic.title: The Phone Battery Panic
6.rp.a.3.c_phone-battery-panic.standardDescription: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30/100 of a quantity means 30% of the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
6.rp.a.3.c_phone-battery-panic.description: Teach Zippy about The Phone Battery Panic
6.rp.a.3.c_phone-battery-panic.problemStatement: My phone screen says I have exactly 20% of my battery left, and it also says that means I have 60 minutes of power remaining. I’m trying to figure out how long this phone actually lasts on a full charge, but I don't know if I should be multiplying the numbers or dividing them. If 20 minutes out of every 100 minutes is 20%, how do we use my '60 minutes' to find the total time for the whole 100%?
6.rp.a.3.c_phone-battery-panic.teachingPrompt: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30/100 of a quantity means 30% of the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
targetConcepts: Students can use ratio reasoning (such as double number lines or scaling) to find the whole quantity when given a part and a percentage.
Students can define a percentage as a rate per 100 and apply that relationship to non-100 totals.
misconceptions: Additive Percentage Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students might think that because 20% is 80 away from 100%, they should just add 80 minutes to the 60 minutes they already have.

Whole Number Bias (whole_number_bias): Students may see the numbers 60 and 20 and immediately divide 60 by 20 to get "3," but then fail to realize that 3 represents how many "20% chunks" fit into the current 60 minutes, rather than the total.

Part-Whole Inversion (partial_concept_model): Students might try to find 20% of 60 minutes ($0.20 \times 60 = 12$) instead of recognizing that 60 is the part and they are searching for the larger total.

sections.studentPrompt: My phone screen says I have exactly 20% of my battery left, and it also says that means I have 60 minutes of power remaining. I’m trying to figure out how long this phone actually lasts on a full charge, but I don't know if I should be multiplying the numbers or dividing them. If 20 minutes out of every 100 minutes is 20%, how do we use my '60 minutes' to find the total time for the whole 100%?

misconceptions.title: Additive Percentage Error
misconceptions.description: Students might think that because 20% is 80 away from 100%, they should just add 80 minutes to the 60 minutes they already have.

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Bias
misconceptions.description: Students may see the numbers 60 and 20 and immediately divide 60 by 20 to get "3," but then fail to realize that 3 represents how many "20% chunks" fit into the current 60 minutes, rather than the total.

misconceptions.title: Part-Whole Inversion
misconceptions.description: Students might try to find 20% of 60 minutes ($0.20 \times 60 = 12$) instead of recognizing that 60 is the part and they are searching for the larger total.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If 20% is 60 minutes, how many minutes would a 10% 'sliver' of the battery be?"
"Wait—how many of those 20% 'chunks' would it take to make the full 100% battery?"
Does it make sense for the total battery time to be bigger or smaller than the 60 minutes you have left?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you want to find the 'total' of something and you only know a piece of it, you can ALWAYS just find what 1% is worth first and then multiply that by 100 to get the answer... or is there a faster way that works every time?
sections.inference: I found this other battery pack that says '15% Charged' and it has 30 minutes left. If this new battery pack followed the same rule as the first one, do you think it would last longer or shorter than the first phone when they both start at 100%? And like, is there a way to figure out the total time for ANY battery if you just know the minutes for a tiny percentage?

6.rp.a.3.d_giant-blueprints.title: The Giant Blueprints
6.rp.a.3.d_giant-blueprints.standardDescription: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
6.rp.a.3.d_giant-blueprints.description: Teach Zippy about The Giant Blueprints
6.rp.a.3.d_giant-blueprints.problemStatement: I'm helping my dad build a giant model of a superhero tower, and the instructions say the base needs to be exactly 108 inches wide. But my tape measure only shows feet, and I don't want to sit there counting every single little tick mark until I hit 108. If there are 12 inches in every single foot, is there a way to figure out exactly where the 'foot' mark is on my tape measure without just counting by ones?
6.rp.a.3.d_giant-blueprints.teachingPrompt: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
targetConcepts: Students can use ratio reasoning to convert between measurement units by identifying the unit rate (e.g., 12 in / 1 ft).
Students can transform units appropriately by choosing multiplication or division based on the direction of the conversion.
misconceptions: Additive Conversion (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may think they need to subtract 12 from 108 repeatedly or add 12 to 1 and assume the relationship stays the same by adding.
Inverse Operation (procedural_overgeneralization): Students might multiply 108 by 12 because they know the numbers are related, resulting in an answer in 'square inches' or a massive number of feet.
Unit Magnitude Bias (whole_number_bias): Students may assume that because 'inches' are smaller than 'feet,' the final number for feet should be larger than 108.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm helping my dad build a giant model of a superhero tower, and the instructions say the base needs to be exactly 108 inches wide. But my tape measure only shows feet, and I don't want to sit there counting every single little tick mark until I hit 108. If there are 12 inches in every single foot, is there a way to figure out exactly where the 'foot' mark is on my tape measure without just counting by ones?

misconceptions.title: Additive Conversion
misconceptions.description: Students may think they need to subtract 12 from 108 repeatedly or add 12 to 1 and assume the relationship stays the same by adding.

misconceptions.title: Inverse Operation
misconceptions.description: Students might multiply 108 by 12 because they know the numbers are related, resulting in an answer in 'square inches' or a massive number of feet.

misconceptions.title: Unit Magnitude Bias
misconceptions.description: Students may assume that because 'inches' are smaller than 'feet,' the final number for feet should be larger than 108.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you have 24 inches, how many of those 12-inch 'foot' chunks are you actually holding?"
"Wait—does the number of inches inside a single foot ever change as the tower gets taller?"
Okay, so if the number of inches is getting way bigger, what is happening to the number of feet to keep the length the same?
sections.generalization: I have a half-confident hunch that if you're switching from a small unit to a bigger one, your total number is ALWAYS going to get smaller... or is there some weird measurement out there where the number actually gets bigger when the unit does?
sections.inference: I found this other part of the tower that needs to be 15 feet long, but now I only have a small ruler that's marked in inches. If 1 foot is the same as 12 inches and 2 feet is the same as 24, do you think there's a trick to find the total inches for 15 feet without drawing 15 separate rulers? And like, would that same trick help us find the inches for ANY number of feet?

6.rp.a.3_blue-slushie-mystery.title: The Blue Slushie Mystery
6.rp.a.3_blue-slushie-mystery.standardDescription: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations.
6.rp.a.3_blue-slushie-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Blue Slushie Mystery
6.rp.a.3_blue-slushie-mystery.problemStatement: I'm helping at the concession stand and we have this giant container of blue raspberry slushie that uses 3 cups of syrup for every 10 cups of water. Someone just dumped in a whole 12-cup bottle of syrup by mistake, and now I have to figure out how much water to add so it doesn't taste like pure sugar. If the recipe has to stay exactly the same as the original, how much water do we need for this giant mistake?
6.rp.a.3_blue-slushie-mystery.teachingPrompt: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations.
targetConcepts: Students can use multiplicative reasoning to create equivalent ratios in a real-world context.
Students can identify the "scale factor" between two quantities and apply it consistently to maintain a proportional relationship.
misconceptions: Additive Thinking (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): A student might think that since 9 more cups of syrup were added ($12 - 3 = 9$), they should simply add 9 more cups of water ($10 + 9 = 19$).

Whole Number Bias (whole_number_bias): A student might try to divide 12 by 10 because they are the two most recent numbers mentioned, rather than looking for the relationship between the syrup amounts.

Internal vs. External Ratio Confusion (partial_concept_model): A student might confuse the part-to-part ratio (3:10) with a part-to-whole ratio and try to use 13 as a divisor for the new syrup amount.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm helping at the concession stand and we have this giant container of blue raspberry slushie that uses 3 cups of syrup for every 10 cups of water. Someone just dumped in a whole 12-cup bottle of syrup by mistake, and now I have to figure out how much water to add so it doesn't taste like pure sugar. If the recipe has to stay exactly the same as the original, how much water do we need for this giant mistake?

misconceptions.title: Additive Thinking
misconceptions.description: A student might think that since 9 more cups of syrup were added ($12 - 3 = 9$), they should simply add 9 more cups of water ($10 + 9 = 19$).

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Bias
misconceptions.description: A student might try to divide 12 by 10 because they are the two most recent numbers mentioned, rather than looking for the relationship between the syrup amounts.

misconceptions.title: Internal vs. External Ratio Confusion
misconceptions.description: A student might confuse the part-to-part ratio (3:10) with a part-to-whole ratio and try to use 13 as a divisor for the new syrup amount.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you look at the 3 cups of syrup jumping up to 12 cups, how many of those 'original' batches are we actually making now?"
"Wait—does the 10-cup water amount stay the same, or does it have to grow by the same 'multiplier' as the syrup?"
If we have 4 times as much syrup as the recipe calls for, what does that tell us about the total amount of water we need?
sections.generalization: I have this hunch that if you want a flavor to stay exactly the same, you ALWAYS have to multiply both ingredients by the same number... or could you just add the same amount to both and have it still taste right?
sections.inference: I found this other 'Super Sour' recipe that uses 2 cups of lemon juice for every 5 cups of sugar water. If we wanted to make enough for the whole school and used 20 cups of lemon juice, is there a way to figure out the total amount of sugar water without drawing out every single cup? And like, would that same trick work if we only had 1 cup of lemon juice left?

6.rp.a_perfect-purple-paint.title: The Perfect Purple Paint
6.rp.a_perfect-purple-paint.standardDescription: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
6.rp.a_perfect-purple-paint.description: Teach Zippy about The Perfect Purple Paint
6.rp.a_perfect-purple-paint.problemStatement: I’m trying to help my art teacher mix a huge batch of 'Perfect Purple' paint for a mural, but I'm kind of confused because the small test batch used 2 cups of blue and 3 cups of red. If I want to make a much bigger bucket that looks exactly the same, but I already accidentally poured in 12 cups of blue, how much red do I need to add so it doesn't end up looking too blue or too red?
6.rp.a_perfect-purple-paint.teachingPrompt: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
targetConcepts: Students can use multiplicative reasoning to scale a ratio up or down while maintaining the relationship between quantities.
Students can identify and describe the constant ratio (rate) that defines a mixture or comparison.
misconceptions: Additive Thinking (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): A student might think that since the original recipe had one more cup of red than blue ($3 - 2 = 1$), they should just add 13 cups of red to the 12 cups of blue.

Whole Number Bias (whole_number_bias): A student might try to divide 12 by 3 because it’s a 'clean' number, rather than identifying the relationship between the 2 cups of blue and the 12 cups of blue.
Scaling the Wrong Quantity (partial_concept_model): A student might correctly find a scale factor of 6 for the blue paint but then add 6 to the red paint ($3 + 6 = 9$) instead of multiplying.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m trying to help my art teacher mix a huge batch of 'Perfect Purple' paint for a mural, but I'm kind of confused because the small test batch used 2 cups of blue and 3 cups of red. If I want to make a much bigger bucket that looks exactly the same, but I already accidentally poured in 12 cups of blue, how much red do I need to add so it doesn't end up looking too blue or too red?

misconceptions.title: Additive Thinking
misconceptions.description: A student might think that since the original recipe had one more cup of red than blue ($3 - 2 = 1$), they should just add 13 cups of red to the 12 cups of blue.

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Bias
misconceptions.description: A student might try to divide 12 by 3 because it’s a 'clean' number, rather than identifying the relationship between the 2 cups of blue and the 12 cups of blue.

misconceptions.title: Scaling the Wrong Quantity
misconceptions.description: A student might correctly find a scale factor of 6 for the blue paint but then add 6 to the red paint ($3 + 6 = 9$) instead of multiplying.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you look at the 2 cups of blue jumping up to 12 cups, how many of those 'original' 2-cup batches are hiding in the big bucket?"
"If you keep adding the same amount of red for every scoop of blue, does that 2-to-3 relationship ever actually change?"
Wait—if you have 6 times more blue than you started with, what has to happen to the red so the color stays the perfect shade?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you want a mixture to stay exactly the same, you ALWAYS have to add the same number of cups to both parts—like if I add 10 more blue, I just add 10 more red—or is there a way that actually ruins the flavor or color?
sections.inference: I found this other recipe for 'Sunset Orange' that uses 4 scoops of yellow for every 5 scoops of red. If we wanted to make a giant vat for the whole school and we used 40 scoops of yellow, is there a way to figure out how much red we'd need without just drawing out every single scoop?

6.sp.a.1_variety-search.title: The Variety Search
6.sp.a.1_variety-search.standardDescription: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers. For example, 'How old am I?' is not a statistical question, but 'How old are the students in my school?' is a statistical question because one anticipates variability in students' ages.
6.sp.a.1_variety-search.description: Teach Zippy about The Variety Search
6.sp.a.1_variety-search.problemStatement:
I'm helping the school newspaper write a survey! I have two questions I want to ask. 

* **Question A:** 'How many pets does the principal have?' 
* **Question B:** 'How many pets do the 6th graders have?' 

My editor says only one of these is a 'statistical question.' I don't get it—they both use numbers and they are both about pets. Why is one 'statistical' and the other just a regular fact? Does 'statistical' just mean more work?
6.sp.a.1_variety-search.teachingPrompt: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers. For example, 'How old am I?' is not a statistical question, but 'How old are the students in my school?' is a statistical question because one anticipates variability in students' ages.
misconceptions: The 'Number' Assumption (surface_feature_matching): Students often think that if a question involves a number or math, it is automatically statistical. They miss the requirement for **variability** (expecting many different answers from a group).

The 'Multiple People' Misconception (partial_concept_model): A student might think that asking 100 people "What is the capital of France?" is a statistical question because they are asking a large group. However, because there is only one correct answer (Paris), there is no *anticipated* variability in the data.

The 'Fact' vs. 'Data' Blur (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students may think a question like "Do you like pizza?" isn't statistical because it’s a simple Yes/No, failing to realize that the *distribution* of those answers across a population is exactly what statistics measures.

sections.studentPrompt:
I'm helping the school newspaper write a survey! I have two questions I want to ask. 

* **Question A:** 'How many pets does the principal have?' 
* **Question B:** 'How many pets do the 6th graders have?' 

My editor says only one of these is a 'statistical question.' I don't get it—they both use numbers and they are both about pets. Why is one 'statistical' and the other just a regular fact? Does 'statistical' just mean more work?

misconceptions.title: The 'Number' Assumption
misconceptions.description: Students often think that if a question involves a number or math, it is automatically statistical. They miss the requirement for **variability** (expecting many different answers from a group).

misconceptions.title: The 'Multiple People' Misconception
misconceptions.description: A student might think that asking 100 people "What is the capital of France?" is a statistical question because they are asking a large group. However, because there is only one correct answer (Paris), there is no *anticipated* variability in the data.

misconceptions.title: The 'Fact' vs. 'Data' Blur
misconceptions.description: Students may think a question like "Do you like pizza?" isn't statistical because it’s a simple Yes/No, failing to realize that the *distribution* of those answers across a population is exactly what statistics measures.

sections.patternRecognition:
* "If I ask 20 people Question A, will I get 20 different answers, or will everyone tell me the exact same number (whatever the principal owns)?"
* "Wait—if you ask the 6th graders Question B, do you expect everyone to say '2 pets,' or do you expect some zeros, some ones, and maybe a five?"
* "Does a statistical question look for a 'snapshot' of a whole group's differences, or just a single 'fixed point'?"
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that for a question to be statistical, it ALWAYS has to be about a group of people or things, rather than just one person... or can you ask a statistical question about a single dog?
sections.inference:
I have a new question: **'How many centimeters long is this specific pencil?'** Is that statistical? 

Now, what if I change it to: **'How many centimeters long are the pencils in the lost-and-found box?'** Which one allows the data to 'wiggle' (vary), and which one is fixed?

6.sp.a.2_shape-of-the-class.title: The Shape of the Class
6.sp.a.2_shape-of-the-class.standardDescription: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.
6.sp.a.2_shape-of-the-class.description: Teach Zippy about The Shape of the Class
6.sp.a.2_shape-of-the-class.problemStatement: I collected data on how many books my classmates read over summer break. I made a dot plot, and most of the dots are clustered around 2 and 3 books, but there's one lonely dot way out at 15! My teacher told me to describe the 'distribution.' I know the 'center' is somewhere near 3, but what does she mean by 'shape' and 'spread'? Is my data a 'circle' or a 'square'? And how does that one kid who read 15 books change the whole picture?
6.sp.a.2_shape-of-the-class.teachingPrompt: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.
targetConcepts: Students can identify the center of a data distribution (mean or median).
Students can describe the spread (variability) of a data set.
misconceptions: Shape as Geometric Figures (semantic_overgeneralization): Students often hear the word "shape" and think of literal geometry (circles, triangles). They struggle to use statistical terms like **symmetrical**, **skewed**, **peaked**, or **gaps**.

Center vs. Most Frequent (partial_concept_model): A student might think the "center" is always the middle of the number line (e.g., if the scale is 0–20, the center must be 10), rather than where the actual data points are concentrated.

Ignoring the 'Tail' (surface_feature_matching): When a distribution has an outlier (like the 15 books), students often ignore it when describing the "spread," thinking the spread only counts where "most" of the people are.

sections.studentPrompt: I collected data on how many books my classmates read over summer break. I made a dot plot, and most of the dots are clustered around 2 and 3 books, but there's one lonely dot way out at 15! My teacher told me to describe the 'distribution.' I know the 'center' is somewhere near 3, but what does she mean by 'shape' and 'spread'? Is my data a 'circle' or a 'square'? And how does that one kid who read 15 books change the whole picture?

misconceptions.title: Shape as Geometric Figures
misconceptions.description: Students often hear the word "shape" and think of literal geometry (circles, triangles). They struggle to use statistical terms like **symmetrical**, **skewed**, **peaked**, or **gaps**.

misconceptions.title: Center vs. Most Frequent
misconceptions.description: A student might think the "center" is always the middle of the number line (e.g., if the scale is 0–20, the center must be 10), rather than where the actual data points are concentrated.

misconceptions.title: Ignoring the 'Tail'
misconceptions.description: When a distribution has an outlier (like the 15 books), students often ignore it when describing the "spread," thinking the spread only counts where "most" of the people are.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you look at the dots from a distance, does the 'hill' look even on both sides, or does it have a long 'tail' stretching out to the right?"
"Wait—if everyone in class read exactly 3 books, would there be any 'spread' at all? What does it mean if the dots are scattered from 0 all the way to 15?"
Does the 'center' feel like the balance point of a seesaw? If you put a heavy weight at 15, which way does the center have to slide?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that the 'center' of a data set is ALWAYS the same number as the 'most popular' answer... or can the center be a number that nobody actually picked?
sections.inference: Imagine we surveyed a class of professional speed-readers. Their dot plot is perfectly **symmetrical** with a center at 50 books and a very **small spread** (everyone read between 48 and 52). How would that graph look different from our class graph that is **skewed** and has a **large spread**? Which group is more 'predictable'?

6.sp.a.3_team-captain-report.title: The Team Captain's Report
6.sp.a.3_team-captain-report.standardDescription: Recognize that a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation describes how its values vary with a single number.
6.sp.a.3_team-captain-report.description: Teach Zippy about The Team Captain's Report
6.sp.a.3_team-captain-report.problemStatement: I'm comparing two basketball teams. Team A’s players all scored exactly 10 points each.
