Team B had one player score 30 points, but everyone else scored 0. My coach says both teams have the same 'average' (mean) of 10 points. If the 'center' is the same, how can I use math to show that Team A is consistent while Team B is all over the place? Is there a different kind of 'magic number' that tells me how 'spread out' the points are, instead of just where the middle is?
6.sp.a.3_team-captain-report.teachingPrompt: Recognize that a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation describes how its values vary with a single number.
targetConcepts: Students can distinguish between a measure of center (summarizing values) and a measure of variation (describing spread).
Students understand that the mean/median provides a "typical" value while the range/MAD provides a "consistency" value.
misconceptions: The 'Average' Equals 'Identity' (partial_concept_model): Students often think the mean or median tells the *whole* story. They might assume that if two sets have a mean of 10, the data sets must look identical, failing to realize that "center" ignores how far the numbers are from that center.

Mixing Up the 'What' and the 'How' (semantic_overgeneralization): A student might try to use the **Range** to describe the center, or use the **Mean** to describe the variation. They struggle to categorize which numbers summarize "typicalness" (center) and which summarize "consistency" (variation).

Variation as an Error (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students sometimes think a large "variation" or "spread" means the math is wrong or the data is "bad," rather than understanding it's just a descriptive characteristic of that specific group.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm comparing two basketball teams. Team A’s players all scored exactly 10 points each. Team B had one player score 30 points, but everyone else scored 0. My coach says both teams have the same 'average' (mean) of 10 points. If the 'center' is the same, how can I use math to show that Team A is consistent while Team B is all over the place? Is there a different kind of 'magic number' that tells me how 'spread out' the points are, instead of just where the middle is?

misconceptions.title: The 'Average' Equals 'Identity'
misconceptions.description: Students often think the mean or median tells the *whole* story. They might assume that if two sets have a mean of 10, the data sets must look identical, failing to realize that "center" ignores how far the numbers are from that center.

misconceptions.title: Mixing Up the 'What' and the 'How'
misconceptions.description: A student might try to use the **Range** to describe the center, or use the **Mean** to describe the variation. They struggle to categorize which numbers summarize "typicalness" (center) and which summarize "consistency" (variation).

misconceptions.title: Variation as an Error
misconceptions.description: Students sometimes think a large "variation" or "spread" means the math is wrong or the data is "bad," rather than understanding it's just a descriptive characteristic of that specific group.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If I tell you the 'center' of a dataset is 50, do you know if the smallest number is 49 or 0? What other information do you need to 'see' the spread?"
"Wait—if a measure of center is like the 'anchor' of the data, is the measure of variation like the 'length of the rope' showing how far the data can float away?"
If Team A has a Range of 0 (10 minus 10) and Team B has a Range of 30 (30 minus 0), which number tells you about the 'gap' between the best and worst scores?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you have a huge 'measure of variation' (like a big Range), the 'measure of center' (the Mean) becomes LESS useful for predicting what a single person in that group actually did... or does the center stay just as helpful no matter the spread?
sections.inference:
Imagine two pizza shops. Both have a 'Mean' delivery time of 30 minutes. 
* **Shop 1** has a **Variation (Range)** of 2 minutes. 
* **Shop 2** has a **Variation (Range)** of 60 minutes. 

If you are starving and need your pizza exactly at 30 minutes, which shop's 'Measure of Center' do you trust more? Why does a smaller variation make the center more 'reliable'?

6.sp.a_mystery-of-the-average-apple.title: The Mystery of the Average Apple
6.sp.a_mystery-of-the-average-apple.standardDescription: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers. Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.
6.sp.a_mystery-of-the-average-apple.description: Teach Zippy about The Mystery of the Average Apple
6.sp.a_mystery-of-the-average-apple.problemStatement: I asked two questions today. First, I asked my teacher, 'How tall are you?' and she said 65 inches. Then, I asked everyone in my class, 'How many minutes did you spend on homework last night?' My friend says the second one is a 'statistical question' because the answer isn't just one number—it's a whole bunch of different numbers. If I get 25 different answers, how am I supposed to describe them? Do I just list them all, or is there a way to describe the 'middle' and how 'spread out' everyone is?
6.sp.a_mystery-of-the-average-apple.teachingPrompt: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers. Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.
targetConcepts: Students can distinguish between statistical and non-statistical questions based on anticipated variability.
Students understand that data sets have a distribution characterized by center (median/mean), spread (interquartile range/mean absolute deviation), and shape (symmetry/skew).
misconceptions: The Single-Answer Trap (partial_concept_model): Students often think any question involving a number is statistical. They struggle to see that "How many pets does the President have?" has one factual answer (non-statistical), while "How many pets do students in the 6th grade have?" expects **variability** (statistical).

The 'Average' Obsession (procedural_overgeneralization): A student might think the "Mean" (average) is the only thing that matters. They ignore the **spread** (range) or **shape** (clusters/outliers), failing to realize that a class where everyone studied 20 minutes looks very different from a class where half studied 0 and half studied 40, even if the "middle" is the same.
Data Point vs. Distribution (surface_feature_matching): When looking at a dot plot, a student might focus on one specific dot rather than the "hill" or "gap" formed by the whole group of dots.

sections.studentPrompt: I asked two questions today. First, I asked my teacher, 'How tall are you?' and she said 65 inches. Then, I asked everyone in my class, 'How many minutes did you spend on homework last night?' My friend says the second one is a 'statistical question' because the answer isn't just one number—it's a whole bunch of different numbers. If I get 25 different answers, how am I supposed to describe them? Do I just list them all, or is there a way to describe the 'middle' and how 'spread out' everyone is?

misconceptions.title: The Single-Answer Trap
misconceptions.description: Students often think any question involving a number is statistical. They struggle to see that "How many pets does the President have?" has one factual answer (non-statistical), while "How many pets do students in the 6th grade have?" expects **variability** (statistical).

misconceptions.title: The 'Average' Obsession
misconceptions.description: A student might think the "Mean" (average) is the only thing that matters. They ignore the **spread** (range) or **shape** (clusters/outliers), failing to realize that a class where everyone studied 20 minutes looks very different from a class where half studied 0 and half studied 40, even if the "middle" is the same.

misconceptions.title: Data Point vs. Distribution
misconceptions.description: When looking at a dot plot, a student might focus on one specific dot rather than the "hill" or "gap" formed by the whole group of dots.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If I ask 'What color is your shirt?', is that statistical if I only ask *you*? What if I ask the whole school?"
"Wait—if most kids studied for 15 minutes, but one kid studied for 4 hours, does that one 'outlier' pull the whole 'middle' of the data away from the rest of the group?"
If you draw a dot plot and all the dots are clustered together, does that mean the data has a 'small spread' or a 'large spread'?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that a statistical question ALWAYS has to have more than one answer... or can a question be statistical even if everyone happens to give you the same number?
sections.inference: I asked 100 people how many siblings they have. Most said 1 or 2, but a few said 0, and one person said 12! If I look at the **shape** of this data on a graph, do you think it will look like a perfect mountain in the middle, or will it have a long 'tail' pointing toward the 12? And if I remove that '12', does the **center** of my data move left or right?

6.sp.b.4_box-plot-blueprint.title: The Box Plot Blueprint
6.sp.b.4_box-plot-blueprint.standardDescription: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms, and box plots.
6.sp.b.4_box-plot-blueprint.description: Teach Zippy about The Box Plot Blueprint
6.sp.b.4_box-plot-blueprint.problemStatement: I collected data on the number of texts my friends sent yesterday: $\{2, 5, 7, 10, 12, 14, 30\}$. I want to make a **Box Plot** to show the 'spread' of our texting habits. My brother says a box plot is like a 'five-number summary' turned into a picture. I found the **Minimum** (2) and the **Maximum** (30), but how do I find the 'middle of the middle' for the box? And once I draw the box and the 'whiskers,' does the length of the whisker tell me there are *more* people out there, or just that the numbers are *farther apart*?
6.sp.b.4_box-plot-blueprint.teachingPrompt: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms, and box plots.
targetConcepts: Students can construct dot plots, histograms, and box plots from a raw numerical data set.
Students can calculate the five-number summary (Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max) required for a box plot.
misconceptions: The 'Number of Dots' Error (partial_concept_model): Students often think a longer whisker or a wider box means there are more data points in that section. They need to understand that each of the four sections of a box plot (the two whiskers and the two halves of the box) always represents exactly **25%** of the data, regardless of how long the section looks.

The Median Misplacement (procedural_overgeneralization): When calculating the **Lower Quartile (Q1)** and **Upper Quartile (Q3)**, students often forget to exclude the Median itself if there is an odd number of data points, or they include it twice, leading to "shifted" boxes.

Histogram vs. Bar Graph (surface_feature_matching): Students often treat a **Histogram** like a categorical bar graph, leaving spaces between the bars. They struggle to realize that in a histogram, the bars must touch because they represent continuous "intervals" of numbers (like 0–10, 11–20).

sections.studentPrompt: I collected data on the number of texts my friends sent yesterday: $\{2, 5, 7, 10, 12, 14, 30\}$. I want to make a **Box Plot** to show the 'spread' of our texting habits. My brother says a box plot is like a 'five-number summary' turned into a picture. I found the **Minimum** (2) and the **Maximum** (30), but how do I find the 'middle of the middle' for the box? And once I draw the box and the 'whiskers,' does the length of the whisker tell me there are *more* people out there, or just that the numbers are *farther apart*?

misconceptions.title: The 'Number of Dots' Error
misconceptions.description: Students often think a longer whisker or a wider box means there are more data points in that section. They need to understand that each of the four sections of a box plot (the two whiskers and the two halves of the box) always represents exactly **25%** of the data, regardless of how long the section looks.

misconceptions.title: The Median Misplacement
misconceptions.description: When calculating the **Lower Quartile (Q1)** and **Upper Quartile (Q3)**, students often forget to exclude the Median itself if there is an odd number of data points, or they include it twice, leading to "shifted" boxes.

misconceptions.title: Histogram vs. Bar Graph
misconceptions.description: Students often treat a **Histogram** like a categorical bar graph, leaving spaces between the bars. They struggle to realize that in a histogram, the bars must touch because they represent continuous "intervals" of numbers (like 0–10, 11–20).

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you line up 100 people by height and split them into 4 equal groups of 25, will the 'tallest group' take up more space on the floor than the 'middle group'?"
"Wait—if the box part of your plot is very tiny, does that mean everyone in the middle of your list had almost the same number of texts?"
In a Histogram, if the bar for '10–20 texts' is the tallest, does that mean that specific range was the most popular 'neighborhood' for your data?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that the Median line in a box plot is ALWAYS exactly in the dead-center of the box... or can the Median 'slide' to one side if the data is skewed?
sections.inference: Imagine we added a new friend to our data who sent **100 texts**. If we update our box plot, which part will stretch the most: the box or the right whisker? Does adding one extreme 'outlier' change where the 'middle 50%' (the box) sits, or does it mostly just pull the whisker way out to the right?

6.sp.b.5.a_headcount-challenge.title: The Headcount Challenge
6.sp.b.5.a_headcount-challenge.standardDescription: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: Reporting the number of observations.
6.sp.b.5.a_headcount-challenge.description: Teach Zippy about The Headcount Challenge
6.sp.b.5.a_headcount-challenge.problemStatement: I'm collecting data for the school cafeteria to see how many chocolate milks are sold each day. I checked the logs for Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday. My friend says my 'number of observations' is 4. But then I looked at the numbers: we sold 45, 50, 48, and 52 milks. Now I'm confused—is my number of observations 4, or is it the total of all those milks (195)? Why does it matter how many days I checked if I already have the big total?
6.sp.b.5.a_headcount-challenge.teachingPrompt: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: Reporting the number of observations.
targetConcepts: Students can identify and report the total number of observations ($n$) in a given data set.
Students understand that each data point represents one specific instance or "observation" within the context.
misconceptions: Sum vs. Count (procedural_overgeneralization): Students often confuse the **sum of the data** (adding all values together) with the **number of observations** (how many pieces of data were collected). They might report $n=195$ instead of $n=4$.

The "Value" Distraction (surface_feature_matching): A student might see a large number in the data set (like 52 milks) and assume that the number of observations must be related to the size of the numbers, rather than the count of the data points.

Zero as "Nothing" (partial_concept_model): If no milks were sold on Friday, the data point is 0. Students often skip this, thinking it doesn't "count" as an observation. In statistics, a zero is a very important observation that must be included in the total count ($n$).

sections.studentPrompt: I'm collecting data for the school cafeteria to see how many chocolate milks are sold each day. I checked the logs for Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday. My friend says my 'number of observations' is 4. But then I looked at the numbers: we sold 45, 50, 48, and 52 milks. Now I'm confused—is my number of observations 4, or is it the total of all those milks (195)? Why does it matter how many days I checked if I already have the big total?

misconceptions.title: Sum vs. Count
misconceptions.description: Students often confuse the **sum of the data** (adding all values together) with the **number of observations** (how many pieces of data were collected). They might report $n=195$ instead of $n=4$.

misconceptions.title: The "Value" Distraction
misconceptions.description: A student might see a large number in the data set (like 52 milks) and assume that the number of observations must be related to the size of the numbers, rather than the count of the data points.

misconceptions.title: Zero as "Nothing"
misconceptions.description: If no milks were sold on Friday, the data point is 0. Students often skip this, thinking it doesn't "count" as an observation. In statistics, a zero is a very important observation that must be included in the total count ($n$).

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you want to know the 'average' milk per day, do you divide the total by the number of milks, or the number of days you watched the cafeteria?"
"Wait—if you only watched for one day, would you trust your data as much as if you watched for 100 days? Does having more 'observations' make your story more reliable?"
If I give you a list of 10 test scores, how many times did someone actually sit down and take a test?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that the more observations ($n$) you have, the ALWAYS more 'accurate' your average will be for the whole school... or can you have too many observations?
sections.inference: Imagine the cafeteria manager says, 'I need a bigger sample size!' If you keep track of the milk for the next 3 weeks, will your 'number of observations' go up or down? And how does that change the way you'd calculate the **Mean** later on?

6.sp.b.5.b_science-fair-audit.title: The Science Fair Audit
6.sp.b.5.b_science-fair-audit.standardDescription: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
6.sp.b.5.b_science-fair-audit.description: Teach Zippy about The Science Fair Audit
6.sp.b.5.b_science-fair-audit.problemStatement:
I’m writing up the results for my Science Fair project! I grew five different bean plants and my data table says: $\{4, 7, 5, 8, 6\}$. My partner says I'm ready to turn it in, but my teacher said, '4 what? 4 elephants? 4 miles?' 

I realized I didn't explain that those numbers are the **heights** of the plants. I measured them with a plastic ruler from the dirt to the tip of the highest leaf. If I just write '6' on my graph, how will people know if my plants are giants or tiny sprouts? And why does it matter if I measured in **inches** or **centimeters**?
6.sp.b.5.b_science-fair-audit.teachingPrompt: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
targetConcepts: Students can identify and describe the attribute being investigated (e.g., height, weight, time).
Students can specify the units of measurement used for a data set.
Students can explain the method or tool used to collect the measurements.
misconceptions: The 'Naked Number' Error (surface_feature_matching): Students often provide a list of raw numbers without labels. They assume the reader knows the context (e.g., reporting "10" instead of "10 grams"), which makes the data impossible to compare or interpret.

Attribute vs. Unit Confusion (partial_concept_model): A student might confuse the **attribute** (what is being measured, like *length*) with the **unit** (the scale used, like *centimeters*). They might say "The attribute is inches," which is incorrect—the attribute is *height*.

Measurement Method Ambiguity (procedural_overgeneralization): Students may forget to explain *how* they got the number. For example, in a "jump" contest, did they measure from the toes or the heels? Without a clear method, the data cannot be replicated by someone else.

sections.studentPrompt:
I’m writing up the results for my Science Fair project! I grew five different bean plants and my data table says: $\{4, 7, 5, 8, 6\}$. My partner says I'm ready to turn it in, but my teacher said, '4 what? 4 elephants? 4 miles?' 

I realized I didn't explain that those numbers are the **heights** of the plants. I measured them with a plastic ruler from the dirt to the tip of the highest leaf. If I just write '6' on my graph, how will people know if my plants are giants or tiny sprouts? And why does it matter if I measured in **inches** or **centimeters**?

misconceptions.title: The 'Naked Number' Error
misconceptions.description: Students often provide a list of raw numbers without labels. They assume the reader knows the context (e.g., reporting "10" instead of "10 grams"), which makes the data impossible to compare or interpret.

misconceptions.title: Attribute vs. Unit Confusion
misconceptions.description: A student might confuse the **attribute** (what is being measured, like *length*) with the **unit** (the scale used, like *centimeters*). They might say "The attribute is inches," which is incorrect—the attribute is *height*.

misconceptions.title: Measurement Method Ambiguity
misconceptions.description: Students may forget to explain *how* they got the number. For example, in a "jump" contest, did they measure from the toes or the heels? Without a clear method, the data cannot be replicated by someone else.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If I tell you the average score on the test was '80,' do you know if that's 80 points out of 100 or 80 points out of 1,000?"
"Wait—if you measure a table in 'paperclips' and I measure it in 'inches,' will our numbers look the same even if the table didn't change size?"
Does the 'attribute' describe the 'personality' of the data (like weight, time, or temperature)?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that the 'unit of measurement' you choose (like miles vs. inches) will ALWAYS change the actual numbers in your data set, but it will NEVER change the 'attribute' you are investigating... is that true?
sections.inference:
Imagine two students are measuring how much water a sponge can hold. 
* **Student A** measures the **volume** in **milliliters** using a graduated cylinder. 
* **Student B** measures the **mass** in **grams** using a digital scale. 

They both get the number '50.' Are they investigating the same attribute? Can we combine their data into one big graph?

6.sp.b.5.c_typical-teenager.title: The Typical Teenager
6.sp.b.5.c_typical-teenager.standardDescription: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
6.sp.b.5.c_typical-teenager.description: Teach Zippy about The Typical Teenager
6.sp.b.5.c_typical-teenager.problemStatement:
I asked 9 friends how much money they spent on snacks this week. Here is my list: **{\$2, \$2, \$3, \$5, \$5, \$5, \$6, \$7, \$45}**. My teacher wants me to find the 'typical' amount spent. 

If I use the **Mean**, I get **\$8.89**. But if I look at my list, *nobody* actually spent \$8.89—almost everyone spent way less! My friend says the \$45 outlier is 'sabotaging' my average. Should I use the **Median** instead? And how do I use the **Interquartile Range (IQR)** to show that most of us are actually pretty similar in our snack spending?
6.sp.b.5.c_typical-teenager.teachingPrompt: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
targetConcepts: Students can calculate measures of center (mean, median) and variability (IQR, MAD).
Students can identify "striking deviations" (outliers) and explain their impact on the mean.
Students can describe the "overall pattern" of a data set using these quantitative measures.
misconceptions: The 'Mean' is Always Boss (partial_concept_model): Students often believe the Mean is the "official" math average. They don't realize that in a skewed data set (like one with a \$45 snack bill), the **Median** ($5) is a much better representation of a "typical" friend than the Mean ($8.89).

The Median 'Middle' Mistake (procedural_overgeneralization): A student might try to find the Median of the *numbers* they see ($2$ and $45$) by picking the number halfway between them (23.5), rather than finding the middle value of the **ordered list**.

MAD vs. IQR Confusion (surface_feature_matching): Students may struggle to choose between **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** and **IQR**. They need to learn that if they use the Mean as their center, they should use MAD for variability; if they use the Median, they should use IQR.

sections.studentPrompt:
I asked 9 friends how much money they spent on snacks this week. Here is my list: **{\$2, \$2, \$3, \$5, \$5, \$5, \$6, \$7, \$45}**. My teacher wants me to find the 'typical' amount spent. 

If I use the **Mean**, I get **\$8.89**. But if I look at my list, *nobody* actually spent \$8.89—almost everyone spent way less! My friend says the \$45 outlier is 'sabotaging' my average. Should I use the **Median** instead? And how do I use the **Interquartile Range (IQR)** to show that most of us are actually pretty similar in our snack spending?

misconceptions.title: The 'Mean' is Always Boss
misconceptions.description: Students often believe the Mean is the "official" math average. They don't realize that in a skewed data set (like one with a \$45 snack bill), the **Median** ($5) is a much better representation of a "typical" friend than the Mean ($8.89).

misconceptions.title: The Median 'Middle' Mistake
misconceptions.description: A student might try to find the Median of the *numbers* they see ($2$ and $45$) by picking the number halfway between them (23.5), rather than finding the middle value of the **ordered list**.

misconceptions.title: MAD vs. IQR Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may struggle to choose between **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** and **IQR**. They need to learn that if they use the Mean as their center, they should use MAD for variability; if they use the Median, they should use IQR.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If we take away the \$45 outlier, what happens to the Mean? What happens to the Median? Which one 'jumped' more?"
"Wait—if the IQR is very small (like \$3), does that mean the 'middle 50%' of your friends are spending almost the exact same amount?"
Does a 'striking deviation' (an outlier) act like a magnet that pulls the Mean toward it, away from the rest of the group?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that the **Mean** will ALWAYS be higher than the **Median** if there is a huge outlier on the high end of the data... or can the Median ever be the bigger number?
sections.inference:
Imagine we surveyed a second group where everyone spent exactly **\$5.00**. 
* The **Mean** is \$5. 
* The **Median** is \$5. 

What would the **IQR** and **MAD** be for this group? If a measure of variability is $0$, does that mean the data is 'perfectly consistent' or 'perfectly broken'?

6.sp.b.5.d_bakery-best-seller.title: The Bakery's Best Seller
6.sp.b.5.d_bakery-best-seller.standardDescription: Relate the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
6.sp.b.5.d_bakery-best-seller.description: Teach Zippy about The Bakery's Best Seller
6.sp.b.5.d_bakery-best-seller.problemStatement: I was looking at how many cupcakes the bakery sells each day, and it's usually around 40 or 50, but last Saturday they had a huge wedding order and sold 500! When I calculated the average, it jumped all the way up to 110 cupcakes, but the baker says that number feels 'wrong' for a normal Tuesday. If we want to tell a new employee what a 'typical' day looks like so they know how many to bake, should we use that 110 number or is there a better way to describe the middle?
6.sp.b.5.d_bakery-best-seller.teachingPrompt: Relate the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
targetConcepts: Students can justify the choice between mean and median based on the presence of outliers and the shape of the distribution.
Students can explain how the context of a problem (e.g., planning inventory) dictates which measure of center is most useful.
misconceptions: Mean-is-Always-Best Bias (procedural_overgeneralization): Students believe the mean is the most "mathematical" and therefore always the most accurate measure of center, regardless of outliers.
Outlier Impact Neglect (partial_concept_model): Students understand how to calculate measures but do not recognize how a single extreme value pulls the mean away from the cluster of data.

Contextual Disconnect (misinterpretation_of_representation): Students treat the data as a list of abstract numbers and forget that the goal is to predict a "typical" day for a practical business purpose.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at how many cupcakes the bakery sells each day, and it's usually around 40 or 50, but last Saturday they had a huge wedding order and sold 500! When I calculated the average, it jumped all the way up to 110 cupcakes, but the baker says that number feels 'wrong' for a normal Tuesday. If we want to tell a new employee what a 'typical' day looks like so they know how many to bake, should we use that 110 number or is there a better way to describe the middle?

misconceptions.title: Mean-is-Always-Best Bias
misconceptions.description: Students believe the mean is the most "mathematical" and therefore always the most accurate measure of center, regardless of outliers.

misconceptions.title: Outlier Impact Neglect
misconceptions.description: Students understand how to calculate measures but do not recognize how a single extreme value pulls the mean away from the cluster of data.

misconceptions.title: Contextual Disconnect
misconceptions.description: Students treat the data as a list of abstract numbers and forget that the goal is to predict a "typical" day for a practical business purpose.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you look at the daily sales on a dot plot, does that 500-cupcake day sit near the others or is it off on its own island?"
"Wait—if the baker actually made 110 cupcakes every single day like the average says, what would happen to the leftovers on a normal Tuesday?"
Does the 'middle' of the data move more when you change the highest number, or does it stay put if you use the median instead?
sections.generalization: I have this hunch that if a data set has one giant 'outlier' that's way bigger than everything else, the median is ALWAYS going to give you a better picture of a 'normal' day than the mean... or is there a case where the mean still wins?
sections.inference: I found the data for a different shop that sells fancy cakes, and their sales are almost always 5, 6, or 7 cakes a day, with no huge wedding orders at all. Since their data is basically a perfect, even clump without any 'islands' far away, do you think it even matters if they use the mean or the median to describe their typical day? And like, if they DID suddenly get a massive order for 100 cakes, which measure would change the most?

6.sp.b.5_final-data-report.title: The Final Data Report
6.sp.b.5_final-data-report.standardDescription: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: reporting the number of observations; describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement; giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered; relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
6.sp.b.5_final-data-report.description: Teach Zippy about The Final Data Report
6.sp.b.5_final-data-report.problemStatement:
I just finished my big science project on how many minutes of 'screen time' my classmates have each day. I surveyed 30 people (my **observations**). Most kids had between 60 and 90 minutes, but one person had 400 minutes! My teacher says I can't just give her a list of numbers; I have to write a summary that explains the 'story' of the data. 

If I use the **Mean**, that 400-minute outlier makes it look like *everyone* watches way more than they actually do. Should I use the **Median** instead? And how do I explain the 'variability' using the **IQR** so people know if we are all similar or all totally different?
6.sp.b.5_final-data-report.teachingPrompt: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: reporting the number of observations; describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement; giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered; relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
targetConcepts: Students can summarize a data set by reporting observations, units, and measurement methods.
Students can calculate and report measures of center (mean, median) and variability (IQR, MAD).
Students can justify why a specific measure (e.g., median) is more appropriate for a skewed distribution.
misconceptions: The 'One Size Fits All' Average (partial_concept_model): Students often believe the Mean is the "best" or "most mathematical" average. They struggle to recognize that the **Median** is actually a more "honest" measure of center when data is **skewed** by an outlier (like the 400-minute student).

Ignoring Units and Context (surface_feature_matching): A student might report the number "75" without saying "minutes." They often forget to describe *how* the data was collected (e.g., "self-reported via survey") or what the "attribute" is (e.g., "daily screen time").

Confusing IQR with the Range (procedural_overgeneralization): When describing variability, students often just give the Range (Max minus Min). They miss that the **Interquartile Range (IQR)** is better for describing the "typical" spread because it ignores those extreme outliers that don't represent the rest of the group.

sections.studentPrompt:
I just finished my big science project on how many minutes of 'screen time' my classmates have each day. I surveyed 30 people (my **observations**). Most kids had between 60 and 90 minutes, but one person had 400 minutes! My teacher says I can't just give her a list of numbers; I have to write a summary that explains the 'story' of the data. 

If I use the **Mean**, that 400-minute outlier makes it look like *everyone* watches way more than they actually do. Should I use the **Median** instead? And how do I explain the 'variability' using the **IQR** so people know if we are all similar or all totally different?

misconceptions.title: The 'One Size Fits All' Average
misconceptions.description: Students often believe the Mean is the "best" or "most mathematical" average. They struggle to recognize that the **Median** is actually a more "honest" measure of center when data is **skewed** by an outlier (like the 400-minute student).

misconceptions.title: Ignoring Units and Context
misconceptions.description: A student might report the number "75" without saying "minutes." They often forget to describe *how* the data was collected (e.g., "self-reported via survey") or what the "attribute" is (e.g., "daily screen time").

misconceptions.title: Confusing IQR with the Range
misconceptions.description: When describing variability, students often just give the Range (Max minus Min). They miss that the **Interquartile Range (IQR)** is better for describing the "typical" spread because it ignores those extreme outliers that don't represent the rest of the group.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you tell a parent the 'average' is 120 minutes because of one outlier, but most kids only watched 60, is your math helping them understand the class, or confusing them?"
"Wait—if the 'middle 50%' of your class (the IQR) is only 15 minutes apart, does that mean most of your friends have very similar habits?"
When you see a 'tail' on your graph stretching toward 400, does that 'pull' the Mean away from the 'hill' where everyone else is?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that the Median and IQR are ALWAYS better than the Mean and MAD when you have a 'striking deviation' (an outlier)... or is there a reason we'd still want the Mean?
sections.inference: Imagine you did the same survey at a 'No-Tech Summer Camp.' You expect 29 people to say '0 minutes' and one person to sneak in '60 minutes.' How would the **shape** of this data change? Would you still use the Median to describe it, or would a 'Mean' of 2 minutes actually be a good summary of the total screen time used by the whole camp?

6.sp.b_data-scientist_s-notebook.title: The Data Scientist's Notebook
6.sp.b_data-scientist_s-notebook.standardDescription: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by reporting the number of observations, describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, giving quantitative measures of center and variability, and relating the choice of measures to the shape of the data distribution.
6.sp.b_data-scientist_s-notebook.description: Teach Zippy about The Data Scientist's Notebook
6.sp.b_data-scientist_s-notebook.problemStatement:
I just finished surveying 20 people about how many hours of sleep they get on school nights. I have a long list of numbers, and my teacher wants me to 'summarize' them. She said I need to find the **Mean**, the **Median**, and the **Interquartile Range (IQR)**. 

I found that most people sleep 7 or 8 hours, but one person sleeps 11 hours and one person sleeps only 4. My friend says the 'Mean' is the best way to show the average, but I think that 11-hour sleeper is pulling the average up too high. How do I decide which number (Mean or Median) actually tells the 'true story' of my class's sleep habits?
6.sp.b_data-scientist_s-notebook.teachingPrompt: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by reporting the number of observations, describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, giving quantitative measures of center and variability, and relating the choice of measures to the shape of the data distribution.
targetConcepts: Students can display numerical data in plots on a number line (dot plots, histograms, box plots).
Students can calculate quantitative measures of center (median, mean) and variability (IQR, MAD).
Students can choose the most appropriate measure of center based on the shape of the distribution (skewness vs. symmetry).
misconceptions: Mean vs. Median Sensitivity (partial_concept_model): Students often believe the Mean is always the "correct" average. They fail to realize that the Mean is highly sensitive to **outliers** (like the 4 or 11 hours), while the Median stays more stable in the "middle" of the actual data.

Computational Fatigue (procedural_overgeneralization): When calculating the Median, students often forget to **order the data from least to greatest** first. They simply pick the middle number from their original random list, leading to a meaningless result.

IQR as a Single Number (surface_feature_matching): Students may think the Interquartile Range is two separate numbers ($Q1$ and $Q3$). They need to understand that $IQR = Q3 - Q1$, representing the "width" of the middle 50% of the data as a single value of variability.

sections.studentPrompt:
I just finished surveying 20 people about how many hours of sleep they get on school nights. I have a long list of numbers, and my teacher wants me to 'summarize' them. She said I need to find the **Mean**, the **Median**, and the **Interquartile Range (IQR)**. 

I found that most people sleep 7 or 8 hours, but one person sleeps 11 hours and one person sleeps only 4. My friend says the 'Mean' is the best way to show the average, but I think that 11-hour sleeper is pulling the average up too high. How do I decide which number (Mean or Median) actually tells the 'true story' of my class's sleep habits?

misconceptions.title: Mean vs. Median Sensitivity
misconceptions.description: Students often believe the Mean is always the "correct" average. They fail to realize that the Mean is highly sensitive to **outliers** (like the 4 or 11 hours), while the Median stays more stable in the "middle" of the actual data.

misconceptions.title: Computational Fatigue
misconceptions.description: When calculating the Median, students often forget to **order the data from least to greatest** first. They simply pick the middle number from their original random list, leading to a meaningless result.

misconceptions.title: IQR as a Single Number
misconceptions.description: Students may think the Interquartile Range is two separate numbers ($Q1$ and $Q3$). They need to understand that $IQR = Q3 - Q1$, representing the "width" of the middle 50% of the data as a single value of variability.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If we added a giant who sleeps 24 hours a day to your survey, which would change more: the Mean (the total divided by everyone) or the Median (the person standing in the middle of the line)?"
"Wait—if you look at a Box Plot, is the 'box' showing you where the most 'typical' sleepers are, or is it showing you the extreme sleepers?"
Does the 'Mean Absolute Deviation' (MAD) feel like a 'distance'? Does it tell you how far, on average, each student is from the 'perfect' center?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if a data set is perfectly **symmetrical** (like a perfect hill), the Mean and the Median will ALWAYS be the exact same number... but if the data is **skewed**, will they always be different?
sections.inference:
Imagine two classes. 
* **Class A** has an IQR of 0.5 hours. 
* **Class B** has an IQR of 4 hours. 

In which class are the students' sleep habits more 'predictable'? If you picked a random student from Class B, would you be more or less surprised by their answer than if you picked a student from Class A?

7.ee.a_discount-dilemma.title: The Discount Dilemma
7.ee.a_discount-dilemma.standardDescription: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients. Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
7.ee.a_discount-dilemma.description: Teach Zippy about The Discount Dilemma
7.ee.a_discount-dilemma.teachingPrompt: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients. Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
targetConcepts: The student can apply properties of operations to add, subtract, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients.
The student can interpret how different forms of an expression represent the same relationship between quantities in a context.
misconceptions: Additive vs. Multiplicative Confusion (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student tries to subtract the 20% as a flat 20 cents ($x - 0.20$) rather than 20% of the variable $x$.
Term Detachment (structural_misunderstanding): The student treats the $0.20x$ as a separate entity and fails to realize that $x$ has an implicit coefficient of 1.
Operational Misinterpretation (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student believes that $0.80x$ represents an 80% discount rather than the remaining 80% of the original cost.

sections.studentPrompt: 20% off everything

misconceptions.title: Additive vs. Multiplicative Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student tries to subtract the 20% as a flat 20 cents ($x - 0.20$) rather than 20% of the variable $x$.

misconceptions.title: Term Detachment
misconceptions.description: The student treats the $0.20x$ as a separate entity and fails to realize that $x$ has an implicit coefficient of 1.

misconceptions.title: Operational Misinterpretation
misconceptions.description: The student believes that $0.80x$ represents an 80% discount rather than the remaining 80% of the original cost.

sections.patternRecognition:
If the skateboard was exactly $100, what happens to the numbers in my way of doing it versus my friend's way?
Does that "0.20" part always have to be attached to the $x$, or can it just hang out by itself?
Why does my friend's version only have one term while mine has two?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if you're taking a percentage off, is it ALWAYS the same as just multiplying by whatever percentage is left over, or does that only work for certain numbers?
sections.inference: Buy 3 skateboards, get 10% off the total.

7.ee.b_phone-repair-fund.title: The Phone Repair Fund
7.ee.b_phone-repair-fund.standardDescription: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies. Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
7.ee.b_phone-repair-fund.description: Teach Zippy about The Phone Repair Fund
7.ee.b_phone-repair-fund.problemStatement: I’m trying to save up exactly $150 to fix my cracked phone screen. I already have $45.50 saved, and I just started a job washing cars where I make $12.25 per car. If I want to have enough money by next Friday, how many cars do I actually need to wash to hit my goal?
7.ee.b_phone-repair-fund.teachingPrompt: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies. Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
targetConcepts: The student can construct and solve multi-step equations and inequalities involving rational numbers to represent real-world constraints.
The student can assess the reasonableness of a solution by converting between fractions and decimals and applying estimation.
misconceptions: Operation Reversal (procedural_overgeneralization): The student adds the existing savings to the goal ($150 + 45.50$) before dividing by the rate, rather than subtracting it to find the remaining balance needed.

Remainder Neglection (partial_concept_model): The student calculates a non-whole number of cars (e.g., 8.53) and rounds down to 8, forgetting that washing 8 cars leaves them short of the $150 requirement.
Whole Number Bias (whole_number_bias): The student ignores the $0.50 and $0.25 decimals to make the division "cleaner," resulting in an estimate that doesn't satisfy the actual constraint.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m trying to save up exactly $150 to fix my cracked phone screen. I already have $45.50 saved, and I just started a job washing cars where I make $12.25 per car. If I want to have enough money by next Friday, how many cars do I actually need to wash to hit my goal?

misconceptions.title: Operation Reversal
misconceptions.description: The student adds the existing savings to the goal ($150 + 45.50$) before dividing by the rate, rather than subtracting it to find the remaining balance needed.

misconceptions.title: Remainder Neglection
misconceptions.description: The student calculates a non-whole number of cars (e.g., 8.53) and rounds down to 8, forgetting that washing 8 cars leaves them short of the $150 requirement.

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Bias
misconceptions.description: The student ignores the $0.50 and $0.25 decimals to make the division "cleaner," resulting in an estimate that doesn't satisfy the actual constraint.

sections.patternRecognition:
If you wash just one car, does that $45.50 you already have change at all?
Every time you finish a car, how much closer does that $150 goal get?
Is there a way to figure out the total money you have without counting the cars one by one?
sections.generalization: Wait, if I have to wash a 'part' of a car to reach exactly $150, does that mean I ALWAYS have to round up to the next whole car to actually have enough money?
sections.inference: units

7.g.a.2_spaghetti-bridge.title: The Spaghetti Bridge
7.g.a.2_spaghetti-bridge.standardDescription: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
7.g.a.2_spaghetti-bridge.description: Teach Zippy about The Spaghetti Bridge
7.g.a.2_spaghetti-bridge.problemStatement: I'm trying to build this triangle support for a bridge using three pieces of dry spaghetti, but no matter how I move them around, the ends won't touch to close the shape. One piece is 10 inches long, and the other two are only 4 inches and 5 inches—do you think it's even possible to make them form a triangle without breaking them?
7.g.a.2_spaghetti-bridge.teachingPrompt: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
targetConcepts: Students can determine the necessary relationship between side lengths (Triangle Inequality Theorem) required to form a closed geometric figure.
Students can predict whether a unique triangle, multiple triangles, or no triangle can be formed based on given side constraints.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm trying to build this triangle support for a bridge using three pieces of dry spaghetti, but no matter how I move them around, the ends won't touch to close the shape. One piece is 10 inches long, and the other two are only 4 inches and 5 inches—do you think it's even possible to make them form a triangle without breaking them?
sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait, if we keep the 10-inch piece but swap the others for two 6-inch pieces, does it suddenly stop being impossible?"
"Does it matter which piece we put on the bottom, or does that 'gap' stay exactly the same size?"
Is there a specific total length the two short pieces have to hit before they finally start to reach each other?
sections.generalization: I was thinking... if you have two short sticks that add up to exactly the same length as the long stick, they'll always be able to bridge the gap and make a triangle, right?
sections.inference: So I tried it again with new pieces: one is 12cm and another is 7cm. When I used a 3cm piece for the last side, it failed, but when I used an 8cm piece, it worked easily. Is there a way to know the shortest and longest possible lengths that third piece could be so I don't keep picking duds?

7.g.b.6_glass-aquarium-gap.title: The Glass Aquarium Gap
7.g.b.6_glass-aquarium-gap.standardDescription: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms.
7.g.b.6_glass-aquarium-gap.description: Teach Zippy about The Glass Aquarium Gap
7.g.b.6_glass-aquarium-gap.teachingPrompt: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms.
targetConcepts: The student can distinguish between the 2D composite area of the faces (surface area) and the 3D capacity (volume) within a constraint-based context.
The student can explain how changing a single dimension affects the total surface area non-proportionally when one or more faces (the base) remain constant.
misconceptions: Volume vs. Surface Area Confusion (structural_misunderstanding): The student assumes that the numerical value for the space inside (volume) should be identical to the material needed for the walls (surface area).

Neglecting the Open Top (surface_feature_matching): The student uses the standard formula for a closed prism ($SA = 2lw + 2lh + 2wh$) and includes a sixth face that doesn't exist in a "tank" scenario.

Linear Scaling Fallacy (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student believes that doubling one dimension (height) results in a proportional doubling of the total surface area, failing to account for the base staying constant.

sections.studentPrompt: amount of space inside

misconceptions.description: The student uses the standard formula for a closed prism ($SA = 2lw + 2lh + 2wh$) and includes a sixth face that doesn't exist in a "tank" scenario.

sections.patternRecognition:
Wait, if we keep making the tank taller and taller, which glass panels are actually getting bigger and which ones stay the exact same?
I noticed the bottom of the tank is 2 square feet whether it’s short or tall, so does that bottom piece even care how much water we put in?
If we look at the four side walls, is there a reason they seem to grow together in a specific way when the height changes?
sections.generalization: So I was thinking... if you have two different boxes and they both hold the exact same amount of water inside, does that mean they will ALWAYS take the exact same amount of glass to build?
sections.inference: Long Tank

7.ns.a.1.a_zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.title: The Zero-Sum Scavenger Hunt
7.ns.a.1.a_zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.standardDescription: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0. For example, a hydrogen atom has 0 charge because its two constituents are oppositely charged.
7.ns.a.1.a_zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.description: Teach Zippy about The Zero-Sum Scavenger Hunt
7.ns.a.1.a_zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.problemStatement: We’re playing this school-wide scavenger hunt where you get points for finding hidden items but lose points for taking too long, and my team’s digital tracker just flashed a big 'ZERO' for our current score. Does that mean we haven't found anything at all, or is there a way we could have found a bunch of stuff and still ended up with absolutely nothing on the screen?
7.ns.a.1.a_zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.teachingPrompt: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0. For example, a hydrogen atom has 0 charge because its two constituents are oppositely charged.
targetConcepts: Students can identify and describe real-world scenarios where opposite forces or quantities result in a net value of zero.
Students can explain the relationship between a rational number and its additive inverse in a situational context.
misconceptions: Null Set Confusion (surface_feature_matching): [The student might assume that a total of zero can only exist if no actions have taken place (the 'starting at zero' state).]

Magnitude Overestimation (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): [A student may believe that if you perform a 'big' action, like finding a 50-point item, the score can never return to zero because 50 is such a large number.]
Directional Misunderstanding (partial_concept_model): [A student might think that 'losing points' just stops your score from growing rather than actively moving the value back toward or past zero.]

sections.studentPrompt: We’re playing this school-wide scavenger hunt where you get points for finding hidden items but lose points for taking too long, and my team’s digital tracker just flashed a big 'ZERO' for our current score. Does that mean we haven't found anything at all, or is there a way we could have found a bunch of stuff and still ended up with absolutely nothing on the screen?

misconceptions.title: Null Set Confusion
misconceptions.description: [The student might assume that a total of zero can only exist if no actions have taken place (the 'starting at zero' state).]

misconceptions.title: Magnitude Overestimation
misconceptions.description: [A student may believe that if you perform a 'big' action, like finding a 50-point item, the score can never return to zero because 50 is such a large number.]

misconceptions.title: Directional Misunderstanding
misconceptions.description: [A student might think that 'losing points' just stops your score from growing rather than actively moving the value back toward or past zero.]

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you found a hidden gold star worth 15 points, what would have to happen with the 'time penalty' to make that 15 disappear instantly?"
"Is there a specific 'match' for every positive point that would drag the score back to the exact middle?"
Does it matter if you lose the points first and then find the item, or do they always cancel each other out the same way?
sections.generalization: If I have a massive pile of 'positive' points, is it ALWAYS possible to find a 'negative' amount that lands me exactly back at zero, or are there some numbers that just don't have a perfect opposite?
sections.inference: So I was reading about these deep-sea divers, and they said their 'net elevation' was zero because they started at the surface, dove way down to a ship wreck, and then came all the way back up to the boat. If they dove down exactly 42.5 meters to find a treasure chest, what would their very next move have to be to make their total change in position zero again?

7.ns.a.1.b_hiking-trail-tracker.title: The Hiking Trail Tracker
7.ns.a.1.b_hiking-trail-tracker.standardDescription: Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
7.ns.a.1.b_hiking-trail-tracker.description: Teach Zippy about The Hiking Trail Tracker
7.ns.a.1.b_hiking-trail-tracker.problemStatement: I was looking at this trail map that starts at an overlook 12.5 feet above the river, but then the first 'jump' on the guide says to move -12.5 feet to get to the base of the waterfall. If the guide says my next move after that is to go another -20 feet into a canyon, where am I actually standing compared to that original river level?
7.ns.a.1.b_hiking-trail-tracker.teachingPrompt: Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
targetConcepts: Students can demonstrate that $p + q$ represents a final position starting at $p$ and moving a distance $|q|$ in a direction determined by the sign of $q$.
Students can identify that $p + (-p) = 0$, confirming the property of additive inverses within a physical context.
misconceptions: Directional Confusion (structural_misunderstanding): A student might see the -12.5 and the 12.5 and think they should add them to get 25, not realizing that the negative sign dictates a specific direction back toward zero.
Distance vs. Position (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): A student might believe you can't go "below" zero in a real-world setting, assuming the -20 feet just means you stopped at the ground.

Additive Inverse Oversight (partial_concept_model): A student might fail to recognize that 12.5 and -12.5 perfectly "cancel" out, trying instead to perform complex subtraction that leads to a non-zero starting point for the next jump.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at this trail map that starts at an overlook 12.5 feet above the river, but then the first 'jump' on the guide says to move -12.5 feet to get to the base of the waterfall. If the guide says my next move after that is to go another -20 feet into a canyon, where am I actually standing compared to that original river level?

misconceptions.title: Directional Confusion
misconceptions.description: A student might see the -12.5 and the 12.5 and think they should add them to get 25, not realizing that the negative sign dictates a specific direction back toward zero.

misconceptions.title: Distance vs. Position
misconceptions.description: A student might believe you can't go "below" zero in a real-world setting, assuming the -20 feet just means you stopped at the ground.

misconceptions.title: Additive Inverse Oversight
misconceptions.description: A student might fail to recognize that 12.5 and -12.5 perfectly "cancel" out, trying instead to perform complex subtraction that leads to a non-zero starting point for the next jump.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you're at 12.5 and you move -12.5, does that 'negative' jump pull you closer to the river or push you further away into the sky?"
"Is there a specific 'zero' spot where the height of the overlook and the depth of the first jump perfectly match up?"
If you start at 0 and move -20, does the 'size' of the move change just because you started at the river instead of the overlook?
sections.generalization: If you start at any height 'p' and move an amount 'q', is your new spot ALWAYS just the distance of 'q' away from 'p', or does the direction of the jump change how far you actually traveled?
sections.inference: So I found this OTHER trail that starts at a cliff 50.25 feet up, and the first instruction is a jump of -50.25 feet followed by a jump of 15 feet. If we use that same 'p + q' logic, do you think we could predict exactly where we'd be if the last jump was -100 feet without having to walk the whole trail first?

7.ns.a.3_snack-stand-tab.title: The Snack Stand Tab
7.ns.a.3_snack-stand-tab.standardDescription: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.
7.ns.a.3_snack-stand-tab.description: Teach Zippy about The Snack Stand Tab
7.ns.a.3_snack-stand-tab.problemStatement: My friends and I have this running tab at the snack stand where we can pay later, but the guy forgot to write down the total. I know I started with a balance of –$12.50 because I owed for last week, then I bought three Gatorades for $2.25 each, and then my cousin gave me a $20 bill to help pay off whatever I owe. After all that, did I actually end up with money left over in my account, or do I still owe that guy?
7.ns.a.3_snack-stand-tab.teachingPrompt: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.
targetConcepts: The student can fluently combine multiple operations (multiplication and addition/subtraction) using both positive and negative rational numbers.
The student can represent a real-world financial "net state" by correctly assigning signs to debts (negative) and payments (positive).
misconceptions: Sign Ignoring (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): The student treats the starting –$12.50 as a positive balance and adds all values together, failing to recognize that debt and payments move in opposite directions.

Operation Order Confusion (structural_misunderstanding): The student subtracts the total cost of the drinks from the $20 before addressing the initial debt, potentially losing track of the negative starting point.

Whole Number Bias (whole_number_bias): The student may struggle with the precision of multiplying $2.25 by 3 or adding decimals, treating the numbers as whole integers and misplacing the decimal point in the final sum.

sections.studentPrompt: My friends and I have this running tab at the snack stand where we can pay later, but the guy forgot to write down the total. I know I started with a balance of –$12.50 because I owed for last week, then I bought three Gatorades for $2.25 each, and then my cousin gave me a $20 bill to help pay off whatever I owe. After all that, did I actually end up with money left over in my account, or do I still owe that guy?

misconceptions.title: Sign Ignoring
misconceptions.description: The student treats the starting –$12.50 as a positive balance and adds all values together, failing to recognize that debt and payments move in opposite directions.

misconceptions.title: Operation Order Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student subtracts the total cost of the drinks from the $20 before addressing the initial debt, potentially losing track of the negative starting point.

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Bias
misconceptions.description: The student may struggle with the precision of multiplying $2.25 by 3 or adding decimals, treating the numbers as whole integers and misplacing the decimal point in the final sum.

sections.patternRecognition:
Wait—if you keep buying more Gatorades, does that starting debt number get further from zero or closer to it?
Does the cost of one single Gatorade ever change no matter how much you already owe the stand?
How does that $20 gift change the "vibe" of the tab compared to the $2.25 purchases?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if you have a negative balance and you add a positive payment that's 'bigger' than the debt, is the answer always going to jump over to a positive number, or could it somehow stay negative?
sections.inference: Okay, so my sister has a tab at the same stand and her situation is way weirder. She started at –$15.00, bought 5 bags of chips that cost $1.20 each, and then she handed the guy some money, but I didn't see how much. Now the guy says her balance is exactly $0.00. I’m trying to figure out how much she must have handed him, and like, if there's a trick to figure that out for any number of chips she buys?

7.rp.a.1_the-marathon-mechanic.title: The Marathon Mechanic
7.rp.a.1_the-marathon-mechanic.standardDescription: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas and other quantities measured in like or different units. For example, if a person walks 1/2 mile in each 1/4 hour, compute the unit rate as the complex fraction 1/2 / 1/4 miles per hour, equivalently 2 miles per hour.
7.rp.a.1_the-marathon-mechanic.description: Teach Zippy about The Marathon Mechanic
7.rp.a.1_the-marathon-mechanic.problemStatement:
I'm training for a mini-marathon and I want to know my exact pace. Yesterday, I ran $\frac{3}{4}$ of a mile in exactly $\frac{1}{6}$ of an hour. My coach wants me to find my **unit rate** in miles per hour ($mph$). 

I tried to solve it by multiplying $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{6}$, but that gave me $\frac{3}{24}$, which is only $\frac{1}{8}$ of a mile per hour. That sounds way too slow—I’m definitely faster than a snail! Did I flip the wrong fraction, or am I using the wrong operation entirely to find a 'unit' rate?
7.rp.a.1_the-marathon-mechanic.teachingPrompt: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas and other quantities measured in like or different units. For example, if a person walks 1/2 mile in each 1/4 hour, compute the unit rate as the complex fraction 1/2 / 1/4 miles per hour, equivalently 2 miles per hour.
targetConcepts: Students can compute unit rates involving complex fractions.
Students can identify the correct relationship between distance and time to express a rate.
Students can simplify complex fractions to find an equivalent whole number or simple fraction rate.
misconceptions: Operation Confusion (procedural_overgeneralization): Students often multiply the two numbers in a ratio instead of dividing. They need to recognize that a "rate" is a division problem: $\text{Quantity } A \div \text{Quantity } B$.

The "Keep-Change-Flip" Fail (partial_concept_model): When dividing fractions ($\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{6}$), students might "flip" the first fraction instead of the divisor, or forget to change the operation to multiplication, leading to a rate of $\frac{1}{8}$ or $\frac{4}{18}$.

Numerator/Denominator Logic (surface_feature_matching): In the phrase "miles per hour," the word **per** acts as the division bar. Students often struggle to identify that "miles" must be the numerator and "hours" must be the denominator.

sections.studentPrompt:
I'm training for a mini-marathon and I want to know my exact pace. Yesterday, I ran $\frac{3}{4}$ of a mile in exactly $\frac{1}{6}$ of an hour. My coach wants me to find my **unit rate** in miles per hour ($mph$). 

I tried to solve it by multiplying $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{6}$, but that gave me $\frac{3}{24}$, which is only $\frac{1}{8}$ of a mile per hour. That sounds way too slow—I’m definitely faster than a snail! Did I flip the wrong fraction, or am I using the wrong operation entirely to find a 'unit' rate?

misconceptions.title: Operation Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students often multiply the two numbers in a ratio instead of dividing. They need to recognize that a "rate" is a division problem: $\text{Quantity } A \div \text{Quantity } B$.

misconceptions.title: The "Keep-Change-Flip" Fail
misconceptions.description: When dividing fractions ($\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{6}$), students might "flip" the first fraction instead of the divisor, or forget to change the operation to multiplication, leading to a rate of $\frac{1}{8}$ or $\frac{4}{18}$.

misconceptions.title: Numerator/Denominator Logic
misconceptions.description: In the phrase "miles per hour," the word **per** acts as the division bar. Students often struggle to identify that "miles" must be the numerator and "hours" must be the denominator.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you run $\frac{3}{4}$ of a mile in a tiny slice of an hour ($\frac{1}{6}$), should your speed for a *whole* hour be much larger or much smaller than $\frac{3}{4}$?"
"Wait—if there are six '$\frac{1}{6}$ hour' chunks in a full hour, shouldn't you be able to run your $\frac{3}{4}$ mile distance six times over?"
When we say 'Unit Rate,' we are asking: 'How much for **one** full unit?' How do you turn $\frac{1}{6}$ into a $1$?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if the denominator of your rate is a **proper fraction** (less than 1), the unit rate will ALWAYS be larger than the original numerator... is that true, or can it sometimes get smaller?
sections.inference:
A different runner covers $\frac{1}{2}$ a mile in $\frac{1}{10}$ of an hour. 
* Who is faster: you or the new runner? 
* If you both keep these exact paces for $\frac{1}{2}$ of a day, will the 'gap' between you get bigger or stay the same?

7.rp.a_coffee-shop-chemist.title: The Coffee Shop Chemist
7.rp.a_coffee-shop-chemist.standardDescription: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems, including calculating unit rates with fractions, identifying proportional relationships in tables and graphs, and representing them with equations.
7.rp.a_coffee-shop-chemist.description: Teach Zippy about The Coffee Shop Chemist
7.rp.a_coffee-shop-chemist.problemStatement:
I'm working at a local coffee shop, and we have a 'Secret Sauce' for our lattes. The recipe says to mix $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of cocoa for every $\frac{3}{4}$ cup of hot water. My manager wants to know the **unit rate** (how much cocoa per 1 full cup of water). 

Also, we have a table showing the cost of different sized lattes: 8oz for \$3.20, 12oz for \$4.80, and 16oz for \$6.40. Is the price **proportional** to the size? If I draw a graph of this, will it go through the $(0,0)$ point, and how do I write an equation ($y = kx$) so I can tell a customer the price of a 20oz latte instantly?
7.rp.a_coffee-shop-chemist.teachingPrompt: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems, including calculating unit rates with fractions, identifying proportional relationships in tables and graphs, and representing them with equations.
targetConcepts: Students can compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions.
Students can identify proportional relationships in tables, graphs, and verbal descriptions.
Students can represent proportional relationships by equations of the form $y = kx$.
misconceptions: The Fraction Flip (procedural_overgeneralization): When calculating unit rates with fractions (e.g., $\frac{1}{2} \div \frac{3}{4}$), students often forget to multiply by the reciprocal or they flip the wrong fraction. They might calculate $\frac{3}{4}$ cups of cocoa per water instead of $\frac{2}{3}$.

The "Origin" Oversight (partial_concept_model): Students often identify a "straight line" on a graph and assume it's proportional, forgetting that a proportional relationship **must** pass through the origin $(0,0)$. For example, a phone plan that costs \$20 plus \$0.10 per minute is a straight line, but it is *not* proportional.

Constant of Proportionality ($k$) Confusion (surface_feature_matching): A student might calculate $x/y$ instead of $y/x$ to find $k$. In the latte example, they might say the constant is 2.5 (ounces per dollar) instead of 0.4 (dollars per ounce), making their equation $y = 2.5x$ predict a 20oz latte costs \$50!

sections.studentPrompt:
I'm working at a local coffee shop, and we have a 'Secret Sauce' for our lattes. The recipe says to mix $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of cocoa for every $\frac{3}{4}$ cup of hot water. My manager wants to know the **unit rate** (how much cocoa per 1 full cup of water). 

Also, we have a table showing the cost of different sized lattes: 8oz for \$3.20, 12oz for \$4.80, and 16oz for \$6.40. Is the price **proportional** to the size? If I draw a graph of this, will it go through the $(0,0)$ point, and how do I write an equation ($y = kx$) so I can tell a customer the price of a 20oz latte instantly?

misconceptions.title: The Fraction Flip
misconceptions.description: When calculating unit rates with fractions (e.g., $\frac{1}{2} \div \frac{3}{4}$), students often forget to multiply by the reciprocal or they flip the wrong fraction. They might calculate $\frac{3}{4}$ cups of cocoa per water instead of $\frac{2}{3}$.

misconceptions.title: The "Origin" Oversight
misconceptions.description: Students often identify a "straight line" on a graph and assume it's proportional, forgetting that a proportional relationship **must** pass through the origin $(0,0)$. For example, a phone plan that costs \$20 plus \$0.10 per minute is a straight line, but it is *not* proportional.

misconceptions.title: Constant of Proportionality ($k$) Confusion
misconceptions.description: A student might calculate $x/y$ instead of $y/x$ to find $k$. In the latte example, they might say the constant is 2.5 (ounces per dollar) instead of 0.4 (dollars per ounce), making their equation $y = 2.5x$ predict a 20oz latte costs \$50!

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you buy **zero** ounces of coffee, should you pay **zero** dollars? If the answer is yes, what does that tell you about your graph's starting point?"
"Wait—if you divide the Price by the Ounces for every row in your table ($3.20 \div 8$, $4.80 \div 12$), do you get the exact same 'magic number' every time?"
In the recipe, if you wanted 1 cup of water, would you need more or less than $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of cocoa? Does your unit rate reflect that?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if a relationship is proportional, the graph will ALWAYS be a straight line... but is every straight line ALWAYS a proportional relationship?
sections.inference:
We found the equation for latte prices is $y = 0.4x$, where $y$ is the cost and $x$ is the ounces. 
* What does the point $(1, 0.4)$ represent in the 'real world' of the coffee shop? 
* If the shop adds a \$0.50 'service fee' to every order, is the new relationship ($y = 0.4x + 0.5$) still proportional? How would the graph change?

7.sp.a.1_cafeteria-survey-snag.title: The Cafeteria Survey Snag
7.sp.a.1_cafeteria-survey-snag.standardDescription: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
7.sp.a.1_cafeteria-survey-snag.description: Teach Zippy about The Cafeteria Survey Snag
7.sp.a.1_cafeteria-survey-snag.teachingPrompt: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
targetConcepts: The student can distinguish between a representative sample and a biased sample based on the method of selection.
The student understands that a valid inference about a population requires a sampling method where every member has an equal chance of being selected.
misconceptions: Size Over Structure (surface_feature_matching): The student believes that any group of 20 students is "enough" to represent 600, regardless of how those 20 were chosen.

Convenience Bias Blindness (structural_misunderstanding): The student fails to recognize that a physical location (the pizza line) creates a pre-filtered group of people who already prefer that option.

Homogeneous Population Assumption (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): The student assumes all members of a population are identical, so any small "piece" of the population must look exactly like the "whole."
