misconceptions.title: Convenience Bias Blindness
misconceptions.description: The student fails to recognize that a physical location (the pizza line) creates a pre-filtered group of people who already prefer that option.

misconceptions.title: Homogeneous Population Assumption
misconceptions.description: The student assumes all members of a population are identical, so any small "piece" of the population must look exactly like the "whole."

sections.patternRecognition:
If we moved the survey to the taco line instead, do you think the "winner" would stay the same or flip-flop?
I noticed you picked the first 20 people you saw, but did every student in the school actually have a fair shot at being picked by you?
What would happen to our results if we only asked the teachers instead of the students?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I pick names out of a hat where every single student's name is inside, is that ALWAYS going to give me a more honest picture than just asking my friends at my lunch table?
sections.inference: random

7.sp.b.3_great-jump-off.title: The Great Jump-Off
7.sp.b.3_great-jump-off.standardDescription: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability. For example, the mean height of players on the basketball team is 10 cm greater than the mean height of players on the soccer team, about twice the variability (mean absolute deviation) on either team; on a dot plot, the separation between the two distributions of heights is noticeable.
7.sp.b.3_great-jump-off.description: Teach Zippy about The Great Jump-Off
7.sp.b.3_great-jump-off.teachingPrompt: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability. For example, the mean height of players on the basketball team is 10 cm greater than the mean height of players on the soccer team, about twice the variability (mean absolute deviation) on either team; on a dot plot, the separation between the two distributions of heights is noticeable.
targetConcepts: The student can informally assess the degree of visual overlap between two numerical data distributions. [cite: 44]
The student can express the difference between the centers of two distributions as a multiple of the measure of variability. [cite: 43]

sections.studentPrompt: better
sections.patternRecognition:
If we look at where most of the players on each team landed, do those "clumps" of jumps sit right on top of each other or is there a gap?
I noticed both teams have a "spread" of about 10 inches between their best and worst jumps, so why does the 5-inch difference in their averages feel so small?
If the basketball team became way more consistent and everyone jumped the exact same distance, would it be easier to tell them apart from the soccer team?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if the distance between the two team averages is smaller than the typical 'spread' of the players on just one team, does that mean the two groups are SOMETIMES basically doing the same thing?
sections.inference: spread

7.sp.c.7_the-wonky-spinner.title: The Wonky Spinner
7.sp.c.7_the-wonky-spinner.standardDescription: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
7.sp.c.7_the-wonky-spinner.description: Teach Zippy about The Wonky Spinner
7.sp.c.7_the-wonky-spinner.problemStatement: My cousin made this spinner with four equal-sized colors—red, blue, green, and yellow—but when we spun it 40 times, it landed on red 18 times and only hit blue twice. If the spaces are all the same size, why didn't we just get 10 of each color?
7.sp.c.7_the-wonky-spinner.teachingPrompt: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
targetConcepts: The student can distinguish between a theoretical probability model (expected outcomes) and experimental probability (observed frequencies).
The student can identify that increased sample size typically reduces the relative discrepancy between observed and expected values.
misconceptions: Equally Likely Bias (surface_feature_matching): The student assumes that because the physical areas look equal, the experimental results must match the theoretical probability exactly even in small samples.
Law of Small Numbers (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): The student believes that a small number of trials (like 40) should perfectly reflect the long-run average of the probability model.

Sample Variability Denial (structural_misunderstanding): The student attributes any deviation from the expected 1/4 frequency to a "broken" tool or "cheating" rather than understanding natural statistical fluctuation.

sections.studentPrompt: My cousin made this spinner with four equal-sized colors—red, blue, green, and yellow—but when we spun it 40 times, it landed on red 18 times and only hit blue twice. If the spaces are all the same size, why didn't we just get 10 of each color?

misconceptions.title: Equally Likely Bias
misconceptions.description: The student assumes that because the physical areas look equal, the experimental results must match the theoretical probability exactly even in small samples.

misconceptions.title: Law of Small Numbers
misconceptions.description: The student believes that a small number of trials (like 40) should perfectly reflect the long-run average of the probability model.

misconceptions.title: Sample Variability Denial
misconceptions.description: The student attributes any deviation from the expected 1/4 frequency to a "broken" tool or "cheating" rather than understanding natural statistical fluctuation.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we kept spinning this thing all day, do you think that "18 out of 40" for red would stay that high, or would it start to settle down?
Wait—is the 1/4 chance we expected for blue ever actually going to change, even if the spinner is acting weird right now?
How far away does a result have to get from 10 before we stop saying it's "just luck" and start thinking the spinner is actually tilted?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if a spinner is perfectly fair, does that mean the more times we spin it, the results will ALWAYS end up getting closer and closer to the exact percentages we expected at the start?
sections.inference: fairer

7.sp.c.8_the-double-drip-cafe.title: The Double-Drip Cafe
7.sp.c.8_the-double-drip-cafe.standardDescription: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
7.sp.c.8_the-double-drip-cafe.description: Teach Zippy about The Double-Drip Cafe
7.sp.c.8_the-double-drip-cafe.problemStatement: The cafe down the street gives out a mystery sticker with every drink, but they only have two designs—a sun and a moon—and they just grab them out of a box without looking. [cite_start]If I buy two drinks today, is it a 50/50 shot that I'll end up with one of each sticker, or is there some other way this could go? [cite: 31, 47, 93]
7.sp.c.8_the-double-drip-cafe.teachingPrompt: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
targetConcepts: The student can identify and represent all possible outcomes in a compound event sample space. [cite: 17, 102]
The student can determine the probability of a specific compound event by comparing favorable outcomes to the total sample space. [cite: 31, 93]
misconceptions: Sample Space Omission (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student forgets to account for different sequences, like getting a sun then a moon versus getting a moon then a sun. [cite: 17, 102]

sections.studentPrompt: The cafe down the street gives out a mystery sticker with every drink, but they only have two designs—a sun and a moon—and they just grab them out of a box without looking. [cite_start]If I buy two drinks today, is it a 50/50 shot that I'll end up with one of each sticker, or is there some other way this could go? [cite: 31, 47, 93]

misconceptions.title: Sample Space Omission
misconceptions.description: The student forgets to account for different sequences, like getting a sun then a moon versus getting a moon then a sun. [cite: 17, 102]

sections.patternRecognition:
If you kept track of every single pair of stickers people walked out with, which specific combinations would you see changing from person to person? [cite: 17, 47]
Does the fact that there are always exactly two stickers in a "set" change how many total ways the morning could go? [cite: 17, 93]
How do the "Sun-Moon" pairs and the "Moon-Sun" pairs connect when we're trying to figure out the total number of ways to get stickers? [cite: 17, 102]
sections.generalization: Wait, so if there are two options for the first thing and two options for the second thing, does that mean the total number of combinations is ALWAYS just going to be those two numbers added together... or is there some other rule that happens when we combine them?
sections.inference: one of each

8.ee.a.1_the-doubling-dilemma.title: The Doubling Dilemma
8.ee.a.1_the-doubling-dilemma.standardDescription: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
8.ee.a.1_the-doubling-dilemma.description: Teach Zippy about The Doubling Dilemma
8.ee.a.1_the-doubling-dilemma.problemStatement: I was looking at this pattern where a cell doubles every hour, so after 3 hours there are $2^3$ cells, and I wondered what happens if you multiply that by another $2^2$ cells. Does that mean you have $4^5$ cells now because you doubled the bottom and added the top, or is that not how it works?
8.ee.a.1_the-doubling-dilemma.teachingPrompt: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
targetConcepts: The student can apply the Product of Powers property ($a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}$) to generate equivalent expressions.
The student can justify why the base remains constant when multiplying powers with like bases.
misconceptions: Base Multiplier Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student multiplies the bases together (2 times 2) while also adding the exponents, resulting in $4^5$ instead of $2^5$.
Exponent Multiplication (procedural_overgeneralization): The student multiplies the exponents (3 times 2) instead of adding them, leading to an answer of $2^6$.
Linear Addition Bias (whole_number_bias): The student treats the entire expression as a simple addition problem ($2^3 + 2^2$) rather than recognizing the multiplicative nature of exponential growth.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at this pattern where a cell doubles every hour, so after 3 hours there are $2^3$ cells, and I wondered what happens if you multiply that by another $2^2$ cells. Does that mean you have $4^5$ cells now because you doubled the bottom and added the top, or is that not how it works?

misconceptions.title: Base Multiplier Error
misconceptions.description: The student multiplies the bases together (2 times 2) while also adding the exponents, resulting in $4^5$ instead of $2^5$.

misconceptions.title: Exponent Multiplication
misconceptions.description: The student multiplies the exponents (3 times 2) instead of adding them, leading to an answer of $2^6$.

misconceptions.title: Linear Addition Bias
misconceptions.description: The student treats the entire expression as a simple addition problem ($2^3 + 2^2$) rather than recognizing the multiplicative nature of exponential growth.

sections.patternRecognition:
If you wrote out all the "2s" being multiplied in $2^3$ and then wrote out all the "2s" in $2^2$ right next to them, what do you notice about the total count?
When the number of hours changes, does the "doubling" part of the math ever actually turn into "quadrupling"?
How does the total number of "2s" in the long string connect back to the little numbers at the top?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if the big numbers at the bottom are the same, is it ALWAYS true that you can just add the tiny numbers at the top to find the total, even if the exponents are negative?
sections.inference: I found this other weird case where a scientist had $2^8$ bacteria but then it got divided by $2^3$ because of some medicine. If multiplying them made the exponent bigger, do you think dividing them just makes the exponent smaller in the same way?

8.ee.b.5_the-speed-dial-sprint.title: The Speed-Dial Sprint
8.ee.b.5_the-speed-dial-sprint.standardDescription: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
8.ee.b.5_the-speed-dial-sprint.description: Teach Zippy about The Speed-Dial Sprint
8.ee.b.5_the-speed-dial-sprint.problemStatement: I’m trying to figure out who’s faster at this new typing game—my friend says she can do 120 words in 2 minutes, but the leaderboard just shows a straight line on a graph that hits the point (5, 250). Since their numbers are in different places, is there a way to tell who’s actually winning?
8.ee.b.5_the-speed-dial-sprint.teachingPrompt: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
targetConcepts: The student can calculate and compare unit rates from different representations (textual descriptions, graphs, and tables).
The student can interpret the slope of a line in a proportional relationship as the unit rate of the scenario.
misconceptions: Coordinate Confusion (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student treats the (5, 250) point as a simple total rather than a ratio of 250 words per 5 minutes.
Steepness Misinterpretation (surface_feature_matching): The student assumes a "higher" number like 250 automatically means a faster rate without comparing the time intervals.
Unit Rate Neglect (procedural_overgeneralization): The student tries to compare the raw numbers 120 and 250 directly without finding out how many words each person types in exactly 1 minute.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m trying to figure out who’s faster at this new typing game—my friend says she can do 120 words in 2 minutes, but the leaderboard just shows a straight line on a graph that hits the point (5, 250). Since their numbers are in different places, is there a way to tell who’s actually winning?

misconceptions.title: Coordinate Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student treats the (5, 250) point as a simple total rather than a ratio of 250 words per 5 minutes.

misconceptions.title: Steepness Misinterpretation
misconceptions.description: The student assumes a "higher" number like 250 automatically means a faster rate without comparing the time intervals.

misconceptions.title: Unit Rate Neglect
misconceptions.description: The student tries to compare the raw numbers 120 and 250 directly without finding out how many words each person types in exactly 1 minute.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we look at the 120 words in 2 minutes, what would happen to that number if we only let her type for 1 minute?
On that leaderboard graph, if the line is perfectly straight, what does that tell us about how many words are added every single minute?
How does the "steepness" of the line on the graph connect back to the actual speed of the typing?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if a graph starts at (0,0) and is a straight line, does that mean the slope is ALWAYS the same thing as the unit rate, no matter which point on the line we pick to check?
sections.inference: I found a third player who uses a table to track their speed, and it shows they did 180 words in 3 minutes. If we put all three players on the same graph, do you think any of their lines would actually overlap, or would one be way steeper than the others?

8.ee.c.7_the-mystery-scale.title: The Mystery Scale
8.ee.c.7_the-mystery-scale.standardDescription: Solve linear equations in one variable. Give examples of linear equations with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming a given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers). Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
8.ee.c.7_the-mystery-scale.description: Teach Zippy about The Mystery Scale
8.ee.c.7_the-mystery-scale.problemStatement: I was messing with a balance scale and put three identical mystery boxes and a 5-gram weight on one side, then two of the same boxes and an 11-gram weight on the other, and it balanced perfectly! But then my brother added a box to both sides and said the scale would still balance no matter what's inside the boxes—is he actually right?
8.ee.c.7_the-mystery-scale.teachingPrompt: Solve linear equations in one variable. Give examples of linear equations with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming a given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers). Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
targetConcepts: The student can transform linear equations into simpler forms ($x = a$, $a = a$, or $a = b$) to determine the number of solutions.
The student can apply the distributive property and collect like terms to solve multi-step equations in a real-world context.
misconceptions: Variable Disappearance (structural_misunderstanding): The student thinks that if the same number of "x" terms are on both sides, the equation just "breaks" or becomes unsolvable rather than having a specific number of solutions.
Operation Mismatch (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student tries to combine the 5-gram weight and the 3 mystery boxes into "8 boxes" instead of keeping the constant and variable separate.
Solution Singularity Bias (partial_concept_model): The student assumes every equation must end with "x equals a number" and feels confused when the math results in a statement like 11 = 11.

sections.studentPrompt: I was messing with a balance scale and put three identical mystery boxes and a 5-gram weight on one side, then two of the same boxes and an 11-gram weight on the other, and it balanced perfectly! But then my brother added a box to both sides and said the scale would still balance no matter what's inside the boxes—is he actually right?

misconceptions.title: Variable Disappearance
misconceptions.description: The student thinks that if the same number of "x" terms are on both sides, the equation just "breaks" or becomes unsolvable rather than having a specific number of solutions.

misconceptions.title: Operation Mismatch
misconceptions.description: The student tries to combine the 5-gram weight and the 3 mystery boxes into "8 boxes" instead of keeping the constant and variable separate.

misconceptions.title: Solution Singularity Bias
misconceptions.description: The student assumes every equation must end with "x equals a number" and feels confused when the math results in a statement like 11 = 11.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we took one box off each side at the same time, what would happen to the balance of the scale?
Does the weight of the individual mystery boxes ever actually change while we're moving things around?
How does the difference between the weights on each side connect to the number of boxes we have left?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I have the exact same number of boxes and weights on both sides of the scale, does that mean it's ALWAYS going to balance perfectly no matter what weight I pick for the boxes?
sections.inference: magic

8.f.a.1_vending-machine-glitch.title: The Vending Machine Glitch
8.f.a.1_vending-machine-glitch.standardDescription: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
8.f.a.1_vending-machine-glitch.description: Teach Zippy about The Vending Machine Glitch
8.f.a.1_vending-machine-glitch.teachingPrompt: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
targetConcepts: The student can define a function as a rule where each input is assigned exactly one output.
The student can identify non-functions
misconceptions: One-to-One Requirement (partial_concept_model): The student believes that a function must have a unique output for every input, thinking that two different buttons (A1 and A2) both giving pretzels would "break" the rule.

Input-Output Reversal (structural_misunderstanding): The student thinks that because they can get pretzels from two different places, the machine isn't working, failing to see that the "rule" only cares about what happens when you push a specific button.

Predictability Confusion (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student mistakes a consistent pattern (A1 always gives pretzels) for a function even if other inputs (B2) are broken, struggling to define the "rule" for the whole system.

sections.studentPrompt: rule,

misconceptions.title: One-to-One Requirement
misconceptions.description: The student believes that a function must have a unique output for every input, thinking that two different buttons (A1 and A2) both giving pretzels would "break" the rule.

misconceptions.title: Input-Output Reversal
misconceptions.description: The student thinks that because they can get pretzels from two different places, the machine isn't working, failing to see that the "rule" only cares about what happens when you push a specific button.

misconceptions.title: Predictability Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student mistakes a consistent pattern (A1 always gives pretzels) for a function even if other inputs (B2) are broken, struggling to define the "rule" for the whole system.

sections.patternRecognition:
If you pushed button A1 a hundred times in a row, what would have to happen for you to be 100% sure the "A1 rule" is working?
When your friend pushed B2, did the machine "know" exactly what to give him, or was it like rolling a die?
How does the "button" you pick connect to the "snack" you get in a way that someone else could predict?
sections.generalization: Wait, so for a machine to be a 'function,' does it mean that if I pick a specific input, it ALWAYS has to give me the exact same result every single time, even if other inputs also give that same result?
sections.inference: glitch,

8.f.b.4_the-birthday-candle-mystery.title: The Birthday Candle Mystery
8.f.b.4_the-birthday-candle-mystery.standardDescription: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
8.f.b.4_the-birthday-candle-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Birthday Candle Mystery
8.f.b.4_the-birthday-candle-mystery.problemStatement: I lit a tall candle and noticed that after 2 minutes of burning, it was 18 centimeters tall, but after 5 minutes, it had dropped down to 12 centimeters. If it keeps melting at this exact same speed, how tall was the candle before I even struck the match?
8.f.b.4_the-birthday-candle-mystery.teachingPrompt: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
targetConcepts: The student can determine the rate of change (slope) and initial value (y-intercept) from two discrete data points.
The student can interpret the initial value as the "starting state" and the rate of change as the "speed of the process" in a real-world context.
misconceptions: Initial Value Neglect (partial_concept_model): The student assumes the candle starts at the first measured height (18 cm) rather than calculating the height at 0 minutes.
Rate Direction Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student treats the "drop" in height as a positive growth rate, resulting in a candle that gets taller as it burns.
Interval Miscalculation (structural_misunderstanding): The student divides the height (12 cm) by the time (5 min) to find the rate, failing to use the change in height over the change in time.

sections.studentPrompt: I lit a tall candle and noticed that after 2 minutes of burning, it was 18 centimeters tall, but after 5 minutes, it had dropped down to 12 centimeters. If it keeps melting at this exact same speed, how tall was the candle before I even struck the match?

misconceptions.title: Initial Value Neglect
misconceptions.description: The student assumes the candle starts at the first measured height (18 cm) rather than calculating the height at 0 minutes.

misconceptions.title: Rate Direction Error
misconceptions.description: The student treats the "drop" in height as a positive growth rate, resulting in a candle that gets taller as it burns.

misconceptions.title: Interval Miscalculation
misconceptions.description: The student divides the height (12 cm) by the time (5 min) to find the rate, failing to use the change in height over the change in time.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we look at those three minutes that passed between the two measurements, exactly how many centimeters did the candle lose?
If the candle lost that much in three minutes, how much must it be losing during every single minute that the flame is lit?
How does the "starting height" of the candle connect to the height we measured after the first two minutes passed?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if a relationship is a straight line, does that mean I can ALWAYS find the exact starting number just by knowing how fast it's changing and where it ended up at any one point?
sections.inference: super-slow

8.g.a.1_the-ghost-shape.title: The Ghost Shape
8.g.a.1_the-ghost-shape.standardDescription: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations: a. Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length. b. Angles are taken to angles of the same measure. c. Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.
8.g.a.1_the-ghost-shape.description: Teach Zippy about The Ghost Shape
8.g.a.1_the-ghost-shape.teachingPrompt: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations: a. Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length. b. Angles are taken to angles of the same measure. c. Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.
targetConcepts: The student can demonstrate that rigid transformations (translation, reflection, rotation) preserve side lengths and angle measures.
The student can verify that parallel lines remain parallel after undergoing a rigid transformation.
misconceptions: Orientation-Length Illusion (surface_feature_matching): The student believes a segment looks longer when vertical than when horizontal, leading them to think the shape changed size during rotation.
Motion Distortion (partial_concept_model): The student assumes that "moving" a shape (like a translation or rotation) is like stretching a rubber band where the distance between points might change.
Angle-Shift Error (structural_misunderstanding): The student thinks that reflecting or turning a shape might "bend" the corners, changing a 90-degree angle into something else.

misconceptions.title: Motion Distortion

misconceptions.title: Angle-Shift Error

If we measured the distance between the two corners on the original plastic and then measured those same two corners on the "ghost" version, what do you think we'd find?
Does the "sharpness" of the corners ever actually change, even if the whole shape is now pointing at a different wall?
How does the space between the two parallel sides of the L-shape stay the same no matter how much we slide the plastic around?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I just slide, flip, or spin a shape without stretching it, does that mean the area and all the side lengths are ALWAYS going to stay exactly the same, no matter how many times I move it?
sections.inference: flip

8.g.b.7_the-shortcut-path.title: The Shortcut Path
8.g.b.7_the-shortcut-path.standardDescription: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
8.g.b.7_the-shortcut-path.description: Teach Zippy about The Shortcut Path
8.g.b.7_the-shortcut-path.problemStatement: I usually walk 30 meters down the sidewalk and then turn a sharp 90-degree corner to walk another 40 meters to get to the park, but today I saw someone just cut straight across the grass from my starting spot to the park gate. If they are walking in a perfectly straight line, does that mean they only had to walk 50 meters, or is there no way to know without a long tape measure?
8.g.b.7_the-shortcut-path.teachingPrompt: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
targetConcepts: The student can identify the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle in a real-world context.
The student can apply the relationship $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ to solve for an unknown diagonal distance.
misconceptions: Additive Distance Bias (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student assumes the diagonal "shortcut" must be equal to the sum of the two legs ($30 + 40 = 70$) because they don't perceive the structural change in the path.

Hypotenuse-Leg Confusion (structural_misunderstanding): The student recognizes a relationship exists but tries to subtract the squares or misidentifies which side is the longest, leading to a shortcut that is shorter than one of the individual legs.

Whole Number Favoritism (whole_number_bias): The student believes that if the two sides are whole numbers, the diagonal must also be a simple whole number, potentially struggling when a scenario results in an irrational square root.

sections.studentPrompt: I usually walk 30 meters down the sidewalk and then turn a sharp 90-degree corner to walk another 40 meters to get to the park, but today I saw someone just cut straight across the grass from my starting spot to the park gate. If they are walking in a perfectly straight line, does that mean they only had to walk 50 meters, or is there no way to know without a long tape measure?

misconceptions.title: Additive Distance Bias
misconceptions.description: The student assumes the diagonal "shortcut" must be equal to the sum of the two legs ($30 + 40 = 70$) because they don't perceive the structural change in the path.

misconceptions.title: Hypotenuse-Leg Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student recognizes a relationship exists but tries to subtract the squares or misidentifies which side is the longest, leading to a shortcut that is shorter than one of the individual legs.

misconceptions.title: Whole Number Favoritism
misconceptions.description: The student believes that if the two sides are whole numbers, the diagonal must also be a simple whole number, potentially struggling when a scenario results in an irrational square root.

sections.patternRecognition:
If the two sidewalks were exactly the same length, what would happen to the length of that "grass shortcut" as both sidewalks got longer?
Does the 90-degree corner in the sidewalk stay the same regardless of how far you have to walk in either direction?
How does the "tilt" of the shortcut change if you walk way further on the first sidewalk than you do on the second one?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I know the exact lengths of any two sides of a right-angled triangle, does that mean I can ALWAYS calculate the third side perfectly, even if the numbers don't come out to be nice and even?
sections.inference: shortcut trick

8.g.c.9_the-melting-cone.title: The Melting Cone
8.g.c.9_the-melting-cone.standardDescription: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
8.g.c.9_the-melting-cone.description: Teach Zippy about The Melting Cone
8.g.c.9_the-melting-cone.problemStatement: I have a cylindrical jar full of water and a cone-shaped cup that is exactly the same height and width as the jar. If I pour the water from the cone into the jar, how many times do you think I'd have to do it to fill the jar all the way to the top without spilling?
8.g.c.9_the-melting-cone.teachingPrompt: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
targetConcepts: The student can explain the fractional relationship between the volumes of cylinders and cones with congruent bases and heights.
The student can apply volume formulas to compare different three-dimensional figures in a practical context.
misconceptions: Height-Only Bias (surface_feature_matching): The student assumes that because the heights are equal, the volumes must be equal or at least very close (1:1 ratio).
Additive Volume Confusion (partial_concept_model): The student guesses a simple 2:1 ratio because the cone "tapers off," failing to account for the specific 1/3 mathematical relationship.

Dimensional Scaling Error (structural_misunderstanding): The student believes that changing the shape from a cylinder to a cone only removes a small fraction of the space, rather than two-thirds of it.

sections.studentPrompt: I have a cylindrical jar full of water and a cone-shaped cup that is exactly the same height and width as the jar. If I pour the water from the cone into the jar, how many times do you think I'd have to do it to fill the jar all the way to the top without spilling?

misconceptions.title: Height-Only Bias
misconceptions.description: The student assumes that because the heights are equal, the volumes must be equal or at least very close (1:1 ratio).

misconceptions.title: Additive Volume Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student guesses a simple 2:1 ratio because the cone "tapers off," failing to account for the specific 1/3 mathematical relationship.

misconceptions.title: Dimensional Scaling Error
misconceptions.description: The student believes that changing the shape from a cylinder to a cone only removes a small fraction of the space, rather than two-thirds of it.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we kept the width of the cone the same but made it twice as tall as the jar, what would happen to how much water it could hold?
Does the "pointy" part of the cone stay the same size regardless of how wide the base of the jar is?
How does the circular bottom of the cone matching the circular bottom of the jar connect to the total amount of water they can each hold?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if a cone and a cylinder have the exact same base and height, is it ALWAYS going to take exactly three cones to fill the cylinder, or does that change if they both get really skinny?
sections.inference: I found a giant spherical fishbowl that has the same width as my cylindrical jar. If I tried to pour three of my cone-cups into the sphere instead of the jar, do you think it would overflow, or is a sphere actually bigger than a cylinder?

8.ns.a.1_the-never-ending-decimal.title: The Never-Ending Decimal
8.ns.a.1_the-never-ending-decimal.standardDescription: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.
8.ns.a.1_the-never-ending-decimal.description: Teach Zippy about The Never-Ending Decimal
8.ns.a.1_the-never-ending-decimal.teachingPrompt: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.
targetConcepts: The student can distinguish between rational numbers (terminating or repeating decimals) and irrational numbers (non-repeating, non-terminating decimals).
The student understands that a repeating decimal expansion is the defining characteristic of a rational number in decimal form.
misconceptions: Infinite Equals Irrational (partial_concept_model): The student believes that any decimal that does not terminate must be an irrational number, failing to recognize repeating patterns as rational.

Pattern vs. Repetition Confusion (surface_feature_matching): The student sees a visual "growth" pattern (like $0.121121112...$) and mistakes it for the kind of repetition that allows a number to be written as a fraction.

Termination Bias (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): The student assumes only "clean" decimals that end (like $0.25$) can be turned back into fractions, viewing all infinite decimals as inherently different.

sections.studentPrompt: broken

misconceptions.title: Infinite Equals Irrational
misconceptions.description: The student believes that any decimal that does not terminate must be an irrational number, failing to recognize repeating patterns as rational.

misconceptions.title: Pattern vs. Repetition Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student sees a visual "growth" pattern (like $0.121121112...$) and mistakes it for the kind of repetition that allows a number to be written as a fraction.

misconceptions.title: Termination Bias
misconceptions.description: The student assumes only "clean" decimals that end (like $0.25$) can be turned back into fractions, viewing all infinite decimals as inherently different.

sections.patternRecognition:
When you look at the $0.333...$ part, is there a specific "chunk" of numbers that just keeps showing up over and over again?
In that $0.121121112...$ number, even though it looks like it has a rule, does it ever actually repeat the exact same block of digits?
How does having a predictable, repeating block change whether we can predict what the millionth decimal place is going to be?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I can find a way to write a decimal as a fraction, does that mean it's ALWAYS going to eventually start repeating a pattern, even if that pattern is just a bunch of zeros?
sections.inference: growing

8.sp.a.1_the-midnight-snack-study.title: The Midnight Snack Study
8.sp.a.1_the-midnight-snack-study.standardDescription: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
8.sp.a.1_the-midnight-snack-study.description: Teach Zippy about The Midnight Snack Study
8.sp.a.1_the-midnight-snack-study.problemStatement: I tracked how many grams of sugar my friends ate before bed and then asked them how many minutes it took them to fall asleep, but the dots on my graph are all over the place. If the dots aren't in a perfect straight line, does that mean the sugar didn't actually do anything to their sleep?
8.sp.a.1_the-midnight-snack-study.teachingPrompt: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
targetConcepts: The student can identify and describe the direction (positive/negative) and shape (linear/nonlinear) of an association in a scatter plot.
The student can identify outliers and clusters and explain how they affect the overall interpretation of the data.
misconceptions: Deterministic Bias (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student believes that if a relationship exists, every single data point must follow a strict, predictable rule without any "stray" dots.
Outlier Overemphasis (surface_feature_matching): The student focuses on one person who ate a lot of sugar and slept instantly, concluding there is no pattern at all because of that one exception.

Linearity Requirement (partial_concept_model): The student assumes that "association" only counts if the dots form a perfectly thin line, rather than recognizing a "cloud" or "trend" that moves in a general direction.

sections.studentPrompt: I tracked how many grams of sugar my friends ate before bed and then asked them how many minutes it took them to fall asleep, but the dots on my graph are all over the place. If the dots aren't in a perfect straight line, does that mean the sugar didn't actually do anything to their sleep?

misconceptions.title: Deterministic Bias
misconceptions.description: The student believes that if a relationship exists, every single data point must follow a strict, predictable rule without any "stray" dots.

misconceptions.title: Outlier Overemphasis
misconceptions.description: The student focuses on one person who ate a lot of sugar and slept instantly, concluding there is no pattern at all because of that one exception.

misconceptions.title: Linearity Requirement
misconceptions.description: The student assumes that "association" only counts if the dots form a perfectly thin line, rather than recognizing a "cloud" or "trend" that moves in a general direction.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we stepped back and looked at the whole "cloud" of dots from across the room, does the cloud seem to be tilting up or sliding down?
Is there one specific person's data that looks like it's totally "lost" and doesn't belong with the rest of the group?
How does the amount of sugar on the bottom of the graph connect to whether the dots are generally getting higher or lower?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I see a pattern where the dots generally go up together, does that ALWAYS mean that the first thing is actually causing the second thing to happen?
sections.inference: huddle

hsa-apr.b_zero-shortcut.title: The Zero Shortcut
hsa-apr.b_zero-shortcut.standardDescription: Know and apply the Remainder Theorem: For a polynomial p(x) and a number a, the remainder on division by x - a is p(a), so p(a) = 0 if and only if (x - a) is a factor of p(x).
hsa-apr.b_zero-shortcut.description: Teach Zippy about The Zero Shortcut
hsa-apr.b_zero-shortcut.problemStatement: I'm trying to figure out if $(x - 3)$ fits perfectly into this long polynomial $p(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6$, but I really don't want to do that whole long division thing. My friend says I can just plug a single number into the formula and if I get zero, it means there’s no remainder. Does just finding $p(3)$ actually tell me the same thing as doing all that division work?
hsa-apr.b_zero-shortcut.teachingPrompt: Know and apply the Remainder Theorem: For a polynomial p(x) and a number a, the remainder on division by x - a is p(a), so p(a) = 0 if and only if (x - a) is a factor of p(x).
targetConcepts: Students can use the Remainder Theorem to determine if a linear expression $(x - a)$ is a factor of a given polynomial.
Students can explain the relationship between the functional value $p(a)$ and the remainder of $p(x) / (x - a)$.
misconceptions: Sign Substitution Error (partial_concept_model): A student might plug in $-3$ instead of $3$ because they see the minus sign in the factor $(x - 3)$.
Remainder-Value Dissociation (structural_misunderstanding): A student might think that $p(3)$ gives a different number than the remainder they would get from long division.
Factor vs. Zero Confusion (misinterpretation_of_representation): A student might conclude that if $p(3) = 10$, then $(x - 10)$ is the factor instead of recognizing that 10 is just the remainder.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm trying to figure out if $(x - 3)$ fits perfectly into this long polynomial $p(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6$, but I really don't want to do that whole long division thing. My friend says I can just plug a single number into the formula and if I get zero, it means there’s no remainder. Does just finding $p(3)$ actually tell me the same thing as doing all that division work?

misconceptions.title: Sign Substitution Error
misconceptions.description: A student might plug in $-3$ instead of $3$ because they see the minus sign in the factor $(x - 3)$.

misconceptions.title: Remainder-Value Dissociation
misconceptions.description: A student might think that $p(3)$ gives a different number than the remainder they would get from long division.

misconceptions.title: Factor vs. Zero Confusion
misconceptions.description: A student might conclude that if $p(3) = 10$, then $(x - 10)$ is the factor instead of recognizing that 10 is just the remainder.

sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait—if you plug in a number and the answer comes out to zero, what does that usually mean in a division problem?"
"Okay, but why are we using positive 3 when the original part we're testing has a minus sign in it?"
How does the final number we get from the 'plug-in' trick connect to the leftover part at the bottom of a long division tower?
sections.generalization: If I plug a number into a polynomial and the result is 5, does that mean $(x - 5)$ is ALWAYS a factor of that polynomial?
sections.inference: I found this other polynomial $q(x)$ where I know $q(2) = 0$ and $q(-5) = 0$, and it feels like a total cheat code. If I know those two things are true, can I figure out what the actual factors are without ever seeing the full equation? And like, if I had a mystery polynomial where $p(10) = 4$, could we predict exactly what the remainder would be if we divided it by $(x - 10)$?

hsa-ced.a_lemonade-stand-logic.title: The Lemonade Stand Logic
hsa-ced.a_lemonade-stand-logic.standardDescription: Create equations that describe numbers or relationships.
hsa-ced.a_lemonade-stand-logic.description: Teach Zippy about The Lemonade Stand Logic
hsa-ced.a_lemonade-stand-logic.problemStatement: I'm trying to figure out how much money we actually made at our lemonade stand yesterday. We spent $12 on lemons and sugar, and we sold every cup for $0.50, but I can't remember how many cups we sold or how much total profit we walked away with. If I know the profit was $18, how can I find out how many people actually bought a drink?
hsa-ced.a_lemonade-stand-logic.teachingPrompt: Create equations that describe numbers or relationships.
targetConcepts: Students can identify and represent constant and varying quantities within a real-world context.
Students can create a mathematical model that correctly relates initial costs, rates of change, and a final outcome.
misconceptions: Ignoring Initial Costs (partial_concept_model): Students might only focus on the income per cup ($0.50) and divide the profit by that amount, forgetting the $12 spent upfront.
Additive vs. Multiplicative Confusion (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may attempt to subtract the per-cup price from the total profit rather than recognizing it as a rate of change.

Misinterpreting Profit vs. Revenue (structural_misunderstanding): Students might confuse total money collected with the final profit, leading to an equation that doesn't account for the starting deficit.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm trying to figure out how much money we actually made at our lemonade stand yesterday. We spent $12 on lemons and sugar, and we sold every cup for $0.50, but I can't remember how many cups we sold or how much total profit we walked away with. If I know the profit was $18, how can I find out how many people actually bought a drink?

misconceptions.title: Ignoring Initial Costs
misconceptions.description: Students might only focus on the income per cup ($0.50) and divide the profit by that amount, forgetting the $12 spent upfront.

misconceptions.title: Additive vs. Multiplicative Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may attempt to subtract the per-cup price from the total profit rather than recognizing it as a rate of change.

misconceptions.title: Misinterpreting Profit vs. Revenue
misconceptions.description: Students might confuse total money collected with the final profit, leading to an equation that doesn't account for the starting deficit.

sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait—does the $12 we spent ever change no matter how many cups we sell?"
"If we sell one more cup, does the $18 profit go up by fifty cents or the whole twelve dollars?"
How does the number of cups we sell affect whether that $18 profit number goes up or down?
sections.generalization: Wait, so does that mean the profit will ALWAYS be less than the total money we collect from the customers, or is there a weird case where those two numbers could be the same?
sections.inference: I was looking at the neighbor's stand and they have a totally different setup where they spent $20 on fancy organic limes and sell each cup for $1.50. They said they also made exactly $25 profit yesterday, just like we did on our best day. Do you think there's a way to figure out how many cups they had to sell compared to us without just guessing numbers for an hour?

hsa-rei.a_square-root-mystery.title: The Square Root Mystery
hsa-rei.a_square-root-mystery.standardDescription: Explain each step in solving a simple equation as following from the equality of numbers asserted at the previous step, starting from the assumption that the original equation has a solution. Construct a viable argument to justify a solution method.
hsa-rei.a_square-root-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Square Root Mystery
hsa-rei.a_square-root-mystery.problemStatement: I was trying to solve $\sqrt{x + 5} = -2$ by squaring both sides to get $x + 5 = 4$, which gave me $x = -1$. But when I plug $-1$ back into the original start, it says $\sqrt{4} = -2$, and my calculator says that's just wrong. If I followed all the rules for equality, why did I end up with a number that doesn't actually work?
hsa-rei.a_square-root-mystery.teachingPrompt: Explain each step in solving a simple equation as following from the equality of numbers asserted at the previous step, starting from the assumption that the original equation has a solution. Construct a viable argument to justify a solution method.
targetConcepts: Students can construct a viable argument to justify each step of a solution process while identifying where the principle of equality might introduce extraneous solutions. [cite: 174, 377]
Students can explain the necessity of checking solutions in radical equations based on the properties of the operations used. [cite: 173, 180]
misconceptions: Squaring Preserves Truth Only (procedural_overgeneralization): [cite_start]Students often assume that if $a^2 = b^2$, then $a$ must have equaled $b$, forgetting that squaring can turn different values (like $2$ and $-2$) into the same value ($4$). [cite: 180, 435]

Root Index Confusion (misinterpretation_of_representation): [cite_start]Students may treat the radical symbol as a grouping symbol only, neglecting the specific property that a principal square root is non-negative by definition. [cite: 435]

Solution vs. Value (structural_misunderstanding): Students might believe any value produced at the end of a correct algebraic chain is automatically a solution, without understanding how extraneous solutions arise. [cite: 180, 435]

sections.studentPrompt: I was trying to solve $\sqrt{x + 5} = -2$ by squaring both sides to get $x + 5 = 4$, which gave me $x = -1$. But when I plug $-1$ back into the original start, it says $\sqrt{4} = -2$, and my calculator says that's just wrong. If I followed all the rules for equality, why did I end up with a number that doesn't actually work?

misconceptions.title: Squaring Preserves Truth Only
misconceptions.description: [cite_start]Students often assume that if $a^2 = b^2$, then $a$ must have equaled $b$, forgetting that squaring can turn different values (like $2$ and $-2$) into the same value ($4$). [cite: 180, 435]

misconceptions.title: Root Index Confusion
misconceptions.description: [cite_start]Students may treat the radical symbol as a grouping symbol only, neglecting the specific property that a principal square root is non-negative by definition. [cite: 435]

misconceptions.title: Solution vs. Value
misconceptions.description: Students might believe any value produced at the end of a correct algebraic chain is automatically a solution, without understanding how extraneous solutions arise. [cite: 180, 435]

sections.patternRecognition:
"When we squared both sides, did we accidentally make a negative number look exactly like a positive one?" [cite: 173, 180]
"Wait—if you have a square root of something, is there ever a time it's allowed to equal a negative number before you do any math to it?" [cite: 435]
"Does every single step we took actually 'go both ways,' or is there a spot where we can't go backward to the start?" [cite: 377]
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I have an equation with a square root, does that mean I'm ALWAYS going to get an extra 'fake' answer, or does that only happen when the other side starts out as a negative?
sections.inference: My friend was solving a different one, $\sqrt[3]{x - 2} = -2$, and they did the same thing—cubing both sides to get $x - 2 = -8$, so $x = -6$. They checked it and it actually worked perfectly, even though the side was negative! Do you think the 'fake answer' thing only happens with square roots, or is there a way to tell which roots will play nice?

hsa-rei.c_phone-plan-cross.title: The Phone Plan Cross
hsa-rei.c_phone-plan-cross.description: Teach Zippy about The Phone Plan Cross
hsa-rei.c_phone-plan-cross.problemStatement: I'm looking at two different monthly phone plans where one charges a flat $30 fee plus $2 for every gigabyte of data, and the other is just a flat $50 with unlimited data. If my bill came out exactly the same for both last month, how much data did I actually use?
targetConcepts: Students can represent a system of linear equations from a contextual scenario and identify the significance of the intersection point.
Students can utilize algebraic methods (substitution or elimination) to find the unique solution that satisfies multiple constraints simultaneously.
misconceptions: The Single Variable Trap (partial_concept_model): Students might try to solve for the cost or the data separately without realizing both variables must be equal at the point of intersection.
Subtraction Confusion (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may attempt to subtract the $30 fee from the $2 rate rather than finding the difference between the two flat fees.
Misinterpretation of 'Unlimited' (surface_feature_matching): Students might struggle to represent a flat rate as an equation because it lacks a visible "rate" or variable term.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm looking at two different monthly phone plans where one charges a flat $30 fee plus $2 for every gigabyte of data, and the other is just a flat $50 with unlimited data. If my bill came out exactly the same for both last month, how much data did I actually use?

misconceptions.title: The Single Variable Trap
misconceptions.description: Students might try to solve for the cost or the data separately without realizing both variables must be equal at the point of intersection.

misconceptions.title: Subtraction Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may attempt to subtract the $30 fee from the $2 rate rather than finding the difference between the two flat fees.

misconceptions.title: Misinterpretation of 'Unlimited'
misconceptions.description: Students might struggle to represent a flat rate as an equation because it lacks a visible "rate" or variable term.

sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait—does the $50 plan ever change price if I use 1GB or 100GB of data?"
"If I use 5GB, which plan is cheaper, and does that switch at some point?"
How much 'extra' money is the second plan charging upfront compared to the first one's flat fee?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if two plans have different starting fees and different rates per gigabyte, is there ALWAYS going to be a specific amount of data where they cost the exact same?
sections.inference: My sister found a third plan that's $10 a month but $4 per gigabyte, and she says it's way better for her. If she ends up paying the same $50 as the unlimited plan, do you think we can find her data usage using the same logic we used for my plan? Or does the higher rate change the 'trick' for finding the crossover point?

hsa-sse.a_phone-plan-mystery.title: The Phone Plan Mystery
hsa-sse.a_phone-plan-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Phone Plan Mystery
hsa-sse.a_phone-plan-mystery.problemStatement: My older brother has this weird phone bill that says his monthly cost is $40 + 0.15(g - 2)$. He says $g$ is how many gigabytes of data he uses, but he can't remember if the 2 is a discount or some kind of limit. If he uses exactly 2 gigabytes, does that $0.15$ part even matter for what he has to pay?
targetConcepts: [cite_start]Students can interpret parts of an expression, such as terms, factors, and coefficients, in a real-world context[cite: 140, 362].
[cite_start]Students can view complicated expressions by seeing one or more of their parts as a single entity to understand the overall structure[cite: 385].

sections.studentPrompt: My older brother has this weird phone bill that says his monthly cost is $40 + 0.15(g - 2)$. He says $g$ is how many gigabytes of data he uses, but he can't remember if the 2 is a discount or some kind of limit. If he uses exactly 2 gigabytes, does that $0.15$ part even matter for what he has to pay?
sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait—if he uses 3 gigabytes instead of 2, which part of that whole string of numbers actually changes?"
"Okay, but is there a specific amount he has to pay every single month even if he turns his data off completely?"
How does the 'g minus 2' part connect the total data he used to that 0.15 charge?
sections.generalization: If we changed the 40 to a higher number, would the extra cost for using 10 gigabytes ALWAYS stay the same as it was before?
sections.inference: I found another plan for a different company that looks like $30 + 0.25(g - 5)$ and it's making me wonder about how these things are built. Do you think the same 'trick' works for figuring out the base price here, and could we tell just by looking which plan is better if you use a ton of data? I'm curious if there's a certain amount of data where both brothers would end up paying the exact same thing.

hsa-sse.b_growing-square.title: The Growing Square
hsa-sse.b_growing-square.standardDescription: Write expressions in equivalent forms to solve problems.
hsa-sse.b_growing-square.description: Teach Zippy about The Growing Square
hsa-sse.b_growing-square.problemStatement: I’m looking at this pattern for a tiled floor where the total number of tiles is $x^{2} - 10x + 25$, but my friend says she can figure out the side length just by looking at that one number at the end. If $x$ is the original measurement we started with, is there a way to see the 'side times side' version of this square without having to test every single number for $x$?
hsa-sse.b_growing-square.teachingPrompt: Write expressions in equivalent forms to solve problems.
targetConcepts: Students can factor a quadratic expression to reveal the zeros or the dimensions of the area it defines.
Students can choose and produce an equivalent form of an expression to reveal properties of the quantity represented.
misconceptions: Linear Reduction Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): [A student might think the side length is simply $x - 10$ or $x - 25$ because they are only looking at the individual terms rather than the squared structure.

Sign Flipping (structural_misunderstanding): [A student may correctly identify the square root of 25 but write the expression as $(x + 5)^{2}$, failing to account for how the negative middle term is produced.

Square Rooting Terms Individually (procedural_overgeneralization): [A student might believe the side length is $x - 5$ but think that $(x - 5)^{2}$ is just $x^{2} - 25$, forgetting the middle product.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m looking at this pattern for a tiled floor where the total number of tiles is $x^{2} - 10x + 25$, but my friend says she can figure out the side length just by looking at that one number at the end. If $x$ is the original measurement we started with, is there a way to see the 'side times side' version of this square without having to test every single number for $x$?

misconceptions.description: [A student might think the side length is simply $x - 10$ or $x - 25$ because they are only looking at the individual terms rather than the squared structure.

misconceptions.title: Sign Flipping
misconceptions.description: [A student may correctly identify the square root of 25 but write the expression as $(x + 5)^{2}$, failing to account for how the negative middle term is produced.

misconceptions.title: Square Rooting Terms Individually
misconceptions.description: [A student might believe the side length is $x - 5$ but think that $(x - 5)^{2}$ is just $x^{2} - 25$, forgetting the middle product.

sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait—if the total number of tiles is a perfect square, what does that tell us about how the length and the width have to relate to each other?"
"Okay, but if we changed that 25 to a 36, what would have to happen to that middle 10 for the floor to stay a perfect square?"
How does the 'minus 10' part connect the $x$ we started with to the final 25 tiles we added or removed?
sections.generalization: If the last number in our expression is a perfect square like 25 or 49, does that mean the whole thing can ALWAYS be written as a single side length squared?
sections.inference: I found this other floor design that follows the rule $x^{2} + 12x + 36$ and it looks totally different, but my friend says the 'squaring trick' still works. Do you think we could use that same logic to find the side length of a massive floor where the tiles are $x^{2} + 20x + 100$? And like, is there a way to know the side length for ANY square floor if we just know those last two numbers?

hsf-bf.a_growing-square-garden.title: The Growing Square Garden
hsf-bf.a_growing-square-garden.standardDescription: Build a function that models a relationship between two quantities.
hsf-bf.a_growing-square-garden.description: Teach Zippy about The Growing Square Garden
hsf-bf.a_growing-square-garden.problemStatement: I'm helping my neighbor build a square garden that starts out 2 feet by 2 feet, but every year she adds exactly 1 foot to the length and 1 foot to the width. I'm trying to figure out how many square feet of dirt we'll need for the floor in 10 years, but do I have to draw the bigger square for every single year to find the total space?
hsf-bf.a_growing-square-garden.teachingPrompt: Build a function that models a relationship between two quantities.
targetConcepts: Students can construct a functional relationship (recursive or explicit) that models the area of a shape based on a change in its dimensions over time.
Students can differentiate between linear changes in dimensions and the resulting non-linear change in area.
misconceptions: Linear Growth Assumption (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students might notice the side length increases by 1 each year and assume the total area also increases by a constant amount each time.
Incorrect Starting Value (partial_concept_model): Students may forget to include the original 2x2 area and only calculate the area of the "new" border added each year.
Input-Output Misalignment (structural_misunderstanding): Students might use the side length as the "year number," forgetting that at Year 0 the side is already 2 feet long.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm helping my neighbor build a square garden that starts out 2 feet by 2 feet, but every year she adds exactly 1 foot to the length and 1 foot to the width. I'm trying to figure out how many square feet of dirt we'll need for the floor in 10 years, but do I have to draw the bigger square for every single year to find the total space?

misconceptions.title: Linear Growth Assumption
misconceptions.description: Students might notice the side length increases by 1 each year and assume the total area also increases by a constant amount each time.

misconceptions.title: Incorrect Starting Value
misconceptions.description: Students may forget to include the original 2x2 area and only calculate the area of the "new" border added each year.

misconceptions.title: Input-Output Misalignment
misconceptions.description: Students might use the side length as the "year number," forgetting that at Year 0 the side is already 2 feet long.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If we look at how much the area grows from Year 1 to Year 2, is that the same amount it grows from Year 2 to Year 3?"
"What is the one thing about the shape that stays exactly the same every single time it gets bigger?"
How does the number of years we've waited connect back to how long just one side of the square is?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if the sides are growing by a steady amount, does that mean the total area is ALWAYS going to grow by a steady amount too, or does it start to accelerate the longer we wait?
sections.inference: Okay, so what if she decided to make it a rectangular garden instead, starting at 2 feet by 5 feet, but she still only adds 1 foot to both sides every year? I noticed the area in Year 1 was 18, and in Year 2 it was 28... do you think we can find a trick to get the area for Year 50 without having to do all the years in between?

hsf-if.b_heart-rate-monitor.title: The Heart Rate Monitor
hsf-if.b_heart-rate-monitor.standardDescription: For a function that models a relationship between two quantities, interpret key features of graphs and tables in terms of the quantities, and sketch graphs showing key features given a verbal description of the relationship. Key features include: intercepts; intervals where the function is increasing, decreasing, positive, or negative; relative maximums and minimums; symmetries; end behavior; and periodicity.
hsf-if.b_heart-rate-monitor.description: Teach Zippy about The Heart Rate Monitor
hsf-if.b_heart-rate-monitor.problemStatement: I was looking at the graph from my watch after a workout, and it shows my heart rate going up and down over thirty minutes. If the line starts at 70, shoots way up to 160, and then eventually levels off back at 70, what was actually happening to me during those flat parts of the line versus the steep parts?
hsf-if.b_heart-rate-monitor.teachingPrompt: For a function that models a relationship between two quantities, interpret key features of graphs and tables in terms of the quantities, and sketch graphs showing key features given a verbal description of the relationship. Key features include: intercepts; intervals where the function is increasing, decreasing, positive, or negative; relative maximums and minimums; symmetries; end behavior; and periodicity.
targetConcepts: Students can identify and interpret key features of a graph (intercepts, extrema, intervals of increase/decrease) in the context of a real-world scenario.
Students can relate the behavior of a function to the physical actions or constraints of the situation it models.
misconceptions: Slope as Speed (surface_feature_matching): Students might think a steep line means the person was running fast, rather than identifying it as a rapid change in heart rate over time.

Interval Confusion (partial_concept_model): Students may struggle to distinguish between the value of the function (how high the heart rate is) and the interval (the time during which it stayed at that height).

Relative vs. Absolute Extrema (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students might assume the highest point on the graph is the only important data point, ignoring smaller "peaks" that represent different intervals of effort.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at the graph from my watch after a workout, and it shows my heart rate going up and down over thirty minutes. If the line starts at 70, shoots way up to 160, and then eventually levels off back at 70, what was actually happening to me during those flat parts of the line versus the steep parts?

misconceptions.title: Slope as Speed
misconceptions.description: Students might think a steep line means the person was running fast, rather than identifying it as a rapid change in heart rate over time.

misconceptions.title: Interval Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may struggle to distinguish between the value of the function (how high the heart rate is) and the interval (the time during which it stayed at that height).

misconceptions.title: Relative vs. Absolute Extrema

sections.patternRecognition:
"When the line on the watch screen goes perfectly flat, does that mean my heart stopped or just that it stopped changing?"
"Wait—if the line is way higher at the ten-minute mark than the five-minute mark, what does that tell us about the intensity of what I was doing?"
Is there a difference between the 'start' point at zero minutes and the 'end' point where the line levels off again?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if a graph is 'increasing,' does that ALWAYS mean the person is getting faster, or could they be slowing down while their heart is still working harder and harder?
sections.inference: I found my friend's graph from their swimming workout, and it looks totally different—it's like a bunch of identical waves that go up and down every two minutes. If we know they did ten laps, is there a way to figure out which parts of the 'wave' show them resting at the wall without them telling us? And do you think the graph would look the same if they waited five minutes between laps instead of thirty seconds?

hsf-le.a_social-media-spark.title: The Social Media Spark
hsf-le.a_social-media-spark.standardDescription: Distinguish between situations that can be modeled with linear functions and with exponential functions.
hsf-le.a_social-media-spark.description: Teach Zippy about The Social Media Spark
hsf-le.a_social-media-spark.problemStatement: I posted a video yesterday and noticed that every hour, exactly 50 more people had viewed it than the hour before. But my friend says their video is 'going viral' because their views are actually doubling every single hour. Since we both started with 10 views, is there a point where my steady growth eventually falls behind their doubling speed?
hsf-le.a_social-media-spark.teachingPrompt: Distinguish between situations that can be modeled with linear functions and with exponential functions.
targetConcepts: Students can distinguish between situations where a quantity grows by a constant real number versus a constant percentage/factor.
Students can demonstrate that an exponential function will eventually exceed a linear function.
misconceptions: Growth Rate Confusion (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may think that adding 50 is "faster" than doubling simply because 50 is a larger number than 2.
Initial Lead Bias (surface_feature_matching): Students might conclude the linear model is superior because it has more views in the first few hours (60 vs 20).
Linearity of Percentage (partial_concept_model): Students may incorrectly assume that "doubling" (100% growth) means adding the same amount of views each time.

sections.studentPrompt: I posted a video yesterday and noticed that every hour, exactly 50 more people had viewed it than the hour before. But my friend says their video is 'going viral' because their views are actually doubling every single hour. Since we both started with 10 views, is there a point where my steady growth eventually falls behind their doubling speed?

misconceptions.title: Growth Rate Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may think that adding 50 is "faster" than doubling simply because 50 is a larger number than 2.

misconceptions.title: Initial Lead Bias
misconceptions.description: Students might conclude the linear model is superior because it has more views in the first few hours (60 vs 20).

misconceptions.title: Linearity of Percentage
misconceptions.description: Students may incorrectly assume that "doubling" (100% growth) means adding the same amount of views each time.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you look at your views from Hour 1 to Hour 2, is that 'jump' the same size as the jump from Hour 10 to Hour 11?"
"Wait—if your friend's views keep doubling, does the amount they add each hour stay the same or does that number change?"
How does the 'starting amount' affect your steady $+50$ growth versus your friend's $\times 2$ growth?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if one thing adds a fixed amount and another thing multiplies by a fixed number, is the multiplying one ALWAYS going to end up with a bigger total if we just wait long enough?
sections.inference: I found a third video where the views are growing by 10% every hour instead of doubling. It feels super slow right now because it's only adding like one view at a time, but do you think it acts more like my '+50' video or my friend's 'doubling' video? Like, if we checked back in a month, would that tiny 10% jump eventually catch up to my steady 50-view hourly gain?

hsf-tf.a.3_mirroring-laser.title: The Mirroring Laser
hsf-tf.a.3_mirroring-laser.standardDescription: (+) Use special triangles to determine geometrically the values of sine, cosine, tangent for π/3, π/4 and π/6, and use the unit circle to express the values of sine, cosine, and tangent for π-x, π+x, and 2π-x in terms of their values for x, where x is any real number.
hsf-tf.a.3_mirroring-laser.description: Teach Zippy about The Mirroring Laser
hsf-tf.a.3_mirroring-laser.problemStatement: I’m playing with this laser simulator where you point a beam from the center of a circle to a point on the edge, and it tells you the height of that point. When I pointed the laser at 30 degrees, the height was exactly 0.5, but when I swung it around to 150 degrees, the height was exactly the same. Since 150 is just 180 minus 30, how can we know for sure which other angles will give us that same height or the exact opposite height without having to check every single degree on the circle?
hsf-tf.a.3_mirroring-laser.teachingPrompt: (+) Use special triangles to determine geometrically the values of sine, cosine, tangent for π/3, π/4 and π/6, and use the unit circle to express the values of sine, cosine, and tangent for π-x, π+x, and 2π-x in terms of their values for x, where x is any real number.
targetConcepts: Students can use symmetry across the axes of the unit circle to relate the trigonometric values of $x$ to $\pi-x$, $\pi+x$, and $2\pi-x$.
Students can justify the signs (positive/negative) of trigonometric functions based on the coordinate quadrant of the terminal side.
misconceptions: Linear Growth Assumption (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students assume that if 30 degrees gives a height of 0.5, then 60 degrees must give a height of 1.0.

Quadrant Sign Neglect (partial_concept_model): Students believe that because the "steepness" or triangle shape is the same, the sine or cosine values must be identical, ignoring the negative signs in different quadrants.

Reference Angle Overgeneralization (procedural_overgeneralization): Students think that any two angles that are 180 degrees apart will have the exact same sine and cosine values because they "line up" across the center.
