When I pointed the laser at 30 degrees, the height was exactly 0.5, but when I swung it around to 150 degrees, the height was exactly the same. Since 150 is just 180 minus 30, how can we know for sure which other angles will give us that same height or the exact opposite height without having to check every single degree on the circle?

misconceptions.title: Linear Growth Assumption
misconceptions.description: Students assume that if 30 degrees gives a height of 0.5, then 60 degrees must give a height of 1.0.

misconceptions.title: Quadrant Sign Neglect
misconceptions.description: Students believe that because the "steepness" or triangle shape is the same, the sine or cosine values must be identical, ignoring the negative signs in different quadrants.

misconceptions.title: Reference Angle Overgeneralization
misconceptions.description: Students think that any two angles that are 180 degrees apart will have the exact same sine and cosine values because they "line up" across the center.

sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait, if we move the laser to the left side of the circle, does the vertical height change at all if the angle from the flat line is still the same?"
"Look at the distance from the center to the right—does that horizontal length stay the same when we flip the triangle upside down?"
Is there a reason why the height at 210 degrees is just the negative version of the height at 30 degrees?
sections.generalization: I was thinking... if you pick any angle and then look at the angle that is exactly 180 degrees 'ahead' of it, will the height always just be the same number but with a negative sign?
sections.inference: So I found this other simulator for a rotating wind turbine where the blade tip starts at (1, 0). When the blade rotates to $\pi/3$, the horizontal distance from the center is 0.5, and when it's at $2\pi/3$, the distance is -0.5. Do you think that same 'flipping' trick works for any angle $x$ we pick? Like, is there a way to know the horizontal distance for an angle like $4\pi/3$ or even $5\pi/3$ just by knowing what happened at the very start?

hsg-c.b.5_sprinkler-reach.title: The Sprinkler's Reach
hsg-c.b.5_sprinkler-reach.standardDescription: Derive using similarity the fact that the length of the arc intercepted by an angle is proportional to the radius, and define the radian measure of the angle as the constant of proportionality; derive the formula for the area of a sector.
hsg-c.b.5_sprinkler-reach.description: Teach Zippy about The Sprinkler's Reach
hsg-c.b.5_sprinkler-reach.problemStatement: I was watching the pivot sprinkler in my backyard, and it’s set to rotate the same amount every time, like a perfect slice of pie. When I moved the sprinkler from the small circular flower bed to the big open lawn, I noticed the wet path along the edge got way longer, even though the 'turn' of the sprinkler didn't change at all. If the sprinkler is turning the exact same amount, how can we tell exactly how much more grass it’s watering just because the hose reached further out?
hsg-c.b.5_sprinkler-reach.teachingPrompt: Derive using similarity the fact that the length of the arc intercepted by an angle is proportional to the radius, and define the radian measure of the angle as the constant of proportionality; derive the formula for the area of a sector.
targetConcepts: Students can identify that arc length is proportional to the radius for a fixed angle and define this constant as the radian measure.
Students can justify the formula for the area of a sector by relating it to the fractional area of the entire circle.
misconceptions: Constant Arc Length (structural_misunderstanding): Students assume that if the angle of rotation is fixed, the distance traveled by the water at the edge remains the same regardless of the radius.
Linear Area Growth (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students believe that doubling the distance the water shoots will simply double the area of the grass being watered.
Unit Confusion (partial_concept_model): Students attempt to relate the angle to the arc length using a 1:1 ratio without accounting for the circumference or the radius.

If the sprinkler is turning the exact same amount, how can we tell exactly how much more grass it’s watering just because the hose reached further out?

misconceptions.description: Students assume that if the angle of rotation is fixed, the distance traveled by the water at the edge remains the same regardless of the radius.

misconceptions.title: Linear Area Growth
misconceptions.description: Students believe that doubling the distance the water shoots will simply double the area of the grass being watered.

misconceptions.title: Unit Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students attempt to relate the angle to the arc length using a 1:1 ratio without accounting for the circumference or the radius.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If we walk twice as far away from the sprinkler, does the curved path we have to walk to stay in the water get twice as long too?"
"Does the 'pointiness' of the pie slice change at all when the sprinkler shoots further out, or does it stay the same shape?"
Wait, if the distance from the center and the length of the curve both grow, is there a specific number that connects them no matter how big the circle gets?
sections.generalization: So, I was thinking... if you have two different sprinklers that both turn the same amount, will the ratio of the 'curved edge' to the 'distance from the center' always stay the same, even if one sprinkler is tiny and the other is huge?
sections.inference: Okay, so I found this old drawing of a stone amphitheater where the rows of seats are just big arcs. In the first row, 10 feet from the center, the seat is about 15.7 feet long. Then in the row that's 20 feet from the center, the seat is about 31.4 feet long. Do you think there’s a way to figure out how long the seat would be for any row we pick, even the 100th one? And like, if we knew that 'magic number' for the turn, could we figure out the total floor space of the whole section without measuring every inch?

hsg-co.d.12_the_folded_banner.title: The Perfectly Centered Mascot
hsg-co.d.12_the_folded_banner.standardDescription: Make formal geometric constructions with a variety of tools and methods (compass and straightedge, string, reflective devices, paper folding, dynamic geometric software, etc.).
hsg-co.d.12_the_folded_banner.description: Teach Zippy about The Perfectly Centered Mascot
hsg-co.d.12_the_folded_banner.problemStatement: Hey, so I’m trying to help the art club with this massive banner for the gym, but I’m totally stuck. The paper is way longer than my 12-inch ruler, and we have to put the mascot exactly in the dead center. I tried folding the whole thing in half to find a crease, but then someone said the mascot has to be perfectly straight up-and-down too, not tilted at all. If I can’t actually measure the long side because it’s too big, how am I supposed to find that one spot that’s the exact middle and make sure my vertical line is perfectly square with the edge without just 'winging it'?
hsg-co.d.12_the_folded_banner.teachingPrompt: Make formal geometric constructions with a variety of tools and methods (compass and straightedge, string, reflective devices, paper folding, dynamic geometric software, etc.).
misconceptions: Visual Estimation Bias (partial_concept_confusion): Believing that "eyeballing" the center point is accurate enough for geometric properties, ignoring that manual estimation lacks the mathematical precision of a construction.

Additive Misunderstanding of Bisectors (partial_concept_confusion): Assuming that any line crossing a segment "splits" it, failing to recognize that a perpendicular bisector must meet specific criteria (equidistance from endpoints) to be truly centered and upright.

Tool Dependency (procedural_overgeneralization): Thinking that a ruler and numerical division are the only ways to find a midpoint, not realizing that properties of symmetry (like folding or compass arcs) create the same result without needing to know the "number."

If I can’t actually measure the long side because it’s too big, how am I supposed to find that one spot that’s the exact middle and make sure my vertical line is perfectly square with the edge without just 'winging it'?

misconceptions.description: Believing that "eyeballing" the center point is accurate enough for geometric properties, ignoring that manual estimation lacks the mathematical precision of a construction.

misconceptions.title: Additive Misunderstanding of Bisectors
misconceptions.description: Assuming that any line crossing a segment "splits" it, failing to recognize that a perpendicular bisector must meet specific criteria (equidistance from endpoints) to be truly centered and upright.

misconceptions.title: Tool Dependency
misconceptions.description: Thinking that a ruler and numerical division are the only ways to find a midpoint, not realizing that properties of symmetry (like folding or compass arcs) create the same result without needing to know the "number."

sections.patternRecognition: "Wait—whenever we fold the corners together, does that crease always land in the same spot every time? And like, if we folded it differently, would the line still be perfectly straight up and down, or is there something special about matching those two specific ends together?"
sections.generalization: So, like... do you think if we fold ANY two points onto each other, the crease is ALWAYS going to be a perfect 90-degree angle to the edge? Or is there a way we could fold it where the line ends up slanted but it still technically hits the middle?
sections.inference: Okay, so I just saw a video of a guy doing this with a piece of string and a pencil instead of folding—he called it a 'compass' thing. He drew two big curves from the ends of the banner and where they crossed, he drew his line. It looked exactly like our crease! For a 10-foot banner, he opened the string to 7 feet and it worked. But for a 4-foot banner, he only opened it to 3 feet. Do you think that same trick works for any size banner we get? And like, is there a way to know exactly how wide to open the string for a 20-foot one without just guessing and checking until the curves hit each other?

hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-debt.title: The Square Root of a Debt
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-debt.standardDescription: Know there is a complex number $i$ such that $i^2 = -1$, and every complex number has the form $a + bi$ with $a$ and $b$ real.
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-debt.description: Teach Zippy about The Square Root of a Debt
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-debt.teachingPrompt: Know there is a complex number $i$ such that $i^2 = -1$, and every complex number has the form $a + bi$ with $a$ and $b$ real.
targetConcepts: The student can define $i$ as the number whose square is -1.
The student can represent the square root of any negative real number in the form $bi$ and combine it with real numbers to form $a + bi$.
misconceptions: The Double Negative Trap (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): The student assumes that since $-4 \times -4 = 16$, the square root of -16 must simply be -4.
The Absolute Value Escape (surface_feature_matching): The student ignores the negative sign entirely and claims the side length is 4, assuming the negative was a typo.

Dimensional Non-Existence (partial_concept_model): The student concludes that because the number cannot be placed on a standard horizontal number line, it is "not a number" and has no mathematical properties.

misconceptions.title: The Absolute Value Escape

misconceptions.title: Dimensional Non-Existence

If we imagine a new kind of number, let's call it $i$, that is specifically designed to be the square root of -1, what happens to the -16?
When you multiply a "regular" number like 4 by this new $i$, does the result still behave like a number you can add to 5 or 10?
How does the "real" part of a number and this new "imaginary" part connect when we try to write them down as a single position?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I have any negative number at all, does that mean I can ALWAYS write its square root as a combination of a normal number and this $i$ thing?

hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-glitch.title: The Square Root Glitch
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-glitch.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-glitch.standardDescription: Know there is a complex number i such that i^2 = -1, and every complex number has the form a + bi with a and b real.
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-glitch.description: Teach Zippy about The Square Root Glitch
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-glitch.problemStatement: I was trying to solve this equation on my calculator and got a 'Non-real calculation' error when I tried to find $\sqrt{-16}$, but then my friend said there’s this 'imaginary' number $i$ that lets us actually write an answer for it. He says $\sqrt{-16}$ is just $4i$, but I’m confused how you’re supposed to write a final answer if you have a regular number like 5 and this $4i$ thing together—do they just have to sit next to each other like $5 + 4i$ forever?
hsn-cn.a.1_square-root-glitch.teachingPrompt: Know there is a complex number i such that i^2 = -1, and every complex number has the form a + bi with a and b real.
targetConcepts: Students can identify $i$ as the unit such that $i^2 = -1$.
Students can express any number (real or imaginary) in the standard complex form $a + bi$.
misconceptions: Combining Unlike Terms (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students may try to simplify $5 + 4i$ into $9i$ or $9$, treating the real and imaginary parts as like terms that can be added together.
Negative Root Confusion (whole_number_bias): Students might assume that $\sqrt{-16}$ should be $-4$ because they are applying the logic of negative integers to the operation of square roots.
Variable Misinterpretation (surface_feature_matching): Students may treat $i$ as an unknown variable (like $x$) that needs to be solved for, rather than a defined constant where $i^2 = -1$.

He says $\sqrt{-16}$ is just $4i$, but I’m confused how you’re supposed to write a final answer if you have a regular number like 5 and this $4i$ thing together—do they just have to sit next to each other like $5 + 4i$ forever?

misconceptions.title: Combining Unlike Terms
misconceptions.description: Students may try to simplify $5 + 4i$ into $9i$ or $9$, treating the real and imaginary parts as like terms that can be added together.

misconceptions.title: Negative Root Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students might assume that $\sqrt{-16}$ should be $-4$ because they are applying the logic of negative integers to the operation of square roots.

misconceptions.title: Variable Misinterpretation
misconceptions.description: Students may treat $i$ as an unknown variable (like $x$) that needs to be solved for, rather than a defined constant where $i^2 = -1$.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you treat $i$ exactly like the variable $x$ in algebra, how would you normally write $5 + 4x$?"
"Wait—if $i^2$ is exactly $-1$, what happens to the 'imaginary' part of the number if you ever end up squaring it?"
Does the 'real' part of the number ever actually merge with the $i$ part when you're just adding them?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that every single number we've ever used—even plain old 7—could actually be written in that $a + bi$ form if we just let $b$ be zero... is that always true, or are 'complex' numbers a totally separate thing from 'real' numbers?
sections.inference: I found this other weird expression where you have $\sqrt{-25} + \sqrt{-9}$. If we use my friend's trick, that would be $5i + 3i$, which feels like it should just be $8i$. But what if we had to add a regular 10 to that—is there any way to squish all three parts into a single number, or are we stuck with a 'real' part and an 'imaginary' part in the final result?

hsn-cn.a.2_i-squared-shortcut.title: The i-Squared Shortcut
hsn-cn.a.2_i-squared-shortcut.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.a.2_i-squared-shortcut.standardDescription: Use the relation i^2 = -1 and the commutative, associative, and distributive properties to add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers.
hsn-cn.a.2_i-squared-shortcut.description: Teach Zippy about The i-Squared Shortcut
hsn-cn.a.2_i-squared-shortcut.problemStatement: I was trying to multiply $(3 + 2i)$ by $(1 + 4i)$ and it felt just like multiplying $(3 + 2x)$ and $(1 + 4x)$ where I got four different parts, but then my teacher said the $i$ version can actually be simplified even further into just two parts. If we know that $i^2$ is exactly the same as $-1$, how does that change the 'messy' middle of the problem into something that actually looks like a normal number again?
hsn-cn.a.2_i-squared-shortcut.teachingPrompt: Use the relation i^2 = -1 and the commutative, associative, and distributive properties to add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers.
targetConcepts: Students can apply the distributive property (FOIL) to complex numbers and correctly substitute $-1$ for $i^2$.
Students can simplify the sum and product of complex numbers into the standard form $a + bi$ by grouping like terms.
misconceptions: Variable Trap (procedural_overgeneralization): Students treat $i$ as an unknown variable and leave the answer as $3 + 14i + 8i^2$, failing to apply the specific numerical identity of $i^2$.
The Imaginary Wall (partial_concept_model): Students believe they can only add real numbers to real numbers and imaginary to imaginary, so they get stuck when a multiplication creates an $i^2$ term.

Sign Flip Oversight (surface_feature_matching): Students replace $i^2$ with $1$ instead of $-1$, leading to an incorrect constant term because they miss the foundational definition of the imaginary unit.

sections.studentPrompt: I was trying to multiply $(3 + 2i)$ by $(1 + 4i)$ and it felt just like multiplying $(3 + 2x)$ and $(1 + 4x)$ where I got four different parts, but then my teacher said the $i$ version can actually be simplified even further into just two parts. If we know that $i^2$ is exactly the same as $-1$, how does that change the 'messy' middle of the problem into something that actually looks like a normal number again?

misconceptions.title: Variable Trap
misconceptions.description: Students treat $i$ as an unknown variable and leave the answer as $3 + 14i + 8i^2$, failing to apply the specific numerical identity of $i^2$.

misconceptions.title: The Imaginary Wall
misconceptions.description: Students believe they can only add real numbers to real numbers and imaginary to imaginary, so they get stuck when a multiplication creates an $i^2$ term.

misconceptions.title: Sign Flip Oversight
misconceptions.description: Students replace $i^2$ with $1$ instead of $-1$, leading to an incorrect constant term because they miss the foundational definition of the imaginary unit.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you multiply those two terms and get an $i^2$, does that part of the number stay 'imaginary' or does it jump over to the 'real' side?"
"Wait—if you have a bunch of $i$ terms and a bunch of regular numbers, which ones are allowed to hang out together in the final answer?"
Does multiplying two complex numbers always end up with that same $a + bi$ look, or can it turn into something completely different?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you multiply any two complex numbers together, the $i$ part will ALWAYS stay there and you can never get a plain old real number as the final answer... or is there a specific pair where the $i$ just completely vanishes?
sections.inference: I found this other pattern where if you multiply $(5 + 2i)$ by $(5 - 2i)$, something really weird happens to the middle terms. If we use that same $i^2 = -1$ trick here, do you think the final answer will still have an $i$ in it, or is there a way to predict which pairs will turn into a 'perfect' real number without any imaginary parts left?

hsn-cn.a.3_vanishing-imaginary.title: The Vanishing Imaginary
hsn-cn.a.3_vanishing-imaginary.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.a.3_vanishing-imaginary.standardDescription: (+) Find the conjugate of a complex number; use conjugates to find moduli and quotients of complex numbers.
hsn-cn.a.3_vanishing-imaginary.description: Teach Zippy about The Vanishing Imaginary
hsn-cn.a.3_vanishing-imaginary.problemStatement: I'm trying to divide $(10 + 5i)$ by $(2 + i)$, but I'm totally stuck because you can't just divide across like a normal fraction when there's an $i$ in the denominator. My friend told me there’s a 'partner' number for $(2 + i)$ that, when you multiply them together, makes the $i$ completely disappear and turns the bottom into a plain old whole number. If we want to get that $i$ out of the basement so we can actually finish the division, what would that 'partner' number have to look like?
hsn-cn.a.3_vanishing-imaginary.teachingPrompt: (+) Find the conjugate of a complex number; use conjugates to find moduli and quotients of complex numbers.
targetConcepts: Students can identify and create the complex conjugate of any given complex number.
Students can use the product of conjugates to rationalize denominators and calculate the modulus of a complex number.
misconceptions: Simple Sign Flip (surface_feature_matching): Students might think the "partner" (conjugate) of $2+i$ is $-2-i$, changing both signs instead of just the imaginary part, which doesn't eliminate the $i$ term in the product.

Additive Inverse Confusion (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students may try to multiply by the reciprocal $1/(2+i)$ or subtract $i$ to "remove" it, not realizing they need a multiplicative strategy that results in a real number.

The i-Squared Sign Error (procedural_overgeneralization): During the multiplication $(2+i)(2-i)$, students often calculate $+i^2$ as $+1$ or forget to change the sign to $-(-1)$, resulting in a 3 instead of 5 in the denominator.

sections.studentPrompt: I'm trying to divide $(10 + 5i)$ by $(2 + i)$, but I'm totally stuck because you can't just divide across like a normal fraction when there's an $i$ in the denominator. My friend told me there’s a 'partner' number for $(2 + i)$ that, when you multiply them together, makes the $i$ completely disappear and turns the bottom into a plain old whole number. If we want to get that $i$ out of the basement so we can actually finish the division, what would that 'partner' number have to look like?

misconceptions.title: Simple Sign Flip
misconceptions.description: Students might think the "partner" (conjugate) of $2+i$ is $-2-i$, changing both signs instead of just the imaginary part, which doesn't eliminate the $i$ term in the product.

misconceptions.title: Additive Inverse Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may try to multiply by the reciprocal $1/(2+i)$ or subtract $i$ to "remove" it, not realizing they need a multiplicative strategy that results in a real number.

misconceptions.title: The i-Squared Sign Error
misconceptions.description: During the multiplication $(2+i)(2-i)$, students often calculate $+i^2$ as $+1$ or forget to change the sign to $-(-1)$, resulting in a 3 instead of 5 in the denominator.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you multiply $(2+i)$ by itself, the $i$ stays there, but what happens to the middle terms if you multiply $(2+i)$ by $(2-i)$ instead?"
"Does changing the sign of just the 'imaginary part' always make the $i$ terms cancel each other out?"
Wait—if the bottom of our fraction becomes a regular number like 5, does the top part stay 'imaginary' or does it change too?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that for ANY complex number $a+bi$, if you multiply it by its 'opposite-sign partner' $a-bi$, the result will ALWAYS be a positive real number... or is there a weird case where you could still end up with an $i$ in your answer?
sections.inference: I found this other problem where I have to find the 'size' or distance of $(3 + 4i)$ from zero. My teacher called it the 'modulus' and said it's related to that same 'partner-number' trick we used for division. If multiplying $(3+4i)$ by its partner $(3-4i)$ gives us a perfect square like 25, do you think there's a way to find the distance without even drawing a graph?

hsn-cn.a_square-root-glitch.title: The Square Root Glitch
hsn-cn.a_square-root-glitch.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.a_square-root-glitch.standardDescription: Perform arithmetic operations with complex numbers.
hsn-cn.a_square-root-glitch.description: Teach Zippy about The Square Root Glitch
hsn-cn.a_square-root-glitch.problemStatement: I was trying to solve a quadratic equation and ended up with $\sqrt{-9}$, which my calculator usually says is an error, but then my friend showed me this 'imaginary' number $i$ where $i^2 = -1$. If we decide that $\sqrt{-9}$ is just $3i$, how are we supposed to add that to a regular number like 4, or do they just have to sit next to each other forever like $4 + 3i$?
hsn-cn.a_square-root-glitch.teachingPrompt: Perform arithmetic operations with complex numbers.
targetConcepts: Students can perform addition, subtraction, and multiplication of complex numbers by treating $i$ as a variable and applying the property $i^2 = -1$.
Students can justify why the sum or product of two complex numbers results in another complex number (closure).
misconceptions: Combining Unlike Terms (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students may try to simplify $4 + 3i$ into $7i$ or $7$, treating the real and imaginary parts as like terms that can be added numerically.

The $i^2$ Sign Error (procedural_overgeneralization): When multiplying complex numbers, students often replace $i^2$ with $1$ instead of $-1$, forgetting that the definition of $i$ forces a sign change.
Square Root of Negatives (whole_number_bias): Students might assume $\sqrt{-9}$ is $-3$, applying the rules of negative integers to the operation of square roots.

sections.studentPrompt: I was trying to solve a quadratic equation and ended up with $\sqrt{-9}$, which my calculator usually says is an error, but then my friend showed me this 'imaginary' number $i$ where $i^2 = -1$. If we decide that $\sqrt{-9}$ is just $3i$, how are we supposed to add that to a regular number like 4, or do they just have to sit next to each other forever like $4 + 3i$?

misconceptions.title: Combining Unlike Terms
misconceptions.description: Students may try to simplify $4 + 3i$ into $7i$ or $7$, treating the real and imaginary parts as like terms that can be added numerically.

misconceptions.title: The $i^2$ Sign Error
misconceptions.description: When multiplying complex numbers, students often replace $i^2$ with $1$ instead of $-1$, forgetting that the definition of $i$ forces a sign change.

misconceptions.title: Square Root of Negatives
misconceptions.description: Students might assume $\sqrt{-9}$ is $-3$, applying the rules of negative integers to the operation of square roots.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you treat $i$ exactly like the variable $x$, how would you usually combine $4 + 3x$?"
"Wait—if $i^2$ is $-1$, what happens to that 'imaginary' part of the number when you multiply $i$ by itself?"
Does the 'real' part of the number ever get mixed into the 'imaginary' part when you're just adding them up?
sections.generalization: I have this hunch that if you multiply two of these 'complex' numbers together, the $i$ part will ALWAYS eventually disappear and leave you with just a normal, real number... or is that only for special pairs?
sections.inference: I found another one where you multiply $(2 + i)$ by $(2 - i)$. If we use the same 'FOIL' trick we use for $(x+1)(x-1)$, we get a middle part that cancels out and an $i^2$ at the end. Since $i^2$ is just $-1$, do you think the final answer to this multiplication ends up being a plain old integer, and could we find another pair that does the same thing?

hsn-cn.b.4_compass-and-grid.title: The Compass and the Grid
hsn-cn.b.4_compass-and-grid.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.b.4_compass-and-grid.standardDescription: (+) Represent complex numbers in rectangular and polar form (including real and imaginary numbers), and explain why the rectangular and polar forms of a given complex number represent the same number.
hsn-cn.b.4_compass-and-grid.description: Teach Zippy about The Compass and the Grid
hsn-cn.b.4_compass-and-grid.problemStatement: I found two different ways to describe where the point $3 + 3i$ sits on a map—one person says to just go '3 right and 3 up,' but another person says you just have to turn $45^\circ$ and walk a certain distance straight from the center. If they both end up at the exact same physical dot on the paper, is there a way to describe that 'walking distance' using the numbers we already have?
hsn-cn.b.4_compass-and-grid.teachingPrompt: (+) Represent complex numbers in rectangular and polar form (including real and imaginary numbers), and explain why the rectangular and polar forms of a given complex number represent the same number.
misconceptions: Angle-Only Definition (partial_concept_model): Students might think the angle ($45^\circ$) is enough to define the number, forgetting that infinite points exist along that same line at different distances.
Component Addition (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may believe the distance from the center is simply $3 + 3 = 6$ instead of using the Pythagorean relationship.

Rectangular-Polar Disconnect (surface_feature_matching): Students might view $3 + 3i$ and its polar equivalent as two different numerical values rather than two different "addressing systems" for the same spot.

sections.studentPrompt: I found two different ways to describe where the point $3 + 3i$ sits on a map—one person says to just go '3 right and 3 up,' but another person says you just have to turn $45^\circ$ and walk a certain distance straight from the center. If they both end up at the exact same physical dot on the paper, is there a way to describe that 'walking distance' using the numbers we already have?

misconceptions.title: Angle-Only Definition
misconceptions.description: Students might think the angle ($45^\circ$) is enough to define the number, forgetting that infinite points exist along that same line at different distances.

misconceptions.title: Component Addition
misconceptions.description: Students may believe the distance from the center is simply $3 + 3 = 6$ instead of using the Pythagorean relationship.

misconceptions.title: Rectangular-Polar Disconnect
misconceptions.description: Students might view $3 + 3i$ and its polar equivalent as two different numerical values rather than two different "addressing systems" for the same spot.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you look at the 'right 3' and 'up 3' moves, what kind of shape do they make with that straight line from the center?"
"Wait—does the angle you have to turn change if the 'right' and 'up'

hsn-cn.b.4_the-radar-signal.title: The Radar Signal
hsn-cn.b.4_the-radar-signal.standardDescription: Represent complex numbers on the complex plane in rectangular and polar form (including real and imaginary numbers), and explain why the rectangular and polar forms of a given complex number represent the same number.
hsn-cn.b.4_the-radar-signal.description: Teach Zippy about The Radar Signal
hsn-cn.b.4_the-radar-signal.teachingPrompt: Represent complex numbers on the complex plane in rectangular and polar form (including real and imaginary numbers), and explain why the rectangular and polar forms of a given complex number represent the same number.
targetConcepts: The student can convert a complex number from rectangular form ($a + bi$) to polar form ($r(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)$).
The student can justify why two different mathematical representations (grid coordinates vs. magnitude and direction) identify the same point on the complex plane.
misconceptions: Category Mismatch (structural_misunderstanding): The student believes that a "rectangular" address and a "polar" address describe two different physical locations rather than two ways to label one point.
The Addition Trap (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student tries to find the distance by adding the coordinates ($3 + 4 = 7$) instead of using the geometric distance from the origin.

Angle-Axis Confusion (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student assumes the angle is measured from the vertical imaginary axis instead of the horizontal real axis, leading to a "flipped" version of the point.

sections.studentPrompt: 5 units away at a $53^\circ$ angle.

misconceptions.title: Category Mismatch
misconceptions.description: The student believes that a "rectangular" address and a "polar" address describe two different physical locations rather than two ways to label one point.

misconceptions.title: The Addition Trap
misconceptions.description: The student tries to find the distance by adding the coordinates ($3 + 4 = 7$) instead of using the geometric distance from the origin.

misconceptions.title: Angle-Axis Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student assumes the angle is measured from the vertical imaginary axis instead of the horizontal real axis, leading to a "flipped" version of the point.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we moved the dot further to the right on the grid, what would have to happen to that "5 units away" distance to keep up?
Does the angle of the turn ever actually change if we keep the dot on the same diagonal line but just slide it further out?
How does the "3" and the "4" on the grid connect to the "5" that the operator mentioned?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I know the 'left-right' and 'up-down' steps for any complex number, does that mean I can ALWAYS use them to figure out exactly how far it is from the center and what angle it’s pointing at?
sections.inference: about 4.2 units away.

hsn-cn.b.5_vector-voyager.title: The Vector Voyager
hsn-cn.b.5_vector-voyager.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.b.5_vector-voyager.standardDescription: (+) Represent addition, subtraction, multiplication, and conjugation of complex numbers geometrically on the complex plane; use properties of this representation for computation. For example, (-1 + √3 i)^3 = 8 because (-1 + √3 i) has modulus 2 and argument 120°.
hsn-cn.b.5_vector-voyager.description: Teach Zippy about The Vector Voyager
hsn-cn.b.5_vector-voyager.problemStatement: I noticed something weird when I was plotting complex numbers—if I have a point like $1 + i$ and I multiply it by itself over and over, the point doesn't just move in a straight line; it starts spiraling away from the center and turning at the same time. If $1 + i$ is sitting at a $45^\circ$ angle and is about $1.41$ units from the center, what is actually happening to that 'angle' and 'distance' every time we multiply by that same number again?
hsn-cn.b.5_vector-voyager.teachingPrompt: (+) Represent addition, subtraction, multiplication, and conjugation of complex numbers geometrically on the complex plane; use properties of this representation for computation. For example, (-1 + √3 i)^3 = 8 because (-1 + √3 i) has modulus 2 and argument 120°.
targetConcepts: Students can interpret complex multiplication geometrically as a combination of rotation (adding arguments) and dilation (multiplying moduli).
Students can use geometric properties of complex numbers to simplify powers of complex numbers (De Moivre’s Theorem logic).
misconceptions: Additive Angle Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students might think that multiplying by a number with a $45^\circ$ angle means the new angle is $45 \times 45 = 2025^\circ$ instead of adding the angles ($45 + 45 = 90^\circ$).

Fixed Distance Assumption (partial_concept_model): Students may assume the point only rotates and stays the same distance from the origin, failing to realize the modulus also multiplies (scaling the distance).

Linear Path Bias (surface_feature_matching): Students might expect the points to follow a linear pattern (like $2+2i, 3+3i, 4+4i$) rather than the geometric progression inherent in complex multiplication.

sections.studentPrompt: I noticed something weird when I was plotting complex numbers—if I have a point like $1 + i$ and I multiply it by itself over and over, the point doesn't just move in a straight line; it starts spiraling away from the center and turning at the same time. If $1 + i$ is sitting at a $45^\circ$ angle and is about $1.41$ units from the center, what is actually happening to that 'angle' and 'distance' every time we multiply by that same number again?

misconceptions.title: Additive Angle Error
misconceptions.description: Students might think that multiplying by a number with a $45^\circ$ angle means the new angle is $45 \times 45 = 2025^\circ$ instead of adding the angles ($45 + 45 = 90^\circ$).

misconceptions.title: Fixed Distance Assumption
misconceptions.description: Students may assume the point only rotates and stays the same distance from the origin, failing to realize the modulus also multiplies (scaling the distance).

misconceptions.title: Linear Path Bias
misconceptions.description: Students might expect the points to follow a linear pattern (like $2+2i, 3+3i, 4+4i$) rather than the geometric progression inherent in complex multiplication.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you multiply a distance of $1.41$ by another $1.41$, does the new point end up closer to the center or further away?"
"Wait—if each multiplication 'adds' another $45^\circ$ turn, how many times do you have to multiply before the point lands exactly on the vertical $i$-axis?"
Does the 'turning' part of the movement ever stop, or does it just keep spinning around the circle as the number gets bigger?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you multiply two complex numbers, you ALWAYS just add their angles together and multiply their distances from the center... or is there a case where the angles do something totally different?
sections.inference: I found this specific number $z = -1 + \sqrt{3}i$ and I noticed its distance from the center is exactly 2 and its angle is $120^\circ$. If we multiply this number by itself three times ($z^3$), the angle would be $120 + 120 + 120$. Do you think the final answer will end up being a 'boring' regular number on the flat horizontal line, and how far from the center would it be?

hsn-cn.b.6_midpoint-meeting.title: The Midpoint Meeting
hsn-cn.b.6_midpoint-meeting.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.b.6_midpoint-meeting.standardDescription: (+) Calculate the distance between numbers in the complex plane as the modulus of the difference, and the midpoint of a segment as the average of the numbers at its endpoints.
hsn-cn.b.6_midpoint-meeting.description: Teach Zippy about The Midpoint Meeting
hsn-cn.b.6_midpoint-meeting.problemStatement: I’m trying to find the exact middle spot between two points on this complex map—one is at $2 + 8i$ and the other is way down at $10 - 2i$. My friend says I should just 'average' them like they're normal numbers, but I'm worried that adding them together and dividing by two might give me a point that isn't even on the line between them. If we want to find the dead-center point and then figure out exactly how far apart the two original points are, how do we even start that without just using a ruler?
hsn-cn.b.6_midpoint-meeting.teachingPrompt: (+) Calculate the distance between numbers in the complex plane as the modulus of the difference, and the midpoint of a segment as the average of the numbers at its endpoints.
targetConcepts: Students can calculate the midpoint of two complex numbers by finding the average of their real and imaginary components.
Students can determine the distance between two complex numbers by calculating the modulus of their difference.
misconceptions: Independent Averaging Error (partial_concept_model): Students might try to average only the real parts or only the imaginary parts, failing to realize the midpoint is a single complex number $(a+bi)$ that requires averaging both components.

Distance vs. Difference (surface_feature_matching): Students often subtract the two numbers $(10-2i) - (2+8i)$ and stop there, presenting a complex number as the "distance" instead of calculating the modulus (a single real value).

Sign Flip in Subtraction (procedural_overgeneralization): When finding the difference to calculate distance, students frequently forget to distribute the negative sign across both the real and imaginary parts of the second number.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m trying to find the exact middle spot between two points on this complex map—one is at $2 + 8i$ and the other is way down at $10 - 2i$. My friend says I should just 'average' them like they're normal numbers, but I'm worried that adding them together and dividing by two might give me a point that isn't even on the line between them. If we want to find the dead-center point and then figure out exactly how far apart the two original points are, how do we even start that without just using a ruler?

misconceptions.title: Independent Averaging Error
misconceptions.description: Students might try to average only the real parts or only the imaginary parts, failing to realize the midpoint is a single complex number $(a+bi)$ that requires averaging both components.

misconceptions.title: Distance vs. Difference
misconceptions.description: Students often subtract the two numbers $(10-2i) - (2+8i)$ and stop there, presenting a complex number as the "distance" instead of calculating the modulus (a single real value).

misconceptions.title: Sign Flip in Subtraction
misconceptions.description: When finding the difference to calculate distance, students frequently forget to distribute the negative sign across both the real and imaginary parts of the second number.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you just look at the 'real' numbers 2 and 10, where is their middle? Now look at the $8i$ and $-2i$—where is their middle?"
"Wait—if the distance is the 'length' of the jump between them, does it matter if you jump from A to B or B to A?"
Does the 'average' point you found actually look like it sits right on the diagonal line connecting the two spots on the grid?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you take any two complex numbers, the 'average' is ALWAYS going to be the exact halfway point on the map... or is there a weird case where averaging them sends you to a totally different part of the grid?
sections.inference: I found another pair of points at $1 + i$ and $7 + 9i$. If we use that same averaging trick, the middle should be at $4 + 5i$. Now I'm wondering about the distance—the 'gap' between them is $6$ units across and $8$ units up. Do you think the distance is just $6 + 8 = 14$, or is there a way to use that $6$ and $8$ to find the 'straight-line' distance like we do with triangles?

hsn-cn.b_hidden-map.title: The Hidden Map
hsn-cn.b_hidden-map.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.b_hidden-map.standardDescription: Represent complex numbers and their operations on the complex plane.
hsn-cn.b_hidden-map.description: Teach Zippy about The Hidden Map
hsn-cn.b_hidden-map.problemStatement: I found this old math puzzle that says complex numbers like $3 + 2i$ aren't just symbols on a page, but actually live on a 'hidden' map where the regular numbers go left-to-right and the $i$ numbers go up-and-down. If $3 + 2i$ is just a single point on this grid, what happens to that point's location if we add another number like $1 - 4i$ to it?
hsn-cn.b_hidden-map.teachingPrompt: Represent complex numbers and their operations on the complex plane.
targetConcepts: Students can represent complex numbers as points or vectors in the complex plane.
Students can interpret the addition of complex numbers geometrically as a translation or vector sum in the plane.
misconceptions: One-Dimensional Thinking (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students may try to plot the number on a standard number line by adding the coefficients (e.g., $3 + 2 = 5$) and ignoring the directional nature of $i$.

Origin-Anchor Confusion (partial_concept_model): Students might think the "plus" sign in $3 + 2i$ means you start at 3 and move 2 units, but fail to see the resulting $a + bi$ as a single coordinate $(a, b)$.

Scalar Addition Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may treat the addition of $1 - 4i$ as a scaling factor (stretching the point) rather than a translation (sliding the point) across the plane.

sections.studentPrompt: I found this old math puzzle that says complex numbers like $3 + 2i$ aren't just symbols on a page, but actually live on a 'hidden' map where the regular numbers go left-to-right and the $i$ numbers go up-and-down. If $3 + 2i$ is just a single point on this grid, what happens to that point's location if we add another number like $1 - 4i$ to it?

misconceptions.title: One-Dimensional Thinking
misconceptions.description: Students may try to plot the number on a standard number line by adding the coefficients (e.g., $3 + 2 = 5$) and ignoring the directional nature of $i$.

misconceptions.title: Origin-Anchor Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students might think the "plus" sign in $3 + 2i$ means you start at 3 and move 2 units, but fail to see the resulting $a + bi$ as a single coordinate $(a, b)$.

misconceptions.title: Scalar Addition Error
misconceptions.description: Students may treat the addition of $1 - 4i$ as a scaling factor (stretching the point) rather than a translation (sliding the point) across the plane.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you only changed the 'real' part of the number, which way would the point on the map actually slide?"
"Wait—does the $i$ part of the number always control the vertical 'up and down' movement, or can it move sideways too?"
When you add two complex numbers together, is the new spot on the map just the result of two separate 'slides'?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that adding a complex number to another one is ALWAYS just like following a set of 'walking directions' (like 3 steps east, 2 steps north)... does that mean the order you add them in doesn't change where you end up on the map?
sections.inference: I noticed that if I have the number $2 + 2i$ and I multiply the whole thing by $-1$, the point jumps to the exact opposite side of the map at $-2 - 2i$. If multiplying by $-1$ is like a half-turn around the center, do you think there’s a way to figure out what multiplying by just $i$ does to the point's location without just guessing?

hsn-cn.c.7_floating-parabola.title: The Floating Parabola
hsn-cn.c.7_floating-parabola.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.c.7_floating-parabola.standardDescription: Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that have complex solutions.
hsn-cn.c.7_floating-parabola.description: Teach Zippy about The Floating Parabola
hsn-cn.c.7_floating-parabola.problemStatement: I was trying to find where the graph of $y = x^2 - 4x + 5$ hits the x-axis, but when I looked at the picture, the parabola just floats in the air and never actually touches the line. My teacher says that even though we can't see the 'hits,' we can still use the quadratic formula to find these hidden 'complex' answers. If the part inside the square root ends up being negative, how do we write the final two spots where this equation is supposed to equal zero?
hsn-cn.c.7_floating-parabola.teachingPrompt: Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that have complex solutions.
targetConcepts: Students can identify when a quadratic equation will have complex solutions based on the discriminant.
Students can express the solutions of a quadratic equation in the form $a \pm bi$.
misconceptions: The 'No Solution' Stop (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students may conclude the problem is impossible once they see a negative value under the radical, based on previous experience with real-number-only domains.

Sign Distribution Error (procedural_overgeneralization): When simplifying $\frac{4 \pm \sqrt{-4}}{2}$, students often divide only the 4 by the 2, or forget that the $\sqrt{-4}$ must also be simplified to $2i$ and then divided.

Ignoring the Conjugate Pair (partial_concept_model): Students might provide only one solution (e.g., $2 + i$) and forget the $\pm$ symbol, missing the fact that complex roots to real quadratics always come in pairs.

sections.studentPrompt: I was trying to find where the graph of $y = x^2 - 4x + 5$ hits the x-axis, but when I looked at the picture, the parabola just floats in the air and never actually touches the line. My teacher says that even though we can't see the 'hits,' we can still use the quadratic formula to find these hidden 'complex' answers. If the part inside the square root ends up being negative, how do we write the final two spots where this equation is supposed to equal zero?

misconceptions.title: The 'No Solution' Stop
misconceptions.description: Students may conclude the problem is impossible once they see a negative value under the radical, based on previous experience with real-number-only domains.

misconceptions.title: Sign Distribution Error
misconceptions.description: When simplifying $\frac{4 \pm \sqrt{-4}}{2}$, students often divide only the 4 by the 2, or forget that the $\sqrt{-4}$ must also be simplified to $2i$ and then divided.

misconceptions.title: Ignoring the Conjugate Pair
misconceptions.description: Students might provide only one solution (e.g., $2 + i$) and forget the $\pm$ symbol, missing the fact that complex roots to real quadratics always come in pairs.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you look at the graph, what does it tell you about whether our answers will be 'normal' numbers or those 'imaginary' $i$ numbers?"
"If you change the $+5$ in the equation to a $+3$, the graph suddenly drops and hits the axis twice—what changed inside the square root part of the formula?"
Does the 'real' part of the answer—the number in front of the $i$—match up with the center line of the parabola on the graph?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if the bottom of a parabola ( the vertex) is anywhere above the x-axis, the solutions you get from the formula are ALWAYS going to have an $i$ in them... or is there a way to have a floating graph with regular numbers as answers?
sections.inference: I found another equation, $x^2 + 100 = 0$, which is just a parabola shifted way up the graph. If we use the same logic, the answers should be $\pm\sqrt{-100}$, which is just $+10i$ and $-10i$. Since there isn't a 'middle' part to this equation, do you think every 'floating' parabola has two answers that are just opposites, or is that only when the graph is centered perfectly on the y-axis?

hsn-cn.c.7_the-floating-parabola.title: The Floating Parabola
hsn-cn.c.7_the-floating-parabola.standardDescription: Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that have complex solutions.
hsn-cn.c.7_the-floating-parabola.description: Teach Zippy about The Floating Parabola
hsn-cn.c.7_the-floating-parabola.problemStatement: I was trying to find where the path of this ball hits the ground using the equation $x^2 - 4x + 5 = 0$, but when I looked at the graph, the curve just floats in the air and never actually touches the x-axis. Does that mean the equation is impossible to solve, or are the answers just hiding somewhere we can't see?
hsn-cn.c.7_the-floating-parabola.teachingPrompt: Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that have complex solutions.
targetConcepts: The student can solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that result in complex solutions using the quadratic formula.
The student can relate the lack of real x-intercepts on a graph to the presence of complex roots.
misconceptions: No-Intercept Equals No-Solution (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student believes that if a graph has no x-intercepts, the corresponding algebraic equation has no solutions of any kind.
Square Root of Negative Error (procedural_overgeneralization): The student reaches the $\sqrt{-4}$ step in the quadratic formula and stops, assuming the negative sign is a mathematical dead end.

Real Coefficient Assumption (partial_concept_model): The student assumes that because the numbers in the equation (1, -4, 5) are "real," the answers must also be real numbers on the standard number line.

sections.studentPrompt: I was trying to find where the path of this ball hits the ground using the equation $x^2 - 4x + 5 = 0$, but when I looked at the graph, the curve just floats in the air and never actually touches the x-axis. Does that mean the equation is impossible to solve, or are the answers just hiding somewhere we can't see?

misconceptions.title: No-Intercept Equals No-Solution
misconceptions.description: The student believes that if a graph has no x-intercepts, the corresponding algebraic equation has no solutions of any kind.

misconceptions.title: Square Root of Negative Error
misconceptions.description: The student reaches the $\sqrt{-4}$ step in the quadratic formula and stops, assuming the negative sign is a mathematical dead end.

misconceptions.title: Real Coefficient Assumption
misconceptions.description: The student assumes that because the numbers in the equation (1, -4, 5) are "real," the answers must also be real numbers on the standard number line.

sections.patternRecognition:
If we shifted the whole graph down by 2 units so it finally touched the axis, how would that change the "stuff" inside the square root in our formula?
When the graph is "floating," what do you notice happens to the discriminant compared to when the graph hits the ground twice?
How does the "center" of the floating curve connect to the real part of the complex numbers we get as answers?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if a parabola never touches the x-axis, does that mean the two solutions are ALWAYS going to be a matching pair of complex numbers that look exactly the same except for the plus or minus sign in the middle?
sections.inference: hidden

hsn-cn.c.8_sum-of-squares-secret.title: The Sum of Squares Secret
hsn-cn.c.8_sum-of-squares-secret.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.c.8_sum-of-squares-secret.standardDescription: (+) Extend polynomial identities to the complex numbers. For example, rewrite x^2 + y^2 as (x + iy)(x - iy).
hsn-cn.c.8_sum-of-squares-secret.description: Teach Zippy about The Sum of Squares Secret
hsn-cn.c.8_sum-of-squares-secret.problemStatement: I remember learning that you can always factor the 'Difference of Squares' like $x^2 - 9$ into $(x-3)(x+3)$, but my teacher always said the 'Sum of Squares' like $x^2 + 9$ was impossible to break apart. Now that we know about the imaginary number $i$, my friend claims there’s actually a way to factor the plus-sign version too. If we want the middle terms to cancel out but still end up with a positive 9 at the end, what would the two factors have to look like?
hsn-cn.c.8_sum-of-squares-secret.teachingPrompt: (+) Extend polynomial identities to the complex numbers. For example, rewrite x^2 + y^2 as (x + iy)(x - iy).
targetConcepts: Students can extend the difference of squares identity to factor sums of squares using complex conjugates.
Students can demonstrate that polynomial identities hold true over the complex number system by expanding complex factors.
misconceptions: Real Number Factor Bias (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students may try to use $(x+3)(x+3)$, failing to realize this creates a $6x$ middle term and a positive 9.
The i-Squared Sign Error (procedural_overgeneralization): Students might write $(x-3i)(x-3i)$, not realizing that $-3i \times -3i$ results in $9i^2$, which is $-9$.
Difference of Squares Habit (surface_feature_matching): Students may simply write $(x-3)(x+3)$ out of habit, ignoring that the product of these is $x^2 - 9$, not $x^2 + 9$.

sections.studentPrompt: I remember learning that you can always factor the 'Difference of Squares' like $x^2 - 9$ into $(x-3)(x+3)$, but my teacher always said the 'Sum of Squares' like $x^2 + 9$ was impossible to break apart. Now that we know about the imaginary number $i$, my friend claims there’s actually a way to factor the plus-sign version too. If we want the middle terms to cancel out but still end up with a positive 9 at the end, what would the two factors have to look like?

misconceptions.title: Real Number Factor Bias
misconceptions.description: Students may try to use $(x+3)(x+3)$, failing to realize this creates a $6x$ middle term and a positive 9.

misconceptions.title: The i-Squared Sign Error
misconceptions.description: Students might write $(x-3i)(x-3i)$, not realizing that $-3i \times -3i$ results in $9i^2$, which is $-9$.

misconceptions.title: Difference of Squares Habit
misconceptions.description: Students may simply write $(x-3)(x+3)$ out of habit, ignoring that the product of these is $x^2 - 9$, not $x^2 + 9$.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you multiply $(x - 3i)$ and $(x + 3i)$, what happens to those 'imaginary' $x$ terms in the middle?"
"Wait—if $i^2$ is exactly $-1$, what does that do to the final number at the end of the multiplication?"
Does the 'plus-sign' in $x^2 + 9$ actually come from a 'minus' times an 'imaginary minus'?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you have ANY sum of two squares like $a^2 + b^2$, you can ALWAYS just use the 'partner' numbers $(a + bi)$ and $(a - bi)$ to break it apart perfectly... or is there a case where adding an $i$ doesn't actually fix the plus sign?
sections.inference: I found another expression, $x^2 + 25$, and using the $i$ trick, it factored into $(x + 5i)(x - 5i)$. If that works, do you think we could use this to factor something even weirder like $4x^2 + 49$? And is there a way to tell just by looking at a polynomial if we’re going to need 'imaginary' parts to break it down?

hsn-cn.c.9_missing-hits-mystery.title: The Missing Hits Mystery
hsn-cn.c.9_missing-hits-mystery.domain: Number and Quantity: The Complex Number System
hsn-cn.c.9_missing-hits-mystery.standardDescription: (+) Know the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; show that it is true for quadratic polynomials.
hsn-cn.c.9_missing-hits-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Missing Hits Mystery
hsn-cn.c.9_missing-hits-mystery.problemStatement: I was looking at graphs of equations like $y = x^2 - 4x + 5$ and $y = x^2 - 4x + 4$, and it’s weird because the first one never touches the x-axis at all, while the second one just barely taps it in one spot. My teacher says there’s this 'Fundamental Theorem' that claims every $x^2$ equation has to have exactly two solutions. If we can only see one 'tap' or no 'hits' at all on the graph, where are those two promised answers hiding?
hsn-cn.c.9_missing-hits-mystery.teachingPrompt: (+) Know the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; show that it is true for quadratic polynomials.
targetConcepts: Students can show that quadratic polynomials always have two complex roots (counting multiplicity) by applying the quadratic formula or factoring.
Students can relate the degree of a polynomial to the total number of solutions as defined by the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.
misconceptions: Visual-Only Validation (surface_feature_matching): Students believe that if a graph does not cross the x-axis, the equation has zero solutions, failing to account for the complex number system.
Single-Root Assumption (partial_concept_model): When a parabola's vertex sits exactly on the x-axis, students identify only one solution, missing the concept of a "double root" or multiplicity.

Linear Logic Overgeneralization (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students assume that because $x + 5 = 0$ has one answer, $x^2 + 5 = 0$ might also only have one answer (like just $\sqrt{-5}$), forgetting the $\pm$ requirement.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at graphs of equations like $y = x^2 - 4x + 5$ and $y = x^2 - 4x + 4$, and it’s weird because the first one never touches the x-axis at all, while the second one just barely taps it in one spot. My teacher says there’s this 'Fundamental Theorem' that claims every $x^2$ equation has to have exactly two solutions. If we can only see one 'tap' or no 'hits' at all on the graph, where are those two promised answers hiding?

misconceptions.title: Visual-Only Validation
misconceptions.description: Students believe that if a graph does not cross the x-axis, the equation has zero solutions, failing to account for the complex number system.

misconceptions.title: Single-Root Assumption
misconceptions.description: When a parabola's vertex sits exactly on the x-axis, students identify only one solution, missing the concept of a "double root" or multiplicity.

misconceptions.title: Linear Logic Overgeneralization
misconceptions.description: Students assume that because $x + 5 = 0$ has one answer, $x^2 + 5 = 0$ might also only have one answer (like just $\sqrt{-5}$), forgetting the $\pm$ requirement.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you use the quadratic formula on a 'floating' parabola, does the $\pm$ part of the formula ever actually go away?"
"Wait—if a graph just barely touches the axis at $x = 2$, is there a reason we write the factored version as $(x-2)(x-2)$ instead of just $(x-2)$?"
Does the highest power of $x$ in the equation always seem to match the total number of 'hidden' and 'visible' answers combined?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you have an $x^2$ equation, you're ALWAYS going to find exactly two solutions if you're willing to count both the 'imaginary' ones and the 'double' ones... or is there a special case where you could end up with three or maybe just one?
sections.inference: I found this other equation $y = x^3 - 1$. When I graph it, it only crosses the x-axis once at $x = 1$, but if the 'power of 2' equations always have two answers, do you think this 'power of 3' equation is secretly hiding two more somewhere else? And if we found a 'power of 10' equation, is there a way to know how many total answers to look for without even solving it?

hsn-q.a.1_commuters-calculation.title: The Commuter's Calculation
hsn-q.a.1_commuters-calculation.standardDescription: Use units as a way to understand problems and to guide the solution of multi-step problems; choose and interpret units consistently in formulas; choose and interpret the scale and the origin in graphs and data displays.
hsn-q.a.1_commuters-calculation.description: Teach Zippy about The Commuter's Calculation
hsn-q.a.1_commuters-calculation.problemStatement: My older brother is trying to figure out if it's cheaper to take the city bus or drive his old truck to his new job 12 miles away. The bus is $2.25 per ride, but his truck gets 18 miles per gallon and gas costs $3.60 per gallon right now. If he has all these different numbers like 'miles,' 'dollars per gallon,' and 'miles per gallon,' how can he mash them together to find out the actual cost for just one trip?
hsn-q.a.1_commuters-calculation.teachingPrompt: Use units as a way to understand problems and to guide the solution of multi-step problems; choose and interpret units consistently in formulas; choose and interpret the scale and the origin in graphs and data displays.
misconceptions: Operation Confusion (procedural_overgeneralization): Students might multiply the distance by the fuel efficiency (12 x 18) instead of dividing, resulting in "square miles per gallon" rather than gallons.
Unit Neglect (surface_feature_matching): Students may simply add all the numbers provided (2.25 + 12 + 18 + 3.60) because they see them all as part of the same "cost" problem.
Incomplete Scaling (partial_concept_model): Students might calculate the cost of one gallon of gas but forget to factor in that the trip only uses a fraction of a gallon.

sections.studentPrompt: My older brother is trying to figure out if it's cheaper to take the city bus or drive his old truck to his new job 12 miles away. The bus is $2.25 per ride, but his truck gets 18 miles per gallon and gas costs $3.60 per gallon right now. If he has all these different numbers like 'miles,' 'dollars per gallon,' and 'miles per gallon,' how can he mash them together to find out the actual cost for just one trip?

misconceptions.title: Operation Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students might multiply the distance by the fuel efficiency (12 x 18) instead of dividing, resulting in "square miles per gallon" rather than gallons.

misconceptions.title: Unit Neglect
misconceptions.description: Students may simply add all the numbers provided (2.25 + 12 + 18 + 3.60) because they see them all as part of the same "cost" problem.

misconceptions.title: Incomplete Scaling
misconceptions.description: Students might calculate the cost of one gallon of gas but forget to factor in that the trip only uses a fraction of a gallon.

hsn-q.a.1_marathon-pace-mystery.title: The Marathon Pace Mystery
hsn-q.a.1_marathon-pace-mystery.standardDescription: Use units as a way to understand problems and to guide the solution of multi-step problems; choose and interpret units consistently in formulas; choose and interpret the scale and the origin in graphs and data displays.
hsn-q.a.1_marathon-pace-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Marathon Pace Mystery
hsn-q.a.1_marathon-pace-mystery.teachingPrompt: Use units as a way to understand problems and to guide the solution of multi-step problems; choose and interpret units consistently in formulas; choose and interpret the scale and the origin in graphs and data displays.
targetConcepts: The student can use dimensional analysis to convert between different units of measure (e.g., metric to imperial, speed to pace).
The student can interpret the meaning of units in a multi-step problem to determine if a specific target or threshold has been met.
misconceptions: Reciprocal Confusion (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student thinks that because 10 is a bigger number than 6, the runner is already going faster than the goal pace, ignoring that "minutes per mile" is an inverse unit (time/distance).

System Mismatch (structural_misunderstanding): The student tries to compare kilometers and miles directly without a conversion factor, assuming the numerical values are equivalent because they both measure "distance."

Unit Type Blindness (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student treats the rates as simple whole numbers and fails to recognize that "speed" (dist/time) and "pace" (time/dist) require different algebraic treatments to compare.

sections.studentPrompt: 10 kilometers per hour,

misconceptions.title: Reciprocal Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student thinks that because 10 is a bigger number than 6, the runner is already going faster than the goal pace, ignoring that "minutes per mile" is an inverse unit (time/distance).

misconceptions.title: System Mismatch
misconceptions.description: The student tries to compare kilometers and miles directly without a conversion factor, assuming the numerical values are equivalent because they both measure "distance."

misconceptions.title: Unit Type Blindness
misconceptions.description: The student treats the rates as simple whole numbers and fails to recognize that "speed" (dist/time) and "pace" (time/dist) require different algebraic treatments to compare.

sections.patternRecognition:
If the runner spends more minutes on every mile, does that "10 kilometers per hour" number on the watch go up or down?
When we talk about "per hour" versus "per mile," which part of the measurement is actually the thing that stays the same while the runner is moving?
How does the "10" on the watch and the "6" from the coach connect when we try to figure out how far the runner goes in just one single minute?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I have a rate like 'miles per hour,' can I ALWAYS just flip the fraction upside down to get 'hours per mile' and have it still describe the exact same speed?
sections.inference: 10 kilometers per hour

hsn-q.a.2_social-media-surge.title: The Social Media Surge
hsn-q.a.2_social-media-surge.standardDescription: Define appropriate quantities for the purpose of descriptive modeling.
hsn-q.a.2_social-media-surge.description: Teach Zippy about The Social Media Surge
hsn-q.a.2_social-media-surge.problemStatement: My friend started a TikTok account and is obsessing over her 'growth,' but it’s getting confusing because one video got 5,000 views in an hour while another got 10,000 views over a whole week. She says the second one is better because the number is bigger, but I feel like we’re missing a piece of the story if we want to predict which one is actually 'trending' faster. If we wanted to compare these fairly to see which account is actually blowing up, what new kind of number should we even be tracking?
hsn-q.a.2_social-media-surge.teachingPrompt: Define appropriate quantities for the purpose of descriptive modeling.
targetConcepts: Students can identify and define the necessary variables (units and quantities) needed to accurately model a real-world relationship.
Students can move beyond raw data points to create derived quantities (like rates) that allow for consistent comparison across different scales.
misconceptions: Magnitude Bias (surface_feature_matching): Students focus only on the largest absolute value (10,000 views) without considering the interval over which the quantity was measured.

Additive Growth Assumption (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may think that adding a fixed number of views per day is the only way to measure success, missing the concept of a rate or ratio.
Variable Mismatch (partial_concept_model): Students might try to compare views to likes or followers instead of finding a common denominator of time to define "growth."

sections.studentPrompt: My friend started a TikTok account and is obsessing over her 'growth,' but it’s getting confusing because one video got 5,000 views in an hour while another got 10,000 views over a whole week. She says the second one is better because the number is bigger, but I feel like we’re missing a piece of the story if we want to predict which one is actually 'trending' faster. If we wanted to compare these fairly to see which account is actually blowing up, what new kind of number should we even be tracking?

misconceptions.title: Magnitude Bias
misconceptions.description: Students focus only on the largest absolute value (10,000 views) without considering the interval over which the quantity was measured.

misconceptions.title: Additive Growth Assumption
misconceptions.description: Students may think that adding a fixed number of views per day is the only way to measure success, missing the concept of a rate or ratio.

misconceptions.title: Variable Mismatch
misconceptions.description: Students might try to compare views to likes or followers instead of finding a common denominator of time to define "growth."

sections.patternRecognition:
"If both videos kept going exactly like they started, which one would have more views by tomorrow morning?"
"Wait—is there a specific 'chunk of time' we could use for both of them so the comparison actually makes sense?"
Okay, so if 'total views' isn't telling the whole story, what is the relationship between the views and the clock?
sections.generalization: I have this hunch that if you want to model how fast something is changing, you ALWAYS have to invent a 'combined' unit like views-per-hour or miles-per-gallon... or is there ever a time where just a single, plain number tells you everything you need to know?
sections.inference: I found this other creator who gained 200 followers in 2 days, and another who gained 500 followers in 5 days. At first, I thought the second person was doing better, but then I realized something weird about the math. If they both keep growing at these exact same rates, is there a way to figure out how many followers they'd each have in a month without just counting day by day?

hsn-q.a.3_golden-gate-paint.title: The Golden Gate Paint
hsn-q.a.3_golden-gate-paint.standardDescription: Choose a level of accuracy appropriate to limitations on measurement when reporting quantities.
hsn-q.a.3_golden-gate-paint.description: Teach Zippy about The Golden Gate Paint
hsn-q.a.3_golden-gate-paint.problemStatement: I’m helping my uncle calculate how much 'International Orange' paint he needs for a huge bridge project, but the numbers are driving me crazy. The surface area is roughly 253,482 square feet, but the paint cans say they cover 'about 350-400 square feet' depending on the wind. If I tell him he needs exactly 675.952 cans of paint, is he going to think I'm a genius or is he just going to laugh at me for being way too specific?
hsn-q.a.3_golden-gate-paint.teachingPrompt: Choose a level of accuracy appropriate to limitations on measurement when reporting quantities.
targetConcepts: Students can determine the appropriate number of significant digits or level of rounding based on the precision of the initial measurements.
Students can justify why a "mathematically precise" answer may be "contextually incorrect" due to physical limitations or safety margins.
misconceptions: Calculator Literalism (procedural_overgeneralization): Students often provide every decimal digit shown on a calculator screen ($675.952$), failing to realize that those digits imply a level of precision that the input data (the "350-400" range) doesn't actually support.

Under-Estimation Risk (partial_concept_model): In a real-world context like painting, a student might round down to 675 cans because it is mathematically closer, ignoring the practical constraint that running out of paint mid-job is a failure.

Range Neglect (surface_feature_matching): Students might average the coverage range (375 sq ft) and treat it as a perfect, certain number rather than acknowledging the uncertainty inherent in the 350-400 sq ft estimate.

sections.studentPrompt: I’m helping my uncle calculate how much 'International Orange' paint he needs for a huge bridge project, but the numbers are driving me crazy. The surface area is roughly 253,482 square feet, but the paint cans say they cover 'about 350-400 square feet' depending on the wind. If I tell him he needs exactly 675.952 cans of paint, is he going to think I'm a genius or is he just going to laugh at me for being way too specific?

misconceptions.title: Calculator Literalism
misconceptions.description: Students often provide every decimal digit shown on a calculator screen ($675.952$), failing to realize that those digits imply a level of precision that the input data (the "350-400" range) doesn't actually support.

misconceptions.title: Under-Estimation Risk
misconceptions.description: In a real-world context like painting, a student might round down to 675 cans because it is mathematically closer, ignoring the practical constraint that running out of paint mid-job is a failure.

misconceptions.title: Range Neglect
misconceptions.description: Students might average the coverage range (375 sq ft) and treat it as a perfect, certain number rather than acknowledging the uncertainty inherent in the 350-400 sq ft estimate.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If the wind picks up and the paint only covers 350 square feet instead of 400, does that change your 'exact' decimal answer a lot?"
"Wait—can you even walk into a hardware store and buy $0.952$ of a can of paint?"
If the surface area of the bridge was measured with a regular tape measure, is it even possible to know the area down to the last square inch?
sections.generalization: I have this hunch that if your starting measurements are just 'rough estimates,' your final answer should ALWAYS be rounded to a simple, clean number... or is there ever a time where you still need to keep all those tiny decimals?
sections.inference: I found this other project for a tiny birdhouse where the wood is exactly 12.0 inches long and I need to cut it into 7 equal pieces. If I tell my friend to mark the wood at 1.71428 inches for each cut, do you think he can actually see that on his ruler? Is there a better way to tell him where to cut so the pieces actually fit together without him getting frustrated?

hsn-q.a_commute-conundrum.title: The Commute Conundrum
hsn-q.a_commute-conundrum.standardDescription: Reason quantitatively and use units to lead to problem solving.
hsn-q.a_commute-conundrum.description: Teach Zippy about The Commute Conundrum
hsn-q.a_commute-conundrum.problemStatement: I’m trying to figure out if it’s cheaper to take the bus or drive to my summer job, but the numbers are all over the place because the bus is $\$2.50$ per ride while my car gets $25$ miles per gallon and gas is $\$3.50$ per gallon. If I know my job is $10$ miles away, what is the best way to set this up so I don't accidentally compare 'miles' to 'dollars' and end up with an answer that makes no sense?
hsn-q.a_commute-conundrum.teachingPrompt: Reason quantitatively and use units to lead to problem solving.
targetConcepts: Students can use units as a guide to choose and interpret the correct formulas for multi-step problems. [cite: 89, 285]
Students can interpret the meaning of units within a contextual situation to evaluate if a solution is appropriate. [cite: 223, 339]
misconceptions: Incomplete Dimensional Analysis (partial_concept_model): Students might calculate the cost of gas for one mile but forget to scale it by the total distance of the trip to compare it to the flat bus fare. [cite: 89, 223]

sections.studentPrompt: I’m trying to figure out if it’s cheaper to take the bus or drive to my summer job, but the numbers are all over the place because the bus is $\$2.50$ per ride while my car gets $25$ miles per gallon and gas is $\$3.50$ per gallon. If I know my job is $10$ miles away, what is the best way to set this up so I don't accidentally compare 'miles' to 'dollars' and end up with an answer that makes no sense?

misconceptions.title: Incomplete Dimensional Analysis
misconceptions.description: Students might calculate the cost of gas for one mile but forget to scale it by the total distance of the trip to compare it to the flat bus fare. [cite: 89, 223]

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you're looking at 'miles per gallon' and 'miles,' what happens to the 'miles' if you divide one by the other?" [cite: 89, 339]
"If gas was $\$1.00$ per gallon instead of $\$3.50$, would the 'per gallon' part of the unit change at all?" [cite: 285]
"How do the 'dollars' end up being the only unit left at the end of the calculation for both the bus and the car?" [cite: 223, 339]
sections.generalization: I’m thinking that if you always line up your units so they cancel out like fractions, you'll ALWAYS end up with the right type of answer... or is there a way that 'correct units' could still give you a totally wrong number?
sections.inference: I found this other job that's much further away—$50$ miles—but they offer a carpool that costs $\$0.15$ per mile. My car still gets $25$ miles per gallon with gas at $\$3.50$. Do you think the 'per mile' carpool is going to stay cheaper than the gas cost as the trip gets longer, or is there a point where the car starts winning?

hsn-rn.a.1_triple-product-mystery.title: The Triple-Product Mystery
hsn-rn.a.1_triple-product-mystery.domain: Number and Quantity: The Real Number System
hsn-rn.a.1_triple-product-mystery.standardDescription: Explain how the definition of the meaning of rational exponents follows from extending the properties of integer exponents to those values, allowing for a notation for radicals in terms of rational exponents.
