For example, we define 5^(1/3) to be the cube root of 5 because we want (5^(1/3))^3 = 5^((1/3)3) to hold, so (5^(1/3))^3 must equal 5.
hsn-rn.a.1_triple-product-mystery.description: Teach Zippy about The Triple-Product Mystery
hsn-rn.a.1_triple-product-mystery.problemStatement: I was messing around with my calculator and found this weird setting where you can raise a number to a fraction, like $8^{1/3}$ or $9^{1/2}$. I noticed that if I take $8^{1/3}$ and multiply it by itself three times, I get exactly 8 back, but I can’t figure out why it doesn't just divide the number like a normal fraction. If we want the rules for regular exponents to still work for these fraction ones, what would $5^{1/2}$ actually have to be?
hsn-rn.a.1_triple-product-mystery.teachingPrompt: Explain how the definition of the meaning of rational exponents follows from extending the properties of integer exponents to those values, allowing for a notation for radicals in terms of rational exponents. For example, we define 5^(1/3) to be the cube root of 5 because we want (5^(1/3))^3 = 5^((1/3)3) to hold, so (5^(1/3))^3 must equal 5.
targetConcepts: Students can justify the definition of $a^{1/n}$ by applying the product and power properties of exponents to show it results in $a^1$
misconceptions: Exponent as Multiplier (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may calculate $9^{1/2}$ as $9 \times 0.5 = 4.5$ because they are treating the exponent as a coefficient.
Division Bias (whole_number_bias): Students might assume $8^{1/3}$ is the same as $8 \div 3$ because they associate the denominator of the exponent with the operation of division.
Radical Misplacement (misinterpretation_of_representation): Students may recognize a root is involved but flip the roles, thinking the numerator is the root and the denominator is the power.

sections.studentPrompt: I was messing around with my calculator and found this weird setting where you can raise a number to a fraction, like $8^{1/3}$ or $9^{1/2}$. I noticed that if I take $8^{1/3}$ and multiply it by itself three times, I get exactly 8 back, but I can’t figure out why it doesn't just divide the number like a normal fraction. If we want the rules for regular exponents to still work for these fraction ones, what would $5^{1/2}$ actually have to be?

misconceptions.title: Exponent as Multiplier
misconceptions.description: Students may calculate $9^{1/2}$ as $9 \times 0.5 = 4.5$ because they are treating the exponent as a coefficient.

misconceptions.title: Division Bias
misconceptions.description: Students might assume $8^{1/3}$ is the same as $8 \div 3$ because they associate the denominator of the exponent with the operation of division.

misconceptions.title: Radical Misplacement
misconceptions.description: Students may recognize a root is involved but flip the roles, thinking the numerator is the root and the denominator is the power.

sections.patternRecognition:
"If you have $5^{1/3} \cdot 5^{1/3} \cdot 5^{1/3}$, what does the addition rule for exponents say the new power should be?"
"When you multiply those three mystery numbers together, why does the base number 5 stay exactly the same?"
Wait, if three of the same number multiply to get 5, is that different than just dividing 5 into three pieces?
sections.generalization: So, I have this hunch that if you have a power like $1/n$, it's ALWAYS just going to be the same thing as the $n$-th root of that number... or is there some weird case where that rule totally breaks?
sections.inference: Okay, so I found this other one where $16^{1/4}$ equals 2, and if you multiply 2 by itself four times, you get 16. If we keep using this 'denominator is the root' trick, do you think there's a way to find $81^{1/4}$ without just guessing random numbers?

hsn-rn.a.2_fractional-power-swap.title: The Fractional Power Swap
hsn-rn.a.2_fractional-power-swap.domain: Number and Quantity: The Real Number System
hsn-rn.a.2_fractional-power-swap.standardDescription: Rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents.
hsn-rn.a.2_fractional-power-swap.description: Teach Zippy about The Fractional Power Swap
hsn-rn.a.2_fractional-power-swap.problemStatement: I was looking at two different math problems, and one had $\sqrt[3]{x^5}$ while the other had $(x^5)^{1/3}$, but my older brother said they are actually the exact same thing written in different 'languages.' I get how normal exponents work, but if we treat that fraction like a regular exponent, is there a way to write the whole thing as just $x$ to a single power without using the radical symbol at all?
hsn-rn.a.2_fractional-power-swap.teachingPrompt: Rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents.
targetConcepts: Students can convert expressions between radical form and rational exponent form using the properties of exponents[cite: 278, 308, 422].
Students can simplify complex radical expressions by rewriting them as rational exponents and applying integer exponent rules[cite: 189, 204].

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at two different math problems, and one had $\sqrt[3]{x^5}$ while the other had $(x^5)^{1/3}$, but my older brother said they are actually the exact same thing written in different 'languages.' I get how normal exponents work, but if we treat that fraction like a regular exponent, is there a way to write the whole thing as just $x$ to a single power without using the radical symbol at all?
sections.patternRecognition:
"When you see $(x^5)^{1/3}$, does the 'power of a power' rule tell you to add those numbers or multiply them?"
"If the $x^5$ part stays the same, what happens to the 3 from the radical when it moves into the exponent?"
Wait—if $\sqrt{x}$ is $x^{1/2}$ and $\sqrt[3]{x}$ is $x^{1/3}$, what is actually changing in the fraction as the root gets bigger?
sections.generalization: My friend thinks that if you have any radical like $\sqrt[n]{x^m}$, you can ALWAYS just rewrite it as $x^{m/n}$ and all the old exponent rules will still work perfectly... is that a safe bet or does that only work sometimes?
sections.inference: I found this really complicated-looking expression $\sqrt[4]{x^{12}}$ and I'm trying to figure out if I can simplify it using that same fraction trick. If $\sqrt[3]{x^6}$ can be written as $x^{6/3}$—which is just $x^2$—do you think the same logic helps us figure out what the 'normal' version of $\sqrt[4]{x^{12}}$ would be?

hsn-rn.a_rapidly-shrinking-block.title: The Rapidly Shrinking Block
hsn-rn.a_rapidly-shrinking-block.standardDescription: Extend the properties of exponents to rational exponents.
hsn-rn.a_rapidly-shrinking-block.description: Teach Zippy about The Rapidly Shrinking Block
hsn-rn.a_rapidly-shrinking-block.problemStatement: So I've been watching this simulation of a block that shrinks by half every minute, but then I started wondering about what happens right in the middle of a minute. If it starts at 64 cubic inches and it's 32 after one minute and 16 after two minutes, is there a way to know exactly how big it is at exactly one minute and thirty seconds without just guessing it's halfway between 32 and 16?
hsn-rn.a_rapidly-shrinking-block.teachingPrompt: Extend the properties of exponents to rational exponents.
targetConcepts: Students can extend properties of integer exponents to justify the interpretation of fractional exponents.
Students can rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents. [cite: 189, 204]
misconceptions:
Root Index Confusion (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student recognizes a root is involved but struggles to identify that the denominator of the rational exponent is the root index.
3. [cite_start]**Procedural Overgeneralization** *(Type: procedural_overgeneralization)* The student attempts to divide the base by the exponent or multiply the base by the fraction instead of using properties of exponents. [cite: 189]

sections.studentPrompt: So I've been watching this simulation of a block that shrinks by half every minute, but then I started wondering about what happens right in the middle of a minute. If it starts at 64 cubic inches and it's 32 after one minute and 16 after two minutes, is there a way to know exactly how big it is at exactly one minute and thirty seconds without just guessing it's halfway between 32 and 16?

misconceptions.title: Root Index Confusion
misconceptions.description:
The student recognizes a root is involved but struggles to identify that the denominator of the rational exponent is the root index.
3. [cite_start]**Procedural Overgeneralization** *(Type: procedural_overgeneralization)* The student attempts to divide the base by the exponent or multiply the base by the fraction instead of using properties of exponents. [cite: 189]

sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait—if we look at the size every full minute, does the amount it loses stay the same or is it changing?"
"Okay, but is there a specific multiplier that carries us from one minute to the next every single time?"
If we had to take two 'mini-steps' to get from 32 to 16 using the exact same multiplier twice, what would that number even have to be?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if a block is shrinking by half every minute, does that mean the multiplier for a 'half-minute' step is ALWAYS going to be the square root of 0.5?
sections.inference: I just found this other simulation where a bacteria colony triples every hour! It starts with 10 bacteria, then 30 after one hour, then 90 after two. I'm curious if we can use that same 'split-step' logic to figure out how many there are at 20 minutes, since that's exactly one-third of an hour.

hsn-rn.b.3_decimal-disruptor.title: The Decimal Disruptor
hsn-rn.b.3_decimal-disruptor.domain: Number and Quantity: The Real Number System
hsn-rn.b.3_decimal-disruptor.standardDescription: Explain why the sum or product of two rational numbers is rational; that the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational; and that the product of a nonzero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.
hsn-rn.b.3_decimal-disruptor.description: Teach Zippy about The Decimal Disruptor
hsn-rn.b.3_decimal-disruptor.problemStatement: I was adding some numbers on my calculator and noticed that when I add two fractions like $1/2$ and $1/4$, I always get a nice, clean decimal that stops or repeats. But when I added a simple $0.5$ to a messy number like $\sqrt{2}$ that goes on forever with no pattern, the whole result stayed messy and never ended. If I take a 'clean' rational number and multiply it by one of those 'infinite-messy' irrational numbers, is there any way the answer could actually turn out clean?
hsn-rn.b.3_decimal-disruptor.teachingPrompt: Explain why the sum or product of two rational numbers is rational; that the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational; and that the product of a nonzero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.
targetConcepts: [cite_start]Students can justify why the sum of a rational and irrational number must be irrational[cite: 164, 203].
[cite_start]Students can identify that the product of a non-zero rational and an irrational is always irrational[cite: 348], while acknowledging the unique role of zero.
misconceptions:
The Rounding Myth (whole_number_bias): Students might believe that an irrational number's infinite decimal eventually "runs out" or gets absorbed if added to a much larger rational number.

2. [cite_start]**Zero Exception Overgeneralization** *(Type: partial_concept_model)* Students may forget that while a rational times an irrational is usually irrational[cite: 165], multiplying by the rational number zero results in a rational zero.

Pattern Emergence (misinterpretation_of_representation): Students might assume that multiplying an irrational number by a large enough rational integer will eventually force a repeating pattern to appear in the decimals.

sections.studentPrompt: I was adding some numbers on my calculator and noticed that when I add two fractions like $1/2$ and $1/4$, I always get a nice, clean decimal that stops or repeats. But when I added a simple $0.5$ to a messy number like $\sqrt{2}$ that goes on forever with no pattern, the whole result stayed messy and never ended. If I take a 'clean' rational number and multiply it by one of those 'infinite-messy' irrational numbers, is there any way the answer could actually turn out clean?

misconceptions.title: The Rounding Myth
misconceptions.description:
Students might believe that an irrational number's infinite decimal eventually "runs out" or gets absorbed if added to a much larger rational number.

2. [cite_start]**Zero Exception Overgeneralization** *(Type: partial_concept_model)* Students may forget that while a rational times an irrational is usually irrational[cite: 165], multiplying by the rational number zero results in a rational zero.

misconceptions.title: Pattern Emergence
misconceptions.description: Students might assume that multiplying an irrational number by a large enough rational integer will eventually force a repeating pattern to appear in the decimals.

sections.patternRecognition:
"When you add two 'ending' decimals together, is there any way the digits at the very end could suddenly start going on forever?"
"If you have a number that never repeats and you multiply it by 10, does that change the fact that the digits are still unpredictable?"
Why does adding a 'clean' fraction to a 'messy' decimal never seem to fix the messiness?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you multiply a non-zero rational number by an irrational one, the answer is ALWAYS going to be irrational... but could there be one specific 'magic' rational number that actually makes the pattern start repeating?
sections.inference: I found this number $\sqrt{5}$ and it's a total mess—the decimals just won't stop. If I multiply it by 0, the answer is just a clean 0, but if I multiply it by any other fraction like $2/3$, it stays totally irrational. Do you think there's any way to predict exactly which rational numbers will 'break' the irrationality and which ones will just keep the mess going?

hsn-rn.b_mystery-number-mix.title: The Mystery Number Mix
hsn-rn.b_mystery-number-mix.domain: Number and Quantity: The Real Number System
hsn-rn.b_mystery-number-mix.standardDescription: Explain why the sum or product of two rational numbers is rational; that the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational; and that the product of a nonzero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.
hsn-rn.b_mystery-number-mix.description: Teach Zippy about The Mystery Number Mix
hsn-rn.b_mystery-number-mix.problemStatement: I was looking at a list of numbers like $1/2$ and $\sqrt{2}$, and I noticed that if I add two fractions together, I always get another fraction back. But when I add a normal fraction to something like $\pi$ or $\sqrt{2}$, the decimal just goes on forever without a pattern and I can't turn it back into a fraction. If I take a regular number like 5 and multiply it by a 'messy' decimal that never repeats, will the answer stay messy forever?
hsn-rn.b_mystery-number-mix.teachingPrompt: Explain why the sum or product of two rational numbers is rational; that the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational; and that the product of a nonzero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.
targetConcepts: [cite_start]Students can justify why the sum or product of two rational numbers must remain rational using the definition of a fraction. [cite: 375]
[cite_start]Students can explain why adding or multiplying a nonzero rational number and an irrational number results in an irrational value. [cite: 203]
misconceptions: Finite Decimal Bias (whole_number_bias): Students might believe that an irrational number multiplied by a rational number will eventually "round off" or terminate if the rational number is small enough.

Closure Confusion (overgeneralization_of_prior_knowledge): Students often assume that because the sum of two integers is always an integer, the sum of any two "types" of numbers (like two irrationals) must always stay that same type.

Product to Zero (partial_concept_model): Students may fail to realize that multiplying an irrational number by the rational number zero results in a rational number, leading to an overgeneralization that the product is *always* irrational.

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at a list of numbers like $1/2$ and $\sqrt{2}$, and I noticed that if I add two fractions together, I always get another fraction back. But when I add a normal fraction to something like $\pi$ or $\sqrt{2}$, the decimal just goes on forever without a pattern and I can't turn it back into a fraction. If I take a regular number like 5 and multiply it by a 'messy' decimal that never repeats, will the answer stay messy forever?

misconceptions.title: Finite Decimal Bias
misconceptions.description: Students might believe that an irrational number multiplied by a rational number will eventually "round off" or terminate if the rational number is small enough.

misconceptions.title: Closure Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students often assume that because the sum of two integers is always an integer, the sum of any two "types" of numbers (like two irrationals) must always stay that same type.

misconceptions.title: Product to Zero
misconceptions.description: Students may fail to realize that multiplying an irrational number by the rational number zero results in a rational number, leading to an overgeneralization that the product is *always* irrational.

sections.patternRecognition:
[cite_start]"When you add two fractions like $1/2$ and $1/3$, is there any way the result could ever NOT be a fraction?" [cite: 375]
[cite_start]"If you have a perfectly repeating pattern in a decimal and you add a 'messy' one that never repeats, what happens to that perfect pattern?" [cite: 203]
Does multiplying a number by 5 ever change whether the decimal part goes on forever or not?
sections.generalization: I have a hunch that if you multiply a 'fraction-style' rational number by a 'messy' irrational one, the answer is ALWAYS going to be irrational... unless maybe the rational number is zero? Can zero actually 'fix' an irrational number and make it rational again?
sections.inference: I found this other number $\sqrt{3}$, which is definitely one of those irrational ones that never ends. If I multiply it by 10, the calculator shows $17.320508...$ and it still looks totally messy. Do you think there is ANY number we could multiply $\sqrt{3}$ by to turn it into a clean, simple integer like 3, or is it stuck being irrational no matter what we do?

hsn-vm.a.1_the-drifting-drone.title: The Drifting Drone
hsn-vm.a.1_the-drifting-drone.standardDescription: Recognize vector quantities as having both magnitude and direction. Represent vector quantities by directed line segments, and use appropriate symbols for vectors (e.g., v, |v|, ||v||, v).
hsn-vm.a.1_the-drifting-drone.description: Teach Zippy about The Drifting Drone
hsn-vm.a.1_the-drifting-drone.problemStatement: I set my drone to fly at 15 miles per hour heading exactly North, but a strong wind started blowing it toward the East at 10 miles per hour at the same time. If the drone is technically moving at two different speeds in two different directions, is there a way to draw a single arrow that shows where it's actually going to end up?
hsn-vm.a.1_the-drifting-drone.teachingPrompt: Recognize vector quantities as having both magnitude and direction. Represent vector quantities by directed line segments, and use appropriate symbols for vectors (e.g., v, |v|, ||v||, v).
targetConcepts: The student can identify a vector as a quantity possessing both magnitude and direction.
The student can represent the combination of two vectors using a directed line segment (resultant vector) and appropriate notation.
misconceptions: Scalar Addition Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): The student adds the two speeds together ($15 + 10 = 25$) and assumes the drone is simply traveling at 25 mph without accounting for the angular change.
Directional Neglect (partial_concept_model): The student focuses only on the "North" instruction and assumes the wind is a separate error to be ignored rather than a component of the final velocity.

Symbolic Confusion (misinterpretation_of_representation): The student treats the vector $v$ as a simple coordinate $(x, y)$ but fails to recognize that the length of the arrow represents the physical speed (magnitude).

sections.studentPrompt: I set my drone to fly at 15 miles per hour heading exactly North, but a strong wind started blowing it toward the East at 10 miles per hour at the same time. If the drone is technically moving at two different speeds in two different directions, is there a way to draw a single arrow that shows where it's actually going to end up?

misconceptions.title: Scalar Addition Error
misconceptions.description: The student adds the two speeds together ($15 + 10 = 25$) and assumes the drone is simply traveling at 25 mph without accounting for the angular change.

misconceptions.title: Directional Neglect
misconceptions.description: The student focuses only on the "North" instruction and assumes the wind is a separate error to be ignored rather than a component of the final velocity.

misconceptions.title: Symbolic Confusion
misconceptions.description: The student treats the vector $v$ as a simple coordinate $(x, y)$ but fails to recognize that the length of the arrow represents the physical speed (magnitude).

sections.patternRecognition:
If the wind suddenly got twice as strong but the drone kept its same Northward power, how would the "tilt" of that path change on your paper?
Does the length of the arrow you drew stay the same if the drone slows down but stays on the exact same North-East line?
How does the "start" and "end" of your arrow connect to the two different forces pushing on the drone at the same time?
sections.generalization: Wait, so if I have two different arrows pushing on an object, does that mean the final path is ALWAYS going to be the diagonal of the box made by those two arrows, even if they aren't pointing at a right angle?
sections.inference: arrow trick

hsn-vm.c_inventory-grid.title: The Inventory Grid
hsn-vm.c_inventory-grid.standardDescription: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
hsn-vm.c_inventory-grid.description: Teach Zippy about The Inventory Grid
hsn-vm.c_inventory-grid.problemStatement: My family owns two smoothie shops, and we keep track of how many strawberry, banana, and kale boosts we use every day in these grids. The North shop used 20, 15, and 10 yesterday, and the South shop used 25, 30, and 5, but today we’re running a 'Double Boost' promotion at both locations. If I just take the grids from yesterday and double every single number in them, will that actually tell me how many total boosts we need to order for both shops today?
hsn-vm.c_inventory-grid.teachingPrompt: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
targetConcepts: Students can perform scalar multiplication and matrix addition to represent changes in real-world data sets.
Students can interpret the resulting matrix elements in the context of the combined inventory needs of multiple locations.
misconceptions: Scalar-Dimension Confusion (structural_misunderstanding): Students may attempt to add the scalar '2' to every element in the matrix rather than multiplying each element by the scalar.

Non-Corresponding Addition (procedural_overgeneralization): Students might add numbers that do not share the same position in the grid, such as adding strawberry usage from North to kale usage from South.
Multiplicative Scale Error (additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion): Students may add 2 to each value to represent 'doubling' instead of multiplying by 2.

sections.studentPrompt: My family owns two smoothie shops, and we keep track of how many strawberry, banana, and kale boosts we use every day in these grids. The North shop used 20, 15, and 10 yesterday, and the South shop used 25, 30, and 5, but today we’re running a 'Double Boost' promotion at both locations. If I just take the grids from yesterday and double every single number in them, will that actually tell me how many total boosts we need to order for both shops today?

misconceptions.title: Scalar-Dimension Confusion
misconceptions.description: Students may attempt to add the scalar '2' to every element in the matrix rather than multiplying each element by the scalar.

misconceptions.title: Non-Corresponding Addition
misconceptions.description: Students might add numbers that do not share the same position in the grid, such as adding strawberry usage from North to kale usage from South.

misconceptions.title: Multiplicative Scale Error
misconceptions.description: Students may add 2 to each value to represent 'doubling' instead of multiplying by 2.

sections.patternRecognition:
"Wait—if the North shop uses more strawberries than the South shop, does that difference get bigger or stay the same after the 'Double Boost'?"
"Okay, but is there a number in these grids that stays exactly where it is no matter which shop we're looking at?"
Does doubling each shop first and then adding them together give the same result as adding the shops first and then doubling the total?
sections.generalization: If we have a grid of our boost usage and we decide to triple everything, will the new total for the whole week ALWAYS be three times the old total?
sections.inference: I found a different grid for our 'Mega-Green' smoothies where we use 2 cups of spinach and 1 cup of protein per drink. If we sell 50 of these at the North shop and 80 at the South shop, is there a way to calculate the total spinach and protein needed for both shops without calculating each location separately? What if we added a third shop next month—would the 'grid way' of adding them up still work to find the total?
